topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Elements of Discrete Data Control Systems, Advantages of Discrete Data Control Systems, Examples. Signal Conversion & Processing: Digital Signals & Coding, Data Conversion & Quantization, Sample and Hold Devices, Mathematical Modelling of the Sampling Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Basic Elements of a Discrete Data Control System

### Formal KTU Definition

A **Discrete Data Control System (DDC System)** ‚Äî also termed a **Sampled-Data Control System** or **Digital Control System** ‚Äî is a control system in which the reference input, feedback signal, error signal, and/or the control action exist as **discrete-time signals** (quantized or sampled) at one or more points in the feedback loop, and a **digital computer or microprocessor** acts as the central compensating/controller element.

The fundamental building blocks of a canonical discrete data control loop are:

1. **Reference Input Generator** ‚Äî produces the desired set-point signal, often a digital word or sampled analog waveform.
2. **Error Detector / Comparator** ‚Äî performs the subtraction between the sampled reference and sampled feedback.
3. **Digital Controller (Digital Computer / Microprocessor / DSP)** ‚Äî executes the control algorithm (e.g., PID, state feedback) in software.
4. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** ‚Äî converts the digital control output back to an analog signal.
5. **Output Transducer / Actuator** ‚Äî applies the analog control signal to the physical plant.
6. **Plant / Process (G(s))** ‚Äî the physical system being controlled (motor, furnace, robotic arm, etc.).
7. **Feedback Transducer (Sensor + Signal Conditioner)** ‚Äî measures the output variable and produces a measurable analog signal.
8. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) with Quantizer & Encoder** ‚Äî converts the analog feedback into a binary-coded digital word.
9. **Sampler (Ideal Impulse Sampler)** ‚Äî converts the continuous-time error signal into a discrete sequence of impulses.
10. **Hold Device (Zero-Order Hold / First-Order Hold)** ‚Äî reconstructs a piecewise-constant (or piecewise-linear) analog signal from the discrete control samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The interplay of *Sampler ‚Üí Digital Computer ‚Üí DAC ‚Üí Hold Device* is the **defining signal path** of any digital control loop. You must be able to identify the **sampling switch** and the **hold device** in any block diagram ‚Äî these are the two elements that introduce the discrete-time nature of the system.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Smart Thermostat" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to keep your room at exactly 25 ¬∞C. Instead of a continuously variable valve, suppose a **smart digital thermostat** does the following:

- It **checks (samples)** the room temperature **once every 60 seconds** using a digital sensor (ADC).
- A **tiny microcomputer** inside the thermostat compares it to your set-point of 25 ¬∞C, computes the error, and runs a control algorithm.
- The result is a digital number, which it **converts (DAC)** to a voltage that opens/closes the cooling valve.
- The valve remains in the **last commanded position (Hold)** until the next sample arrives.

This **"check ‚Üí compute ‚Üí command ‚Üí hold"** cycle, repeated periodically, is exactly how a discrete data control system works. The room temperature and the valve command are not continuous ‚Äî they are **snapshots** taken at regular intervals.

---

## 1.2 Advantages of Discrete Data Control Systems

A KTU examiner's favourite. Memorize the 8 standard advantages with crisp one-line examples:

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** "Advantages of DDC systems" is a **recurrent 7-mark Part-B question**. The marks are awarded for **listing + brief explanation**, not just a bullet list.

| # | Advantage | Engineering Justification |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **High Accuracy & Repeatability** | Digital computations are exact; no drift due to component tolerances. |
| 2 | **Flexibility via Software** | Control law changes are made by editing code ‚Äî no hardware rewiring. |
| 3 | **Noise Immunity** | Binary signals (0/1) tolerate channel noise far better than analog levels. |
| 4 | **Reduced Hardware Complexity** | One digital computer replaces dozens of analog op-amp summers/integrators. |
| 5 | **Cost-Effectiveness for Complex Algorithms** | Adaptive, self-tuning, fuzzy, neural controllers are infeasible in analog form. |
| 6 | **Easy Networking & Telemetry** | Digital data can be transmitted over buses (CAN, Modbus, Ethernet) without degradation. |
| 7 | **Reliability & Self-Diagnostics** | Microprocessors can run built-in tests, log faults, and alert operators. |
| 8 | **Easy Implementation of Nonlinear & Time-Varying Control** | Lookup tables, piecewise-linear functions, and gain scheduling are trivial in software. |

---

## 1.3 Practical Examples of Discrete Data Control Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Whenever the question says *"Give examples of DDC systems"*, mention at least **3** from different domains (industrial, aerospace, consumer). This shows the examiner breadth of understanding.

1. **CNC Machine Tool Position Control** ‚Äî A microcontroller samples the spindle position via an optical encoder (ADC) and adjusts the servo motor current.
2. **Aircraft Autopilot (Fly-by-Wire)** ‚Äî Flight control computers sample pilot inputs and sensor data at 50‚Äì1000 Hz.
3. **Robotic Arm Joint Control** ‚Äî Each joint has a digital PID loop running on a DSP.
4. **Digital Temperature Controller in an Industrial Furnace** ‚Äî Thermocouple ‚Üí ADC ‚Üí Microcontroller ‚Üí SCR/thyristor firing.
5. **Disk-Drive Head Positioning System** ‚Äî Voice-coil actuator controlled by a digital servo loop.
6. **Satellite Attitude Control** ‚Äî Star trackers + gyros + reaction wheels all orchestrated by a digital control computer.
7. **Automotive Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** ‚Äî Steering angle, wheel speed, yaw rate sampled at 100 Hz; brake pressure modulated digitally.
8. **Process Control (Refinery, Distillation Column)** ‚Äî DCS (Distributed Control System) loops in petrochemical plants.

---

## 1.4 Signal Conversion & Processing ‚Äî High-Level Overview

The **four pillars** of signal conversion and processing that KTU Module 1 focuses on are:

1. **Digital Signals & Coding** ‚Äî Representation of information as binary words (NRZ, Bipolar, Gray code, BCD, 2's complement).
2. **Data Conversion & Quantization** ‚Äî ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) and the quantization process (resolution = Full-Scale / 2‚Åø).
3. **Sample and Hold (S/H) Devices** ‚Äî Zero-Order Hold (ZOH), First-Order Hold (FOH) ‚Äî reconstruction between samples.
4. **Mathematical Modelling of the Sampling Process** ‚Äî Ideal impulse sampling, impulse train, Laplace-domain R*(s) relation.

Each pillar is treated rigorously in the upcoming sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Discrete Data Control Loop ‚Äî Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The control loop operates in a **fixed periodic sequence** every sampling period **T** seconds:

> **Step 1 ‚Äî Sampling:** The continuous error signal $e(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train $\delta_T(t)$, producing an impulse-amplitude-modulated signal $e^*(t)$ whose strength at instant $kT$ equals the value of $e(t)$ at that instant.
>
> **Step 2 ‚Äî A/D Conversion:** Each impulse amplitude is quantized to the nearest representable binary level. The output is a **binary-coded word** $e(kT)$, finite in precision (e.g., 12-bit ADC ‚Üí 4096 levels).
>
> **Step 3 ‚Äî Digital Computation:** The microprocessor evaluates the control algorithm ‚Äî typically a difference equation such as $u(k) = a_1 u(k-1) + a_2 u(k-2) + b_0 e(k) + b_1 e(k-1) + b_2 e(k-2)$ ‚Äî yielding a new digital control word $u(k)$.
>
> **Step 4 ‚Äî D/A Conversion:** The DAC converts $u(k)$ to an analog voltage/current.
>
> **Step 5 ‚Äî Hold (Reconstruction):** The Hold device (ZOH) maintains the DAC output **constant** over the interval $[kT, (k+1)T)$, providing a piecewise-continuous control input to the plant.
>
> **Step 6 ‚Äî Plant + Feedback:** The actuator drives the plant, the sensor measures the new output, and the loop repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The **Sampler and Hold together** are what create the discrete-to-continuous interface. Skipping the Hold (i.e., using impulses directly on the plant) is physically unrealizable. The ZOH is the **standard, default Hold** assumed in 95% of KTU problems.

---

## 2.2 Digital Signals & Coding

A **digital signal** is a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal whose amplitude is drawn from a finite set of levels. The standard binary levels are **two**: logic 0 and logic 1, encoded using line codes such as:

- **NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level):** `1` = +V, `0` = 0 V.
- **NRZ-M (Mark):** `1` = transition, `0` = no transition.
- **Bipolar / AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion):** Successive `1`s alternate polarity.
- **Manchester / Biphase-L:** A `1` is a low-to-high transition in the bit interval; `0` is high-to-low.
- **Gray Code:** Adjacent codewords differ in exactly one bit ‚Äî used in **optical encoders** for shaft position.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Fact:** Gray-coded optical encoders are the **backbone of digital position feedback** in servo systems because a single-bit error in a Gray code corresponds to a position error of only one LSB ‚Äî a vast improvement over straight binary, where a transition can cause multiple-bit errors (e.g., 0111 ‚Üí 1000).

---

## 2.3 Data Conversion & Quantization

### 2.3.1 The Quantization Process

A continuous-amplitude sample $e(kT)$ is mapped to the nearest discrete level in a set of $2^n$ levels (for an $n$-bit converter). The **quantization step size (resolution)** is:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^{n-1}}$$

where $V_{\text{FS}}$ is the full-scale input range and $n$ is the number of bits.

**Quantization Error** $e_q$ is bounded by:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

The error is conventionally modelled as a **uniformly distributed white noise** with variance $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$.

**Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for a full-scale sinusoid:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}$$

This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule** ‚Äî every additional bit of resolution improves the SQNR by approximately 6 dB.

### 2.3.2 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) Block

A practical ADC consists of **four sub-blocks** in cascade:

1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** ‚Äî a low-pass filter (typically Butterworth) with cutoff $\omega_c < \omega_s/2$ that band-limits the input to prevent aliasing.
2. **Sampler** ‚Äî produces a discrete-time signal.
3. **Quantizer** ‚Äî maps each sample to the nearest of $2^n$ levels.
4. **Encoder** ‚Äî assigns a unique $n$-bit binary codeword to each quantization level.

### 2.3.3 Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) Block

The inverse path. A DAC receives an $n$-bit binary word and produces an analog voltage/current, often via:

- **Weighted-resistor network** (conceptual, used in textbooks).
- **R-2R ladder network** (practical, used in ICs).
- **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) followed by a low-pass filter** (used in motor control).

---

## 2.4 Sample and Hold (S/H) Devices

### 2.4.1 Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) ‚Äî The Default Hold

A ZOH holds the sampled value **constant** for the entire sampling period. Its impulse response is a **rectangular pulse of width T and height 1**:

$$g_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The transfer function of the ZOH is therefore:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \mathcal{L}\{g_{\text{ZOH}}(t)\} = \int_0^T e^{-st}\,dt = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

The **frequency response** is:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

The magnitude has a **$(\sin x)/x$** shape ‚Äî a low-pass-like behaviour with infinite zeros at $\omega = 2\pi k/T$ ($k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$). The phase lag is $\omega T/2$ ‚Äî equivalent to a **half-sample delay**, which is why many KTU problems model the ZOH as $G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) \approx \dfrac{T}{1 + sT/2}$ for low frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The combined transfer function of *Sampler + ZOH + Plant* is $G(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot G_p(s)$. This is the **single most tested expression** in the entire module. Do NOT forget the $(1 - e^{-sT})$ factor.

### 2.4.2 First-Order Hold (FOH)

A FOH linearly **extrapolates** between the current sample and the previous sample. Its impulse response is a **triangular pulse of base 2T**:

$$g_{\text{FOH}}(t) = \begin{cases} 1 + \dfrac{t}{T}, & 0 \leq t < T \\ 1 - \dfrac{t}{T}, & T \leq t < 2T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The transfer function is:

$$G_{\text{FOH}}(s) = \frac{(1 - e^{-sT})^2}{s^2 T} \cdot (1 + sT)$$

FOH gives a smoother reconstructed signal than ZOH, but is **rarely used in practice** due to its complexity and tendency to amplify high-frequency noise.

---

## 2.5 Mathematical Modelling of the Sampling Process

### 2.5.1 The Ideal Impulse Sampler

Define the **uniform impulse train**:

$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

The sampled signal is the product:

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$$

> The superscript `*` denotes the **sampled** version. This notation is KTU-standard and is used **without exception** in textbooks.

### 2.5.2 Laplace Transform of a Sampled Signal

Since $\delta_T(t)$ is **periodic** with period $T$, it admits a Fourier series expansion:

$$\delta_T(t) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j k \omega_s t}, \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Multiplying by $r(t)$ and taking the Laplace transform:

$$R^*(s) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} R(s - jk\omega_s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most important formula in Module 1.** It states that the Laplace transform of a sampled signal is an **infinite periodic repetition** of the original signal's transform, shifted by integer multiples of the sampling frequency $j\omega_s$, and scaled by $1/T$. Each shifted copy is a "spectral image" or "harmonic lobe."

### 2.5.3 Frequency-Domain Interpretation

Taking $s = j\omega$:

$$R^*(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} R\bigl(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\bigr)$$

- If the original signal is **band-limited** to $\omega_M$ and we sample with $\omega_s > 2\omega_M$ (the **Shannon‚ÄìNyquist condition**), the spectral images do **not overlap**, and the original spectrum can be recovered with a low-pass filter.
- If $\omega_s < 2\omega_M$, the images **overlap** ‚Üí **aliasing** ‚Üí irreversible information loss.

### 2.5.4 The Sampling Theorem (Shannon‚ÄìNyquist‚ÄìWhittaker)

> A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $\omega_M$ rad/s can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_M$, i.e., $f_s \geq 2f_M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The factor of 2 is **strict**; the actual KTU/control-engineering recommendation is **5√ó to 10√ó** the signal bandwidth in practice, to allow for non-ideal anti-aliasing filters and guard bands.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table.** Every KTU 2024 Module-1 numerical problem will draw 70‚Äì80% of its marks from these exact expressions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Impulse Train | $\delta_T(t) = \sum_k \delta(t-kT)$ | Period $T$ in seconds |
| 2 | Sampled Signal | $r^*(t) = \sum_k r(kT)\,\delta(t-kT)$ | KTU `*` notation |
| 3 | Fourier Series of $\delta_T$ | $\delta_T(t) = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_k e^{jk\omega_s t}$ | $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ rad/s |
| 4 | Laplace of Sampled Signal | $R^*(s) = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_k R(s - jk\omega_s)$ | Infinite sum, periodic in $j\omega$ |
| 5 | ZOH Transfer Function | $G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$ | $e^{-sT}$ is the **key** |
| 6 | ZOH Approximation (low freq.) | $G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) \approx \dfrac{T}{1 + sT/2}$ | Useful for analysis |
| 7 | FOH Transfer Function | $G_{\text{FOH}}(s) = \dfrac{(1 - e^{-sT})^2}{s^2 T}(1 + sT)$ | Rarely used |
| 8 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$ | Volts per LSB |
| 9 | Quantization Error | $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$ | Worst-case bound |
| 10 | Quantization Noise Variance | $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$ | Uniform-distribution model |
| 11 | SQNR (sinusoid) | $6.02n + 1.76$ dB | 6 dB per bit rule |
| 12 | Shannon-Nyquist Rate | $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_M$ | Sampling theorem |
| 13 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1/T$ | Hz |
| 14 | Combined Sampler-ZOH-Plant | $G(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} G_p(s)$ | Star-product block |
| 15 | Output of ZOH for $r^*(t)$ | $y(t) = \sum_k r(kT)\,[u(t-kT) - u(t-(k+1)T)]$ | Piecewise-constant form |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** In tables above, the absolute-value expressions are written as `\vert e_q \vert` etc. ‚Äî using `\mid` or `\vert`, never a raw pipe `|`, because the raw pipe **breaks the markdown table renderer** in many KTU-endorsed PDF/MD pipelines.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Disk-Drive Servos** ‚Äî The read-head position is controlled by a digital servo with a ZOH; the $(1 - e^{-sT})/s$ model is essential for stability analysis.
- **Industrial Process Control (Honeywell, Yokogawa DCS)** ‚Äî Thousands of digital PID loops run in parallel; each loop is a discrete data control system with a fixed sampling period (typically 0.1 s to 1 s).
- **Electric Vehicle Motor Drives (FOC)** ‚Äî Field-Oriented Control algorithms run on DSPs at 10‚Äì20 kHz sampling; quantization noise directly affects torque ripple.
- **Aerospace Flight Control** ‚Äî Sample rates are pushed to >1 kHz; quantization resolution (16‚Äì18 bit ADCs) determines attitude accuracy.
- **5G/6G Digital Predistortion (DPD)** ‚Äî High-speed ADCs/DACs in base-station power amplifiers use the same mathematical model of sampling taught in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, no-skip derivations** that KTU examiners expect when a 7- or 14-mark question is asked on the sampling process or the ZOH.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Laplace Transform of a Sampled Continuous Signal

**Objective:** Starting from the time-domain definition $r^*(t) = \sum_k r(kT)\,\delta(t-kT)$, derive the infinite-sum expression $R^*(s) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_k R(s - jk\omega_s)$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the time-domain sampled signal.**

We begin with the definition of impulse modulation. The continuous signal $r(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train $\delta_T(t)$ whose impulses occur at $t = kT$, $k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$:

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = r(t) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

Since $r(t)$ is continuous, the product with $\delta(t - kT)$ extracts the value of $r(t)$ at the impulse location $t = kT$. This is the sifting property of the Dirac delta. Applying it explicitly:

$$r^*(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Recognize that the impulse train is periodic.**

The function $\delta_T(t)$ is periodic with period $T$. Any periodic function can be expanded as a complex Fourier series. Let $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ denote the sampling angular frequency. The Fourier coefficients are computed by integrating over one period:

$$C_n = \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} \delta_T(t) \, e^{-j n \omega_s t}\,dt = \frac{1}{T}$$

This follows because, within the interval $[-T/2, T/2]$, the impulse train contains exactly one impulse at $t = 0$ (with strength 1), so the integral collapses to $1/T$. The Fourier series is therefore:

$$\delta_T(t) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j n \omega_s t}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute the Fourier series back into the time-domain product.**

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \frac{1}{T} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j n \omega_s t} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} r(t) \, e^{j n \omega_s t}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Take the Laplace transform of both sides.**

Using the linearity of the Laplace transform and the **frequency-shift property** $\mathcal{L}\{r(t)e^{at}\} = R(s - a)$ with $a = jn\omega_s$:

$$R^*(s) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} R(s - jn\omega_s)$$

This is the desired result. $\blacksquare$

**Interpretation:** The transform $R^*(s)$ is a **periodic function of $s$ in the imaginary direction** with period $j\omega_s$. It is built from infinitely many shifted copies of the original transform $R(s)$, each multiplied by $1/T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The summation index $n$ runs over **all integers** ‚Äî including negative ones. The result is symmetric about the imaginary axis because $R(s)$ is generally complex-valued. In most problems, however, the system is real and we only need to consider $n \geq 0$ plus the conjugate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Transfer Function of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

**Objective:** Starting from the impulse response, derive $G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the ZOH impulse response.**

The ZOH holds the input constant for one full sampling period. When fed an impulse $\delta(t)$ of strength 1 at $t = 0$, the ZOH output is a rectangular pulse of unit height lasting from $0$ to $T$:

$$g_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Take the Laplace transform.**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 1/s$ and the time-shift property $\mathcal{L}\{u(t - T)\} = e^{-sT}/s$:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

This is the desired result. $\blacksquare$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Frequency response (for completeness).**

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega} = \frac{e^{-j\omega T/2}(e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2})}{j\omega} = \frac{2e^{-j\omega T/2}\sin(\omega T/2)}{j\omega}$$

Using $j = e^{j\pi/2}$ and $\omega T = (\omega T/2)\cdot 2$:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

**Magnitude:** $\vert G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega)\vert = T \,\vert\text{sinc}(\omega T/2)\vert$ ‚Äî has zeros at $\omega = 2\pi k/T$ for non-zero integers $k$.

**Phase:** $\angle G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = -\omega T/2$ ‚Äî a pure linear phase corresponding to a **half-sample delay**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Quantization Error Variance

**Objective:** Show that the quantization noise variance of a uniform mid-tread quantizer is $\Delta^2/12$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define the quantization error.**

Let $x$ be a continuous input sample. The quantizer output is $Q(x) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}(x/\Delta)$, so the error is $e_q = x - Q(x)$. For a mid-tread quantizer, $e_q$ lies in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2)$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Assume uniform distribution.**

The error is typically modelled as a **uniform random variable** over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2)$, with probability density function $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ within the interval and zero elsewhere. This assumption holds when the input $x$ is "busy" ‚Äî it traverses many quantization levels randomly within one sampling period.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the mean.**

$$\mu_{e_q} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\,de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e_q^2}{2}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = 0$$

The mean is zero ‚Äî the quantizer is unbiased.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the variance.**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = E\{e_q^2\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\,de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot (\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

This is the desired result. $\blacksquare$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Connect to SQNR.**

For a full-scale sinusoid of peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{\text{pp}} = 2^n \Delta$ (using all $2^n$ levels), the signal power is $P_s = V_{\text{pp}}^2/8$. The noise power is $P_n = \Delta^2/12$. Hence:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{(2^n \Delta)^2/8}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20n\log_{10}(2) \approx 1.76 + 6.02n \;\; \text{dB}$$

This is the **"6 dB per bit" rule**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Combined Sampler‚ÄìZOH‚ÄìPlant Transfer Function

**Objective:** Derive the equivalent transfer function of a ZOH feeding a plant $G_p(s)$, when the input to the ZOH is the sampled signal $r^*(t)$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Sampled signal at the ZOH input.**

$$r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Convolution with the ZOH impulse response.**

The ZOH output is the convolution of $r^*(t)$ with $g_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$:

$$y_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = r^*(t) * g_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \bigl[u(t-kT) - u(t-(k+1)T)\bigr]$$

This is the **piecewise-constant staircase** output of the ZOH.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Laplace transform.**

In the Laplace domain, $Y_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = R^*(s) \cdot G_{\text{ZOH}}(s)$. Substituting the ZOH:

$$Y_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = R^*(s) \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Plant output.**

The plant $G_p(s)$ acts on $Y_{\text{ZOH}}(s)$:

$$C(s) = G_p(s) \cdot R^*(s) \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Define the "star-product" block.**

The combined transfer function seen from $R^*(s)$ to $C(s)$ is:

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R^*(s)} = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \cdot G_p(s)$$

This is the **canonical star-transformed plant model** in continuous-time. The factor $(1 - e^{-sT})/s$ is the ZOH, and the multiplication is **not** a simple product in the z-domain ‚Äî it must be handled carefully using the **star-product operator**, which is the topic of Module 2. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparing Sampler, ZOH, and FOH

| Property | Ideal Sampler | Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) | First-Order Hold (FOH) |
|----------|---------------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| Time-domain output for input $\delta(t)$ | $\delta(t)$ | $u(t) - u(t-T)$ | Triangular pulse of base $2T$ |
| Transfer function | 1 (ideal) | $\dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$ | $\dfrac{(1 - e^{-sT})^2}{s^2 T}(1 + sT)$ |
| Frequency response | Flat | $(\sin x)/x$ shape, half-sample delay | Better high-freq. roll-off |
| Reconstruction quality | Requires ideal LPF | Piecewise-constant (staircase) | Piecewise-linear |
| Introduces phase lag? | No | Yes ‚Äî $\omega T/2$ | Yes ‚Äî $\omega T$ approx. |
| Realizability | Not realizable | Easily realizable with a capacitor + switch | More complex |
| KTU usage | Conceptual | **Default in 95% of problems** | Rare |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Canonical Discrete Data Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference Input r(t)"] --> S1{"Ideal Sampler"}
    S1 --> ER["Sampled Reference r*(t)"]
    CMT["Comparator / Error Detector"] --> ER
    FBOUT["Feedback Signal b*(t)"] --> CMT
    ER --> ERR["Error e*(t)"]
    ERR --> ADC["ADC + Quantizer + Encoder"]
    ADC --> DC["Digital Controller / Microprocessor"]
    DC --> DAC["Digital-to-Analog Converter DAC"]
    DAC --> HLD["Hold Device Zero-Order Hold ZOH"]
    HLD --> ACT["Actuator"]
    ACT --> PLT["Plant Gp(s)"]
    PLT --> OUT["Output y(t)"]
    OUT --> FS["Feedback Sensor H(s)"]
    FS --> S2{"Sampler at Feedback"}
    S2 --> FBOUT
    style R fill:#fde2e2
    style OUT fill:#d2f8d2
    style DC fill:#fff3b0
    style ADC fill:#cfe8ff
    style DAC fill:#cfe8ff
    style HLD fill:#e0d4fd
    style PLT fill:#ffd6a5
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **two samplers** (at the reference and at the feedback) ensure both signals entering the comparator are discrete-time.
- The **ADC** quantizes the error to a binary word.
- The **Digital Controller** executes the control law in software.
- The **DAC + ZOH** reconstruct the analog control signal.
- The **Plant + Sensor** complete the physical feedback path.

---

## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Analog Input x(t)"] --> AF["Anti-Aliasing Filter Low-Pass"]
    AF --> SMP{"Sampler"}
    SMP --> QTZ["Quantizer 2 to the n levels"]
    QTZ --> ENC["Encoder Binary to Gray to BCD"]
    ENC --> OUT["n-bit Digital Word x(kT)"]
    style IN fill:#fde2e2
    style OUT fill:#cfe8ff
    style AF fill:#fff3b0
    style QTZ fill:#e0d4fd
    style ENC fill:#ffd6a5
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The anti-aliasing filter band-limits the input to $\omega_M < \omega_s/2$.
- The sampler discretizes time.
- The quantizer discretizes amplitude into $2^n$ levels, introducing the quantization error $e_q$.
- The encoder maps each level to a unique binary codeword (often Gray code to avoid multi-bit errors).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of the Sampling Process ‚Äî Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Continuous Signal r(t)"] --> MUL(("Multiplication by Impulse Train"))
    IT["Impulse Train delta_T(t) = sum delta of t minus kT"] --> MUL
    MUL --> RS["Sampled Signal r*(t) = sum r of kT times delta of t minus kT"]
    RS --> FS["Fourier Series of delta_T: one over T times sum exp of j k omega_s t"]
    FS --> LT["Apply Laplace Transform R*(s) = one over T times sum R of s minus j k omega_s"]
    LT --> SPEC["Spectrum Periodic in j omega with period j omega_s"]
    SPEC --> ALI{"Is omega_s greater than 2 omega_M ?"}
    ALI -->|Yes| OK["No Aliasing ‚Äî original recoverable by LPF"]
    ALI -->|No| BAD["Aliasing ‚Äî Information Lost"]
    style A fill:#fde2e2
    style RS fill:#fff3b0
    style SPEC fill:#cfe8ff
    style OK fill:#d2f8d2
    style BAD fill:#ffb3b3
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The continuous signal $r(t)$ is multiplied by the periodic impulse train.
- The result is a sequence of weighted impulses.
- The Laplace-domain representation shows the spectrum is **periodic with period $j\omega_s$**.
- The decision branch highlights the **Shannon‚ÄìNyquist condition**: $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_M$ avoids aliasing.

---

## 4.4 Timing Diagram ‚Äî ZOH vs FOH Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TLINE["Time Axis"]
        T0["t = 0"] --> T1["t = T"] --> T2["t = 2T"] --> T3["t = 3T"]
    end
    SAMP["Sampled Values r of 0 r of T r of 2T r of 3T"] -.-> T0
    SAMP -.-> T1
    SAMP -.-> T2
    SAMP -.-> T3
    ZOHOUT["ZOH Output: Constant on each interval 0 to T T to 2T 2T to 3T"] --> T0
    ZOHOUT --> T1
    ZOHOUT --> T2
    ZOHOUT --> T3
    FOHOUT["FOH Output: Linear ramp within each interval"] --> T0
    FOHOUT --> T1
    FOHOUT --> T2
    FOHOUT --> T3
    style SAMP fill:#fde2e2
    style ZOHOUT fill:#cfe8ff
    style FOHOUT fill:#e0d4fd
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The ZOH produces a **staircase** waveform.
- The FOH produces a **piecewise-linear** waveform that better approximates the original continuous signal.
- The ZOH is the practical default; the FOH is rarely used in actual hardware.

---

## 4.5 Signal Flow ‚Äî Quantization Error Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Continuous Sample x"] --> QTZ["Quantizer Q of x"]
    QTZ --> XD["Quantized Output x_d"]
    X --> ERR(("Subtraction"))
    XD --> ERR
    ERR --> EQ["Quantization Error e_q bounded by Delta over 2"]
    EQ --> NOISE["Modeled as Uniform White Noise variance Delta squared over 12"]
    style X fill:#fde2e2
    style XD fill:#cfe8ff
    style EQ fill:#fff3b0
    style NOISE fill:#ffd6a5
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The quantizer introduces an error $e_q$ that is **deterministic** for a given input but is **modelled stochastically** as additive white noise.
- The variance is $\Delta^2/12$ for a uniform quantizer.
- This noise model is essential for **SQNR analysis** in digital control loops.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Pattern Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A carries **3 marks** (one concept, 2‚Äì3 sentences expected). Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** between two full questions. Each Part-B question is split into (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks, both fully solved.

---

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **Q: List any six advantages of discrete data control systems over continuous-data control systems.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Model Answer (target: 6 bullets, ~1 mark per justified point √ó 0.5 each = 3 marks):**

A discrete data control system (DDCS) offers several advantages over its continuous counterpart:

1. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** ‚Äî Digital computations are based on binary arithmetic, which is exact and not affected by component tolerances, drift, or aging.
2. **Software-implemented flexibility** ‚Äî The control law (PID gains, filter coefficients) can be changed by editing code, eliminating hardware rewiring.
3. **Superior noise immunity** ‚Äî Binary 0/1 levels tolerate large amplitude noise without corruption, unlike sensitive analog voltages.
4. **Reduced hardware complexity** ‚Äî A single microprocessor replaces dozens of op-amp summers, integrators, and differentiators.
5. **Cost-effective for complex algorithms** ‚Äî Adaptive, fuzzy, neural, and self-tuning controllers are infeasible in analog form but trivial in software.
6. **Built-in diagnostics and communication** ‚Äî Digital systems can log faults, run self-tests, and interface seamlessly with buses (CAN, Modbus, Ethernet).

---

### Question A2

> **Q: Define an ideal sampler. Obtain an expression for the sampled signal $r^*(t)$ in terms of the impulse train $\delta_T(t)$.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **ideal sampler** is a mathematical abstraction that converts a continuous-time signal $r(t)$ into a discrete-time sequence of weighted Dirac impulses. The sampler is modelled as a **switch that closes instantaneously** at regular intervals $T$ seconds apart, producing an output that is mathematically the **product** of the continuous signal and an impulse train.

The **uniform impulse train** is defined as:

$$\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$$

The sampled signal is therefore:

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r(kT) \cdot \delta(t - kT)$$

where the notation `*` denotes the **sampled** version of $r(t)$. The strength of each impulse equals the value of $r(t)$ at the sampling instant $kT$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) ‚Äî 14 Marks

> **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the basic elements of a discrete data control system. Explain the role of the sampler, digital computer, DAC, and hold device. **(7 marks)**
> **Q: (b)** Derive the transfer function of a zero-order hold (ZOH). Show that the ZOH introduces a half-sample delay. **(7 marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Set A]`

---

#### Model Solution to (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above) ‚Äî 2 marks for correct diagram.**

**Description of the basic elements:**

1. **Sampler** ‚Äî A switching device that closes instantaneously at regular intervals $T$ seconds. It converts the continuous error signal $e(t)$ into a discrete-time sequence of weighted impulses $e^*(t)$. The sampler is the entry point of digital processing and is essential because a digital computer cannot accept continuous signals. **(1 mark)**

2. **Digital Computer / Microprocessor** ‚Äî Acts as the **controller** in the loop. It receives the sampled error, runs a control algorithm (PID, state feedback, etc.) coded in software, and outputs a digital control word. The advantage is that complex control laws can be implemented without extra hardware. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** ‚Äî Converts the digital control word from the computer into an analog voltage or current. Without the DAC, the plant cannot be driven because the plant is a continuous physical system. **(1 mark)**

4. **Hold Device (Zero-Order Hold)** ‚Äî Reconstructs a piecewise-constant analog signal from the DAC output, holding each sample value constant over the interval $[kT, (k+1)T)$. This makes the control input **physically realizable**. Without the hold, the output would be a sequence of impulses, which cannot drive an actuator. **(1.5 marks)**

**Concluding sentence (for full 7 marks):** The cascade Sampler ‚Üí ADC ‚Üí Digital Computer ‚Üí DAC ‚Üí ZOH is the **defining signal path** that distinguishes a discrete data control system from a continuous one.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define the ZOH impulse response.** The ZOH holds its input constant for one sampling period. When fed a unit impulse at $t = 0$, the output is a rectangular pulse of height 1 from $t = 0$ to $t = T$:

$$g_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Take the Laplace transform.** Using $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 1/s$ and the time-shift property:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Obtain the frequency response.** Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Factor out a half-period phase.** Using $1 - e^{-j\omega T} = e^{-j\omega T/2}(e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2}) = 2j e^{-j\omega T/2}\sin(\omega T/2)$:

$$G_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = \frac{2j e^{-j\omega T/2}\sin(\omega T/2)}{j\omega} = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Interpret the phase.** The factor $e^{-j\omega T/2}$ represents a **time delay of $T/2$ seconds** ‚Äî a half-sample delay. Hence the ZOH is equivalent, in terms of phase, to a pure delay element of $T/2$. The magnitude $\vert \sin(\omega T/2) / (\omega T/2) \vert$ has a **$(\sin x)/x$** shape. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **sampler and the ZOH are two separate elements** in the block diagram ‚Äî the sampler produces impulses, the ZOH reconstructs the analog signal. Conflating them loses 2 marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) ‚Äî 14 Marks

> **Q: (a)** Explain the quantization process in an analog-to-digital converter. Derive an expression for the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) of an $n$-bit uniform quantizer for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)**
> **Q: (b)** With the help of a clear block diagram, derive the mathematical model of the sampling process. State and explain the Shannon‚ÄìNyquist sampling theorem. **(7 marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Set C]`

---

#### Model Solution to (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Step 1 ‚Äî Describe the quantization process.** Quantization is the mapping of a continuous-amplitude sample $x$ to one of $2^n$ discrete levels. The step size (resolution) is $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^n$. The quantization error $e_q = x - Q(x)$ is bounded by $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$. The error is modelled as an additive uniform white noise with probability density $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2)$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the noise variance.**

$$\sigma_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\,de_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the signal power.** For a full-scale sinusoid with peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{\text{pp}} = 2^n \Delta$:

$$P_s = \frac{V_{\text{pp}}^2}{8} = \frac{(2^n \Delta)^2}{8} = \frac{2^{2n} \Delta^2}{8} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Form the SQNR.**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{2^{2n} \Delta^2 / 8}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Convert to decibels.**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20n \log_{10}(2) \approx 1.76 + 6.02n \quad \text{dB} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}}$$

This is the famous **6 dB per bit rule**: each additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by approximately 6 dB.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Step 1 ‚Äî Block diagram (refer Section 4.3 above).** Show the input $r(t)$ multiplied by the impulse train $\delta_T(t)$ to produce $r^*(t)$, then take the Laplace transform. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 ‚Äî Time-domain expression for $r^*(t)$.**

$$r^*(t) = r(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r(kT)\,\delta(t - kT) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Fourier series of the impulse train.** Since $\delta_T(t)$ is periodic with period $T$, it admits the complex Fourier series:

$$\delta_T(t) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j k \omega_s t}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute and take the Laplace transform.**

$$r^*(t) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r(t)\,e^{j k \omega_s t} \;\Rightarrow\; R^*(s) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} R(s - j k \omega_s) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî State and explain Shannon‚ÄìNyquist theorem.** **(1.5 marks)**

> A band-limited continuous signal $r(t)$ with no spectral content above $\omega_M$ rad/s can be reconstructed **exactly** from its samples $r(kT)$ if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_M$ (equivalently $f_s \geq 2f_M$).

If $\omega_s < 2\omega_M$, the shifted spectra $R(s - jk\omega_s)$ **overlap** ‚Äî this phenomenon is called **aliasing**, and it causes irreversible loss of information. In practice, $\omega_s$ is chosen to be **5√ó to 10√ó $\omega_M$** to allow for non-ideal anti-aliasing filters.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Quantization):** Many students write $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$ but forget the factor of 2 in the variance integral. The correct limits are $\pm \Delta/2$ (mid-tread quantizer), and the variance comes out as $\Delta^2/12$ ‚Äî **not** $\Delta^2/3$. A wrong variance loses 1.5 marks.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Sampling Theorem):** Some students write the Nyquist rate as $f_s \geq f_M$ ‚Äî this is **WRONG**; it must be $f_s \geq 2f_M$ (i.e., **at least twice the highest frequency**, not the highest frequency itself). This is one of the most common errors and costs 1 full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

### ‚úÖ Core Concepts You MUST Know Cold
- [ ] Block diagram of a canonical DDC system with **all 10 elements** labelled.
- [ ] Sampled-signal notation: $r^*(t) = \sum_k r(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$.
- [ ] Laplace transform: $R^*(s) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_k R(s - jk\omega_s)$.
- [ ] ZOH transfer function: $G_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$.
- [ ] FOH transfer function: $G_{\text{FOH}}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})^2 (1 + sT)/(s^2 T)$.
- [ ] ADC resolution: $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$.
- [ ] Quantization error bound: $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$.
- [ ] Quantization noise variance: $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$.
- [ ] SQNR formula: $1.76 + 6.02n$ dB.
- [ ] Shannon‚ÄìNyquist sampling theorem: $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_M$.
- [ ] Combined Sampler‚ÄìZOH‚ÄìPlant transfer function: $G(s) = (1 - e^{-sT}) G_p(s)/s$.

### ‚úÖ Common Pitfalls to AVOID
- [ ] **Don't confuse the sampling rate with the Nyquist rate.** Sampling rate = actual $f_s$ used; Nyquist rate = $2f_M$.
- [ ] **Don't forget the $(1 - e^{-sT})$ factor** when writing the ZOH transfer function.
- [ ] **Don't write $\vert e_q \vert$ using a raw pipe `|` in tables** ‚Äî use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown breakage.
- [ ] **Don't conflate sampler and ZOH** ‚Äî they are **two distinct elements** in the block diagram.
- [ ] **Don't confuse quantization noise variance** ($\Delta^2/12$) with the **maximum error** ($\Delta/2$).
- [ ] **Don't assume the input to the quantizer is bounded** without an anti-aliasing filter ‚Äî aliasing is **irreversible**.

### ‚úÖ Three "Magic" Sentences Examiners Love
1. "*The ZOH can be modelled as a half-sample delay element with a $(\sin x)/x$ frequency shaping.*"
2. "*Each additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by approximately 6 dB.*"
3. "*The Laplace transform of a sampled signal is a periodic function of $s$ in the imaginary direction, with period $j\omega_s$.*"

### ‚úÖ Numerical Problem Recipe (for fast solving)
1. Identify $T$ and $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$.
2. Write $r(t)$ and evaluate $r(kT)$ for the first 2‚Äì3 samples.
3. Apply the requested operation (ZOH reconstruction, quantization, etc.).
4. Use the formula sheet in Section 2.6 to substitute.
5. **Always include units** in the final answer ‚Äî KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

### ‚úÖ High-Probability Exam Questions
- "Derive the transfer function of the ZOH and show it introduces a half-sample delay." ‚Äî **Almost guaranteed.**
- "State and prove the Shannon‚ÄìNyquist sampling theorem." ‚Äî **Almost guaranteed.**
- "Explain quantization and derive the SQNR for an $n$-bit ADC." ‚Äî **Very common.**
- "Compare ZOH and FOH with sketches." ‚Äî **Common in Part-A.**
- "List advantages of digital control systems." ‚Äî **Common 3-mark Part-A.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Reconstruction and Filtering of Sampled Signals: Zero Order Hold, First Order Hold and Polygonal Hold
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Reconstruction and Filtering of Sampled Signals: Zero Order Hold, First Order Hold and Polygonal Hold

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Fundamental Need for Data Reconstruction

In any practical **Digital Control System (DCS)**, the continuous-time (analog) plant must be driven by a continuous signal, but the digital controller (microprocessor, DSP, FPGA) can only output **discrete-time samples** at the sampling instants $t = 0, T, 2T, \dots, kT$, where $T$ is the **sampling period**. A purely discrete sequence of impulses is physically unrealizable. Therefore, a **Data Reconstruction Device** (also called a **Reconstruction Filter** or **Hold Device**) is required at the Digital-to-Analog (D/A) converter stage to "fill in" the gaps between samples and produce a smooth, physically realizable continuous-time signal $y(t)$ that can actuate the plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The reconstruction filter is the *bridge* between the discrete digital domain and the continuous analog domain. The three canonical hold circuits studied are the **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**, **First-Order Hold (FOH)**, and **Polygonal Hold (PH)**. Of these, the ZOH is overwhelmingly the most common in real D/A converters.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):** A reconstruction device that holds the value of each sample $x(kT)$ **constant** for the entire interval $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$. The output is a **piecewise-constant (staircase) signal** of order zero (no derivatives are continuous).

> [!NOTE]
> **First-Order Hold (FOH):** A reconstruction device that **linearly extrapolates** between the *previous* sample $x((k-1)T)$ and the *current* sample $x(kT)$, producing a **piecewise-linear** approximation. The output has continuous first derivative (slope) within each segment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Polygonal Hold (PH):** A reconstruction device that performs **linear interpolation** between the *current* and *next* sample (a causal variant), or equivalently between successive samples in a piecewise-linear fashion. It produces a polygonal/polyline waveform ‚Äî hence the name. *(Note: In many KTU textbooks, the FOH and Polygonal Hold are used interchangeably, but the FOH is an extrapolation-based version, while the Polygonal Hold strictly refers to the triangle-hold shape formed by joining sample points.)*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Staircase vs Ramp vs Zigzag" Picture

Imagine a child is told a number by his teacher every second: **"3, 5, 7, 4, 6..."** and he has to write that number on a board continuously between the announcements.

- **Zero-Order Hold behavior:** The child writes the *new number* and *freezes his hand* for the entire next second. The board shows a **staircase**: 3 (flat) ‚Üí 5 (jump up, flat) ‚Üí 7 (jump up, flat) ‚Üí 4 (jump down, flat)...
- **First-Order Hold behavior:** The child, hearing "3" and then "5" one second later, *predicts* the value will keep rising and draws a **ramp from 3 to 5** over that second ‚Äî but starts the ramp *immediately* from the current sample. When "7" arrives, he ramps steeply up to it; when "4" arrives, he ramps sharply *down* (overshoots/undershoots).
- **Polygonal Hold behavior:** The child simply *connects the dots* ‚Äî he draws a straight line from 3 to 5, then from 5 to 7, then from 7 to 4, forming a **zig-zag polyline** (polygonal chain). The line goes up, up, then sharply down.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** A **staircase plotter** (ZOH) is used in digital oscilloscopes in "DOT" vs "VECTOR" mode. Most low-cost DACs use a ZOH internally, followed by a low-pass analog filter (a "reconstruction filter") to smooth the staircase corners into a smooth curve. This is exactly the textbook diagram of a compact disc (CD) player's DAC!

### 1.4 The Role of the Hold in the Closed-Loop System

In a canonical digital control loop, the signal flow is:

$$e(t) \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} e(kT) \xrightarrow{\text{Processor}} u(kT) \xrightarrow{\text{Hold}} u(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Plant}} y(t)$$

The hold circuit's transfer function, $G_h(s)$, becomes part of the **continuous-time equivalent model** of the digital portion of the system. It is therefore essential to derive its transfer function accurately for stability and transient analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ‚Äî Step-by-Step

#### **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) ‚Äî Operational Logic**

1. At instant $t = kT$, the sample $x(kT)$ arrives at the hold input.
2. The ZOH output is *clamped* to the value $x(kT)$ via an internal latch/sample-and-hold amplifier.
3. The output remains **constant** at $x(kT)$ for the entire interval $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$.
4. At $t = (k+1)T$, the latch is updated instantly to the new value $x((k+1)T)$, and the cycle repeats.
5. The resulting waveform is a **staircase**, with vertical jumps of magnitude $\Delta x = x((k+1)T) - x(kT)$ at every sampling instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the ZOH have a delay of $T/2$?** Because on average, a sample represents information from the *middle* of the previous interval. This manifests as a **half-sample phase lag** in its frequency response.

#### **First-Order Hold (FOH) ‚Äî Operational Logic**

1. The FOH must *store* the previous sample $x((k-1)T)$ and the current sample $x(kT)$.
2. The slope of the line segment is computed as: $m_k = \dfrac{x(kT) - x((k-1)T)}{T}$.
3. The FOH extrapolates linearly: $y(t) = x(kT) + m_k \cdot (t - kT)$ for $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$.
4. At $t = (k+1)T$, the extrapolation lands exactly on $x((k+1)T)$ only if the data is truly linear; otherwise there is a **mismatch** (extrapolation error).
5. The output is piecewise-linear with **discontinuous slopes** at the sample instants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse FOH with Polygonal Hold. Remember: **FOH uses only PAST samples** (causal extrapolation), while **Polygonal Hold connects CURRENT and PAST** (causal interpolation). The Polygonal Hold has zero average error for ramp inputs, while the FOH has zero error for step inputs.

### 2.2 Derivation of Hold Transfer Functions (Conceptual Setup)

The transfer function of any hold circuit can be derived as the **Laplace transform of its unit-impulse response**, or equivalently as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the sampled input $X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) e^{-kTs}$.

The ZOH impulse response is a **rectangular pulse** of height 1 and width $T$:

$$g_{h0}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

The FOH impulse response is a **trapezoidal pulse**: rises linearly from 0 to 1 over $[0,T]$, stays at 1 momentarily, then falls linearly from 1 to 0 over $[T,2T]$.

The Polygonal Hold impulse response is a **triangular pulse**: rises linearly from 0 to 1 over $[0,T]$, then falls linearly from 1 to 0 over $[T,2T]$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table for last-minute revision!**

| **Hold Type** | **Time-Domain Output $y(t)$ for $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$** | **Transfer Function $G_h(s)$** | **Magnitude $\vert G_h(j\omega) \vert$** | **Phase $\angle G_h(j\omega)$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** | $y(t) = x(kT)$ (constant) | $\dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | $T \cdot \left\vert \operatorname{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega T}{2}\right) \right\vert = \dfrac{\left\vert \sin(\omega T/2) \right\vert}{\omega/2}$ | $-\dfrac{\omega T}{2}$ (radians) |
| **First-Order Hold (FOH)** | $y(t) = x(kT) + \dfrac{x(kT) - x((k-1)T)}{T}(t - kT)$ | $\dfrac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2 (1 + Ts)}{T s^2}$ | $\dfrac{2(1 + \omega^2 T^2)^{1/2} \cdot \sin^2(\omega T/2)}{\omega^2 T}$ | $\arctan(\omega T) - \omega T$ |
| **Polygonal Hold (PH)** | $y(t) = x(kT) + \dfrac{x((k+1)T) - x(kT)}{T}(t - kT)$ | $\dfrac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2}{T s^2}$ | $\dfrac{2 \sin^2(\omega T/2)}{\omega^2 T / 2} = T \cdot \operatorname{sinc}^2(\omega T/2)$ | $-\omega T$ (radians) |
| **Pure Delay (for reference)** | $y(t) = x(t - T/2)$ | $e^{-Ts/2}$ | $1$ | $-\omega T/2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sinc function convention used in KTU textbooks:** $\operatorname{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(x)}{x}$ (unnormalized). The ZOH's sinc-shaped frequency response is what makes it behave as a **natural low-pass filter**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ZOH in Industry:** Used in virtually every commercial D/A converter, including audio CD players, motor drives, and PLC analog output modules. It is cheap, stable, and well-behaved.
- **FOH in Industry:** Rarely used in pure form because the extrapolation overshoot can damage actuators. It appears in some **motion control interpolators** (e.g., CNC machines) and in **B-spline** based path planning.
- **Polygonal Hold:** Used in **digital oscilloscopes** in "vector" display mode to draw straight lines between sample points, and in some **signal generators** for synthesizing smooth waveforms.
- **Sinc-Shaped Frequency Response:** The ZOH's sinc behavior is exploited in the design of **compensation networks** for sampled-data systems ‚Äî its inherent low-pass filtering attenuates high-frequency noise and aliases before the analog domain.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Zero-Order Hold Transfer Function

**Step 1: Define the ZOH impulse response.**
The ZOH produces a rectangular pulse of unit height and width $T$ in response to a unit impulse:

$$g_{h0}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

**Step 2: Take the Laplace transform.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(s) &= \mathcal{L}\{u(t) - u(t-T)\} \\
&= \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} - \mathcal{L}\{u(t-T)\} \\
&= \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} \\
&= \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $s = j\omega$ to get the frequency response.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(j\omega) &= \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Rationalize the numerator using Euler's formula.**
Recall that $1 - e^{-j\theta} = e^{-j\theta/2}(e^{j\theta/2} - e^{-j\theta/2}) = e^{-j\theta/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\theta/2)$.

Applying with $\theta = \omega T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - e^{-j\omega T} &= e^{-j\omega T/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega T/2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(j\omega) &= \frac{e^{-j\omega T/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega T/2)}{j\omega} \\
&= \frac{2\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Identify magnitude and phase.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|G_{h0}(j\omega)| &= \frac{2|\sin(\omega T/2)|}{\omega} = T \cdot \left|\frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2}\right| = T \cdot |\operatorname{sinc}(\omega T/2)|
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle G_{h0}(j\omega) &= -\frac{\omega T}{2} \quad \text{(radians)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: DC gain check.**
Using L'H√¥pital's rule as $\omega \to 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{\omega \to 0} |G_{h0}(j\omega)| &= \lim_{\omega \to 0} \frac{2\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega} = \lim_{\omega \to 0} \frac{2 \cdot (\omega T/2)}{\omega} = T
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The DC gain is $T$ (not 1!), which is a *scaling artifact* due to the impulse-sampled input convention. When modeling a digital-to-analog converter, we use $X^*(s) = \sum x(kT) e^{-kTs}$, so the ZOH output's average value naturally contains this factor of $T$. Engineers often absorb this $T$ into the plant model.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the First-Order Hold Transfer Function

**Step 1: Construct the FOH impulse response.**
The FOH response to a unit impulse is a trapezoidal pulse. Mathematically, it is the integral of two ZOHs (each with width $T$) ‚Äî one delayed by $T$ and subtracted. Equivalently:

$$g_{h1}(t) = \frac{t}{T}[u(t) - u(t-T)] - \frac{t-T}{T}[u(t-T) - u(t-2T)] + [u(t-T) - u(t-2T)]$$

A cleaner approach: build it as the **convolution** of a ZOH with a ramp generator.

**Step 2: Use the ramp-ZOH representation.**
The FOH impulse response equals the convolution of the ZOH rectangular pulse $g_{h0}(t)$ with a **zero-mean ramp** $r(t)$ of duration $T$:

$$g_{h1}(t) = g_{h0}(t) * r(t), \quad \text{where } r(t) = \frac{1}{T}[u(t) - u(t-T)]$$

Therefore, in the Laplace domain (and using the ZOH result):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h1}(s) &= G_{h0}(s) \cdot R(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{Ts} = \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2}{T s^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the derivative correction (for the FOH, not the Polygonal Hold).**
The first-order hold has a "linear extrapolation" nature that gives it a transfer function of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h1}(s) &= \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2 (1 + Ts)}{T s^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The extra $(1 + Ts)$ factor is what distinguishes the FOH from the Polygonal Hold. It comes from the **linear extrapolation** of the slope forward from the current sample.

**Step 4: Derive the frequency response.**
Substituting $s = j\omega$ and using $1 + j\omega T = \sqrt{1 + \omega^2 T^2} \cdot e^{j\arctan(\omega T)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|G_{h1}(j\omega)| &= \frac{|1 - e^{-j\omega T}|^2 \cdot |1 + j\omega T|}{T \omega^2} \\
&= \frac{4\sin^2(\omega T/2) \cdot \sqrt{1 + \omega^2 T^2}}{T \omega^2} \\
&= \frac{2\sqrt{1 + \omega^2 T^2} \cdot \sin^2(\omega T/2)}{\omega^2 T / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle G_{h1}(j\omega) &= \angle(1 - e^{-j\omega T})^2 + \angle(1 + j\omega T) - \angle(T) - \angle(j\omega)^2 \\
&= 2 \cdot (-\omega T/2) + \arctan(\omega T) - 0 - \pi \\
&= -\omega T + \arctan(\omega T) - \pi
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait ‚Äî let me carefully re-derive the phase. Since $(1-e^{-j\omega T}) = 2j\sin(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$, we have:

$$\angle(1 - e^{-j\omega T}) = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\omega T}{2}$$

For $\sin(\omega T/2) > 0$ (i.e., $0 < \omega T/2 < \pi$), this equals $\pi/2 - \omega T/2$.

So $\angle(1 - e^{-j\omega T})^2 = \pi - \omega T$.

And $\angle(1 + j\omega T) = \arctan(\omega T)$.

Total: $\angle G_{h1}(j\omega) = \pi - \omega T + \arctan(\omega T) - \pi = \arctan(\omega T) - \omega T$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The phase of the FOH is *less negative* than the ZOH at low frequencies (because the $\arctan(\omega T)$ term provides positive phase). This means the FOH **introduces less phase lag** than the ZOH, which can be advantageous for stability ‚Äî but the gain curve has a faster rolloff and a peakier response.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Polygonal Hold Transfer Function

**Step 1: Define the impulse response.**
The Polygonal Hold responds to a unit impulse with a **triangular pulse** of base $2T$ and peak value 1 at $t = T$:

$$g_{hp}(t) = \frac{t}{T}[u(t) - u(t-T)] + \frac{2T - t}{T}[u(t-T) - u(t-2T)]$$

**Step 2: Take the Laplace transform term-by-term.**

First term: $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{t}{T}[u(t) - u(t-T)]\right\} = \dfrac{1}{Ts^2}(1 - e^{-Ts}) - \dfrac{e^{-Ts}}{s}$

Second term: $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{2T - t}{T}[u(t-T) - u(t-2T)]\right\} = \dfrac{2(1 - e^{-Ts})}{s^2 T} - \dfrac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2}{Ts^2}$

Combining and simplifying (a non-trivial algebraic manipulation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{hp}(s) &= \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2}{T s^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Derive the frequency response.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{hp}(j\omega) &= \frac{(1 - e^{-j\omega T})^2}{T (j\omega)^2} = \frac{4\sin^2(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T}}{-T \omega^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitude:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|G_{hp}(j\omega)| &= \frac{4\sin^2(\omega T/2)}{T \omega^2} = T \cdot \left[\frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2}\right]^2 = T \cdot \operatorname{sinc}^2(\omega T/2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Phase: $\angle G_{hp}(j\omega) = -\omega T$ (radians) ‚Äî a pure time delay of $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU observation:** The Polygonal Hold is a **pure time delay of $T$** at all frequencies (constant phase slope $-\omega T$). This is why it is sometimes called a "fractional delay" element ‚Äî its magnitude response alone determines the system behavior, with no additional phase distortion.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix ‚Äî The Three Holds at a Glance

| **Property** | **ZOH** | **FOH** | **Polygonal Hold** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Output shape | Staircase | Piecewise-linear (extrapolated) | Piecewise-linear (interpolated) |
| Transfer function | $\dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | $\dfrac{(1-e^{-Ts})^2(1+Ts)}{Ts^2}$ | $\dfrac{(1-e^{-Ts})^2}{Ts^2}$ |
| DC gain | $T$ | $T$ | $T$ |
| Magnitude response | $T \cdot \operatorname{sinc}(\omega T/2)$ | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{1+\omega^2 T^2}\sin^2(\omega T/2)}{\omega^2 T/2}$ | $T \cdot \operatorname{sinc}^2(\omega T/2)$ |
| Phase response | $-\omega T/2$ | $\arctan(\omega T) - \omega T$ | $-\omega T$ |
| First zero of $\vert G_h \vert$ | $\omega = 2\pi/T$ | $\omega = 2\pi/T$ (and asymptotically rising) | $\omega = 2\pi/T$ |
| Common use | Standard D/A converters | Motion control, B-splines | Digital scopes, signal synthesis |
| Overshoot on step | None | Yes (50% of step) | None (interpolates linearly) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the exam:** "**ZOH is Z**ero-slope, **FOH is First-derivative** continuous, **Polygonal is P**urely linear between points."
