## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question Paper, May 2024]**

**Define Zero-Order Hold (ZOH). Derive its transfer function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Zero-Order Hold is a data reconstruction device that holds the value of each sample $x(kT)$ constant for the entire sampling interval $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$, producing a staircase (piecewise-constant) output.

> **Derivation (2 marks):** The ZOH impulse response is a rectangular pulse:
> $$g_{h0}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$
> Taking the Laplace transform:
> $$G_{h0}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question Paper, Dec 2023]**

**Compare Zero-Order Hold and First-Order Hold with respect to (i) output waveform, (ii) transfer function, and (iii) phase lag.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Property** | **ZOH** | **FOH** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Output waveform | Staircase (piecewise-constant) | Piecewise-linear (extrapolated) |
| Transfer function | $\dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | $\dfrac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2 (1 + Ts)}{T s^2}$ |
| Phase lag | $-\omega T/2$ (half-sample delay) | $\arctan(\omega T) - \omega T$ (less than ZOH at low $\omega$) |

The ZOH has **no overshoot** but exhibits a **staircase ripple**, while the FOH produces a **smoother signal** but **overshoots** sudden changes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** omit the $T$ in the denominator of the FOH and Polygonal Hold transfer functions ‚Äî this is a 1-mark deduction across the entire solution.
> 2. **Do not** confuse the $(1 + Ts)$ factor ‚Äî it appears ONLY in the FOH, not the Polygonal Hold.
> 3. **Always show the impulse response shape** before taking its Laplace transform. Marks are awarded for *identifying* the waveform (rectangle, trapezoid, triangle) before the math.
> 4. **Phase derivation:** L'H√¥pital's rule is required to evaluate the DC gain. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Final answer must be in the standard $G_h(s)$ form**, not just the impulse response plot. Plot alone = 0 marks for Part B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question Paper, May 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH). Hence obtain its frequency response and plot its magnitude response. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the transfer function of a First-Order Hold (FOH) and explain why it has a $(1 + Ts)$ factor in the numerator. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) ‚Äî ZOH Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the ZOH impulse response as a rectangular pulse of unit height and width $T$:

$$g_{h0}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Take the Laplace transform:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(s) &= \mathcal{L}\{u(t) - u(t-T)\} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-Ts}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $s = j\omega$ and use Euler's identity $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta/2) e^{-j\theta/2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h0}(j\omega) &= \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega} = \frac{2\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify magnitude and phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|G_{h0}(j\omega)| &= \frac{2|\sin(\omega T/2)|}{\omega} = T \cdot \left|\frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2}\right| \\
\angle G_{h0}(j\omega) &= -\frac{\omega T}{2} \text{ radians}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Plot description ‚Äî The magnitude response is a **sinc function** with the following key features:
- DC value $= T$ at $\omega = 0$
- First zero at $\omega = 2\pi/T$ (i.e., $\omega = \omega_s$, the sampling frequency)
- Successive nulls at $\omega = 2n\pi/T$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$
- Slow $1/\omega$ rolloff in the envelope
- The phase is a **straight line** with slope $-T/2$

---

**Part (b) ‚Äî FOH Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The FOH impulse response is a **trapezoidal pulse**: a unit-amplitude plateau from $t=0$ to $t=T$ with triangular skirts rising from $0$ to $1$ over $[0,T]$ and falling from $1$ to $0$ over $[T,2T]$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The FOH output for $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$ is:

$$y(t) = x(kT) + \frac{x(kT) - x((k-1)T)}{T}(t - kT)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The trapezoidal impulse response can be viewed as the **convolution of the ZOH rectangular pulse with a linear ramp of slope $1/T$**, giving in the Laplace domain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{h1}(s) &= G_{h0}(s) \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{Ts} \cdot (1 + Ts) \\
&= \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2 (1 + Ts)}{T s^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The $(1 + Ts)$ factor arises because the FOH performs **linear extrapolation** using the slope between the previous and current sample, then extends the line forward. This extrapolation is equivalent to a ZOH cascaded with a **proportional-plus-derivative** stage $(1 + Ts)$, where the derivative action "anticipates" the next sample's value. Without this factor, we would obtain the Polygonal Hold, which only *interpolates* between two samples.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question Paper, Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a Polygonal Hold and show that it is equivalent to a pure time delay of $T$ seconds. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Zero-Order Hold with sampling period $T = 0.1$ s receives a sampled sine wave $x(kT) = \sin(k\pi/5)$. Find the first three non-zero frequencies at which the output magnitude is **zero**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) ‚Äî Polygonal Hold Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The Polygonal Hold interpolates linearly between current and next sample:

$$y(t) = x(kT) + \frac{x((k+1)T) - x(kT)}{T}(t - kT), \quad kT \leq t < (k+1)T$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The impulse response is a **triangular pulse** of base $2T$ and peak 1 at $t = T$:

$$g_{hp}(t) = \frac{t}{T}[u(t) - u(t-T)] + \frac{2T - t}{T}[u(t-T) - u(t-2T)]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Taking the Laplace transform and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{hp}(s) &= \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})^2}{T s^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{hp}(j\omega) &= \frac{(1 - e^{-j\omega T})^2}{(j\omega)^2 T} = \frac{4\sin^2(\omega T/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega T}}{-T \omega^2} = \frac{4\sin^2(\omega T/2)}{T \omega^2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $e^{-j\omega T}$ is **the transfer function of a pure time delay of $T$ seconds**, hence the Polygonal Hold is equivalent to a $\operatorname{sinc}^2(\omega T/2)$-shaped magnitude response in series with a delay of $T$.

---

**Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T = 0.1$ s, signal $x(kT) = \sin(k\pi/5)$. Sampling frequency $\omega_s = 2\pi/T = 20\pi$ rad/s.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Recall the ZOH magnitude response:

$$|G_{h0}(j\omega)| = T \cdot \left|\frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega T/2}\right|$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Zeros occur when $\sin(\omega T/2) = 0$, i.e., $\omega T/2 = n\pi$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$:

$$\omega_n = \frac{2n\pi}{T} = 2n\pi \cdot 10 = 20n\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The first three non-zero frequencies:

| $n$ | $\omega_n$ (rad/s) | $f_n$ (Hz) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $20\pi \approx 62.83$ | 10 |
| 2 | $40\pi \approx 125.66$ | 20 |
| 3 | $60\pi \approx 188.50$ | 30 |

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Note that these frequencies coincide with **multiples of the sampling frequency** $\omega_s = 20\pi$ rad/s. The ZOH therefore acts as a **natural anti-aliasing filter**, completely rejecting frequency components at integer multiples of $\omega_s$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- üîë **ZOH is the default hold** in 99% of practical D/A converters ‚Äî it is **cheap, stable, and never overshoots**.
- üîë **ZOH transfer function:** $G_{h0}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ ‚Äî the **single most important formula** in this module.
- üîë **ZOH phase lag** $= -\omega T/2$ ‚Äî equivalent to a **half-sample delay**, NOT a full-sample delay.
- üîë **ZOH magnitude zeros** occur at $\omega = n \cdot \omega_s = n \cdot (2\pi/T)$, providing natural rejection of sampling-frequency multiples.
- üîë **FOH transfer function** has the unique factor $(1 + Ts)$ ‚Äî distinguishes it from Polygonal Hold. This factor gives **less phase lag** but introduces **overshoot** and **high-frequency gain peaking**.
- üîë **Polygonal Hold** is a **pure time delay of $T$** in phase, with a $\operatorname{sinc}^2$ magnitude shape ‚Äî twice as aggressive in low-pass filtering as the ZOH.
- üîë **Sampling theorem:** The holds only work properly if the input signal is **band-limited** to $\omega < \omega_s/2$ (Nyquist criterion). Otherwise, **aliasing** corrupts the reconstructed signal.
- üîë **Order of hold = polynomial order used for reconstruction:** ZOH = 0th-order (constant), FOH = 1st-order (linear). Higher-order holds (2nd, 3rd) exist but are **never used in practice** due to instability and overshoot.
- üîë **DC gain is $T$** for all three holds ‚Äî a mathematical artifact of the impulse-sampling assumption that must be absorbed into the plant model.
- üîë **KTU frequently asked question:** "*Compare ZOH and FOH*" or "*Show that the Polygonal Hold is equivalent to a delay of $T$*" ‚Äî these appear almost every semester in Part B.
- üîë **Engineering rule of thumb:** The ZOH is often approximated as $\dfrac{T}{1 + Ts/2}$ ‚Äî a first-order lag with time constant $T/2$ ‚Äî for **approximate frequency response analysis** in the low-frequency range ($\omega \ll \omega_s$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Control Systems - Pulse Transfer Function. Z Transform Analysis of Closed Loop and Open Loop Systems - Modified Z-Transfer Function, Difference Equation. Solution by Recursion and Z Transform. Steady State Error Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Control Systems - Pulse Transfer Function. Z Transform Analysis of Closed Loop and Open Loop Systems - Modified Z-Transfer Function, Difference Equation. Solution by Recursion and Z Transform. Steady State Error Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Pulse Transfer Function (PTF)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Pulse Transfer Function** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) discrete-time system is defined as the ratio of the Z-transform of the output sequence to the Z-transform of the input sequence, under the assumption of **zero initial conditions**.
>
> $$G(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \quad \text{(with zero initial conditions)}$$

In the **Z-domain**, this function completely characterizes the input-output behaviour of a sampled-data system, just as the Laplace transfer function $G(s)$ characterizes a continuous-time system. It is derived by taking the Z-transform of the sampled impulse response $g(kT)$ of the system.

$$\boxed{G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{g(kT)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT)\, z^{-k}}$$

#### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE] **The "Discretized Conveyor Belt" Analogy**
> Imagine a continuous conveyor belt moving boxes smoothly. Now imagine a robotic arm that takes a **snapshot (sample)** of the belt's state every second and decides how to adjust the speed.
>
> - The **continuous transfer function** $G(s)$ describes the entire belt's behaviour at *every* instant of time.
> - The **pulse transfer function** $G(z)$ describes how the system behaves **only at the sampling instants** (every 1 second, 2 seconds, etc.).
>
> It is like a series of still photographs that, when played in sequence, reveal the system's trajectory. The PTF is the mathematical "rulebook" that converts each photograph (sample) into the next one.

### 1.2 Z-Transform Analysis of Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Systems

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Concept**
> In digital control systems, signals are sampled using an **ideal sampler**. The Z-transform of a sampled signal $r^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,\delta(t-kT)$ is:
>
> $$R(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\, z^{-k}$$
>
> For a **cascade of blocks**, the output's Z-transform is the **product** of the individual pulse transfer functions only if a **sampler exists between every block**. Otherwise, the rule is: take the Z-transform of the *entire* continuous transfer function as a single unit.

#### Open-Loop Configuration

$$C(z) = G(z)\,R(z)$$

#### Closed-Loop Configuration (Unity Feedback)

$$M(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

#### Closed-Loop Configuration (Non-Unity Feedback with $H(z)$)

$$M(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

### 1.3 Modified Z-Transfer Function

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition**
> The **Modified Z-Transfer Function** $G(z, m)$ captures the response of a digital system at instants **between two consecutive sampling instants** ‚Äî i.e., at time $t = (k + m)T$, where $0 \le m \le 1$.
>
> $$G(z, m) = \mathcal{Z}_m\{G(s)\} = z^{-1} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT + mT)\, z^{-k}$$
>
> It is indispensable for analyzing **transportation lag**, **inter-sample behaviour**, and **dead-time elements**.

### 1.4 Difference Equation

> [!NOTE] **System Representation**
> A discrete-time LTI system can be modelled by an $n$-th order **linear constant-coefficient difference equation**:
>
> $$c(k+n) + a_1 c(k+n-1) + \dots + a_n c(k) = b_0 r(k+n) + b_1 r(k+n-1) + \dots + b_n r(k)$$
>
> This is the time-domain counterpart of the PTF. Each value of $c(k)$ depends on past input and output samples ‚Äî making it the discrete analog of a differential equation.

### 1.5 Steady-State Error Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition**
> **Steady-state error** $e_{ss}$ is the difference between the desired reference input and the actual output as $k \to \infty$. For a **Type-0, Type-1, or Type-2** digital system, the static error constants ($K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$) determine $e_{ss}$ for step, ramp, and parabolic inputs, respectively.

The standard input forms and their finite steady-state errors are:

| Input Type | Time Function $r(t)$ | $R(z)$ | $e_{ss}$ Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit Step | $1$ | $\frac{z}{z-1}$ | $\frac{1}{1+K_p}$ |
| Unit Ramp | $t$ | $\frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\frac{1}{K_v}$ |
| Unit Parabolic | $\frac{t^2}{2}$ | $\frac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3}$ | $\frac{1}{K_a}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Pulse Transfer Function ‚Äî Operational Logic

1. **Start with the continuous plant** $G_p(s)$.
2. **Place an ideal sampler** at the input with period $T$.
3. **Star-red notation**: $G(s)$ with an ideal sampler at input ‚Üí sampled signal $G^*(s)$.
4. **Apply the Z-transform**: $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{g(kT)\}$ where $g(kT)$ is sampled impulse response.
5. For systems with a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**, prepend the ZOH transfer function $G_h(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ to the plant.

> [!NOTE] **Starred Transform Property**
> If two blocks $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$ are in series **with no sampler between them**, their combined starred transfer function is:
> $$[G_1 G_2]^*(s) = G_1^*(s)\, G_2^*(s) \quad \text{is NOT valid!}$$
> Correct form: $[G_1(s) G_2(s)]^* = \mathcal{L}\{g_1(kT) \cdot g_2(kT)\}$.

### 2.2 Z-Transform Block-Diagram Algebra ‚Äî The Six Rules

| Rule | Configuration | Pulse Transfer Function |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | $G(s)$ preceded by sampler | $G(z)$ |
| **R2** | $G(s)$ with no sampler at input | $\mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\} = G(z)$ only if input is already sampled |
| **R3** | $G_1(s) \to \text{sampler} \to G_2(s)$ | $G_1(z)\, G_2(z)$ |
| **R4** | Sampler at input of unity feedback with $G(s)$ in forward path | $\frac{G(z)}{1+G(z)}$ |
| **R5** | $G(s)$ forward, $H(s)$ feedback, sampler in error path | $\frac{G(z)}{1+G(z)H(z)}$ |
| **R6** | Digital controller $D(z)$ and plant $G(z)$ | $D(z) G(z)$ (both already discrete) |

### 2.3 Modified Z-Transfer Function ‚Äî Operational Logic

The modified Z-transform introduces a fictitious delay of $mT$ ($0 \le m \le 1$) into the system to study response at intermediate times:

$$\boxed{G(z, m) = z^{-1} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT + mT)\, z^{-k} = \mathcal{Z}_m\{G(s)\}}$$

A common practical formulation is:

$$G(z, m) = z^{-1}\,\mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\, e^{mTs}\}\big|_{z = e^{Ts}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Utility in Engineering**
> Modified Z-transfer functions are used in **dead-time compensation** (Smith Predictor), **transportation lag analysis**, and verifying **inter-sample ripple** in digital controllers ‚Äî all crucial in process control and aerospace applications.

### 2.4 Difference Equation & Solution Methods

**Two primary methods** to solve difference equations:

1. **Method of Recursion (Direct Substitution)**: Compute successive values $c(0), c(1), c(2), \dots$ by direct substitution using the difference equation and given initial conditions. Simple but provides no closed-form answer.
2. **Method of Z-Transform**: Apply the Z-transform to both sides of the difference equation (incorporating initial conditions), solve algebraically for $C(z)$, and then apply **inverse Z-transform** (partial fractions or long division) to recover $c(kT)$.

### 2.5 Steady-State Error ‚Äî Static Error Constants

For a **unity feedback** digital system with open-loop PTF $G(z)$ and input $R(z)$:

$$E(z) = \frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

Applying the **Final Value Theorem** (Z-domain):

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \lim_{k \to \infty} e(kT) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{R(z)}{1+G(z)}}$$

The static error constants are:

$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) \quad ; \quad K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{T\,z} G(z) \quad ; \quad K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2\,z} G(z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> Steady-state error analysis is the **backbone of tracking control**. For example, in a **CNC milling machine**, zero steady-state error to a step input ensures the cutter reaches the exact commanded position. In **satellite antenna pointing systems**, zero velocity error ensures the antenna tracks moving targets without lag.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Conditions / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pulse Transfer Function | $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$ | Zero initial conditions |
| 2 | With ZOH | $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ | ZOH precedes plant |
| 3 | Unity Feedback Closed-Loop | $M(z) = G(z)/[1+G(z)]$ | Sampler in error path |
| 4 | Non-Unity Feedback | $M(z) = G(z)/[1+G(z)H(z)]$ | Sampler in error path |
| 5 | Modified Z-TF | $G(z,m) = z^{-1} \mathcal{Z}\{G(s) e^{mTs}\}$ | $0 \le m \le 1$ |
| 6 | Standard Difference Equation | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i c(k+n-i) = \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j r(k+m-j)$ | $a_0 = 1$ (assumed) |
| 7 | Recursion Solution | $c(k) = f\{c(k-1), \dots, r(k), \dots\}$ | Iterative substitution |
| 8 | Final Value Theorem | $\lim_{k \to \infty} c(k) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) C(z)/z$ | Poles of $(z-1)C(z)/z$ inside unit circle |
| 9 | Static Position Constant | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Static Velocity Constant | $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{Tz}\, G(z)$ | Units of $\text{sec}^{-1}$ |
| 11 | Static Acceleration Constant | $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2 z}\, G(z)$ | Units of $\text{sec}^{-2}$ |
| 12 | Steady-State Error (Step) | $e_{ss} = 1/(1+K_p)$ | Finite if $K_p \ne \infty$ |
| 13 | Steady-State Error (Ramp) | $e_{ss} = 1/K_v$ | Finite if $K_v \ne 0$ |
| 14 | Steady-State Error (Parabolic) | $e_{ss} = 1/K_a$ | Finite if $K_a \ne 0$ |
| 15 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ | Roots determine stability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Pulse Transfer Function with Zero-Order Hold

A typical digital control system has a **ZOH** followed by a continuous plant $G_p(s)$, with an ideal sampler at the input.

**Step 1: Transfer function of ZOH**

The output of a zero-order hold for an impulse input at $t=0$ is a rectangular pulse of width $T$ and unit height. Its Laplace transform is:

$$G_h(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

**Step 2: Combined continuous transfer function**

The ZOH and plant are cascaded:

$$G(s) = G_h(s)\, G_p(s) = \frac{(1 - e^{-Ts})}{s}\, G_p(s)$$

**Step 3: Take Z-transform via the key identity**

Splitting using linearity and applying the property that $\mathcal{Z}\{F(s)(1-e^{-Ts})\} = (1-z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{F(s)/s\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(z) &= \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\} = \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{(1-e^{-Ts})G_p(s)}{s} \right\} \\
&= \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{G_p(s)}{s} \right\} - \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{e^{-Ts} G_p(s)}{s} \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the shift property of Z-transform**

Since multiplication by $e^{-Ts}$ corresponds to a unit delay in discrete domain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(z) &= \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} - z^{-1} \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \\
&= (1 - z^{-1})\, \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\therefore G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**: This compact result is the **most-tested formula** in KTU exams for digital systems with ZOH.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function (Unity Feedback)

**System Description**: A sampler acts on the error signal $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$. The sampled error $e^*(t)$ drives the plant $G(s)$ to produce $c(t)$.

**Step 1: Write sampled error equation**

$$e^*(t) = r^*(t) - c^*(t)$$

Taking the Z-transform:

$$E(z) = R(z) - C(z)$$

**Step 2: Output through plant**

$$C(z) = G(z)\, E(z)$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$C(z) = G(z)[R(z) - C(z)]$$

**Step 4: Solve for the ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(z) + G(z)\,C(z) &= G(z)\,R(z) \\
C(z)\,[1 + G(z)] &= G(z)\,R(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Difference Equation Solution by Z-Transform

**Given Difference Equation**:

$$c(k+2) + 3c(k+1) + 2c(k) = r(k+1) + 4r(k)$$

with $c(0)=0$, $c(1)=1$, $r(k) = 1$ (unit step), find $c(k)$.

**Step 1: Apply Z-transform to both sides**

Using the **shift theorem**: $\mathcal{Z}\{c(k+n)\} = z^n C(z) - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(i)\, z^{n-1-i}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Z}\{c(k+2)\} &= z^2 C(z) - z^2 c(0) - z\,c(1) = z^2 C(z) - z \\
\mathcal{Z}\{3c(k+1)\} &= 3[z\,C(z) - z\,c(0)] = 3z\,C(z) \\
\mathcal{Z}\{2c(k)\} &= 2 C(z) \\
\mathcal{Z}\{r(k+1)\} &= z\,R(z) - z\,r(0) = z\,R(z) - z \\
\mathcal{Z}\{4r(k)\} &= 4 R(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute and simplify**

For $r(k) = 1$, $R(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
[z^2 C(z) - z] + 3z C(z) + 2 C(z) &= z\,R(z) - z + 4 R(z) \\
C(z)[z^2 + 3z + 2] - z &= (z + 4) R(z) - z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $R(z)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(z)(z^2 + 3z + 2) &= (z+4)\cdot \frac{z}{z-1} \\
C(z) &= \frac{z(z+4)}{(z-1)(z^2 + 3z + 2)} = \frac{z(z+4)}{(z-1)(z+1)(z+2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply partial fraction expansion**

$$\frac{C(z)}{z} = \frac{(z+4)}{(z-1)(z+1)(z+2)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z+1} + \frac{C}{z+2}$$

Computing residues:
- $A = \frac{1+4}{(2)(3)} = \frac{5}{6}$
- $B = \frac{-1+4}{(-2)(1)} = \frac{3}{-2} = -\frac{3}{2}$
- $C = \frac{-2+4}{(-3)(-1)} = \frac{2}{3}$

**Step 5: Apply inverse Z-transform**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(z) &= \frac{5/6}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{3/2}{1+z^{-1}} + \frac{2/3}{1+2z^{-1}} \\
c(kT) &= \frac{5}{6}(1)^k - \frac{3}{2}(-1)^k + \frac{2}{3}(-2)^k \quad ; \quad k = 0,1,2,\dots
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Steady-State Error Derivation

**Step 1: Express error in Z-domain** (for unity feedback):

$$E(z) = \frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Final Value Theorem** (valid only if all poles of $(z-1)E(z)/z$ are strictly inside unit circle):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z}\cdot \frac{R(z)}{1+G(z)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $R(z)$ for each standard input**

**Case A ‚Äî Unit Step Input** $R(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z}\cdot \frac{z/(z-1)}{1+G(z)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{1}{1+G(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$.

**Case B ‚Äî Unit Ramp Input** $R(z) = \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z}\cdot \frac{Tz/(z-1)^2}{1+G(z)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{T}{(z-1)[1+G(z)]}
\end{aligned}
$$

After algebraic manipulation (using L'H√¥pital or series expansion):

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} \quad ; \quad K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{Tz}\, G(z)$$

**Case C ‚Äî Unit Parabolic Input** $R(z) = \frac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3}$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_a} \quad ; \quad K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2 z}\, G(z)$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Difference Equation Solution Methods Comparison

| **Aspect** | **Method of Recursion** | **Method of Z-Transform** |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Direct substitution, iterative | Transform ‚Üí solve algebraically ‚Üí inverse transform |
| Output Format | Numerical sequence $c(0), c(1), c(2), \dots$ | Closed-form expression $c(k)$ |
| Initial Conditions | Used directly at each iteration | Encoded via shift-theorem terms |
| Best For | Short sequences, numerical simulation | Symbolic answer, general solution |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Very High (Part A) | Very High (Part B) |
| Pitfall | Risk of arithmetic errors | Risk in partial fractions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Sampled-Data System with ZOH

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["R_s"] --> nodeS1["Sampler"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeE["E_star"]
    nodeE --> nodeD["D_z (Digital Controller)"]
    nodeD --> nodeZOH["ZOH G_h_of_s"]
    nodeZOH --> nodeGp["G_p_of_s"]
    nodeGp --> nodeC["C_s"]
    nodeC --> nodeS2["Sampler"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeFb["Feedback Path H_s"]
    nodeFb --> nodeSum["Summing Junction"]
    nodeSum --> nodeS1
    nodeR --> nodeSum
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram ‚Äî Closed-Loop Digital System (Unity Feedback)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["R_s Input"] --> nodeSum["Summing Junction"]
    nodeSum -->|"E_star Sampled Error"| nodeG["G_z Pulse Transfer Function"]
    nodeG --> nodeC["C_s Output"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["H_s Continuous Feedback"]
    nodeH --> nodeSh["Sampler"]
    nodeSh --> nodeSum
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow ‚Äî Deriving Closed-Loop $M(z)$ Using Block Diagram Algebra

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA[Step 1 Write Error Equation]
        nodeA1["E_z equals R_z minus C_z"]
    end
    subgraph StageB[Step 2 Express Output]
        nodeB1["C_z equals G_z times E_z"]
    end
    subgraph StageC[Step 3 Substitute and Simplify]
        nodeC1["C_z times 1 plus G_z equals G_z R_z"]
    end
    subgraph StageD[Step 4 Final Closed Loop TF]
        nodeD1["M_z equals G_z over 1 plus G_z"]
    end
    StageA --> StageB
    StageB --> StageC
    StageC --> StageD
```

### 4.4 Steady-State Error Computation ‚Äî Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Identify R_z from input type"] --> nodeCompute["Compute E_z equals R_z divided by 1 plus G_z"]
    nodeCompute --> nodeCheck{"All poles of E_z times z minus 1 inside unit circle"}
    nodeCheck -->|"Yes"| nodeFVT["Apply Final Value Theorem lim z to 1 of z minus 1 over z times E_z"]
    nodeCheck -->|"No"| nodeInvalid["FVT not applicable, system is unstable"]
    nodeFVT --> nodeConstants["Compute K_p K_v K_a from G_z"]
    nodeConstants --> nodeResult["Use 1 over 1 plus K_p etc. to find e_ss"]
```

### 4.5 Closed-Loop System with Non-Unity Digital Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["R_s"] --> nodeSum["Summing Junction"]
    nodeSum --> nodeSampErr["Sampler in Error Path"]
    nodeSampErr -->|"E_star"| nodeG["G_z Forward Path"]
    nodeG --> nodeC["C_s Output"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["H_z Feedback Element"]
    nodeH --> nodeSampFb["Sampler in Feedback Path"]
    nodeSampFb --> nodeSum
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading Guide**: In the diagrams, subscript `_z` denotes Z-domain signals (e.g., $R(z)$ shown as `R_z`), while subscript `_s` denotes Laplace-domain (continuous) signals. The `Sampler` blocks convert continuous signals to discrete impulse trains.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A ‚Äî 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1.** Define Pulse Transfer Function. State two situations in which the pulse transfer functions of two cascaded elements can be multiplied directly.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Model Answer (3 Marks)**
> **Definition (2 marks):** The Pulse Transfer Function (PTF) of an LTI discrete system is the ratio of the Z-transform of the output sequence to the Z-transform of the input sequence, assuming zero initial conditions:
> $$G(z) = \frac{C(z)}{R(z)}$$
> **Multiplication conditions (1 mark):** (i) When a sampler exists between the two blocks (Rule R3), (ii) When both blocks are already discrete in nature (digital controller and digital plant).

**Q2.** Write the steady-state error expressions for a digital control system with unity feedback for unit step, unit ramp, and unit parabolic inputs in terms of static error constants.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Model Answer (3 Marks)**
> - For **unit step**: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$, where $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$.
> - For **unit ramp**: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$, where $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{Tz} G(z)$.
> - For **unit parabolic**: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$, where $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)^2}{T^2 z} G(z)$.

---

### PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A ‚Äî Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the pulse transfer function of a sampled-data system consisting of a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) followed by a plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}$. Take sampling period $T = 1$ sec. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the difference equation $c(k+2) + 5c(k+1) + 6c(k) = r(k)$ with initial conditions $c(0)=0$, $c(1)=1$ and unit step input, determine $c(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ using the method of recursion. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî ZOH transfer function:**
$$G_h(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-s}}{s}$$ (with $T=1$ sec)

**Step 2 ‚Äî Combined transfer function:**
$$G(s) = G_h(s) G_p(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-s}}{s^2 (s+1)}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the ZOH formula:**
$$G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\, \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}\right\}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Partial fractions of $1/[s^2(s+1)]$:**
$$\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s+1}$$

Solving: $B = 1$, $A = -1$, $C = 1$.

$$\therefore \frac{1}{s^2(s+1)} = -\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{1}{s+1}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Take Z-transform (with $T=1$):**
$$\mathcal{Z}\left\{-\frac{1}{s}\right\} = -\frac{z}{z-1}, \quad \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1}{s^2}\right\} = \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}, \quad \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1}{s+1}\right\} = \frac{z}{z-e^{-1}}$$

$$\mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}\right\} = -\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{z}{z-0.368}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Multiply by $(1-z^{-1}) = \frac{z-1}{z}$:**

$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{z-1}{z}\left[-\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{z}{z-0.368}\right] = -1 + \frac{1}{z-1} + \frac{z-1}{z-0.368}}$$

**Final simplified form:**
$$G(z) = \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$$

---

**(b) Solution:**

The difference equation: $c(k+2) + 5c(k+1) + 6c(k) = r(k)$ with $c(0)=0$, $c(1)=1$, and $r(k)=1$.

**Recursion formula:** $c(k+2) = -5c(k+1) - 6c(k) + r(k)$

- **$k = 0$:** $r(0) = 1$. $c(2) = -5(1) - 6(0) + 1 = -4$
- **$k = 1$:** $r(1) = 1$. $c(3) = -5(-4) - 6(1) + 1 = 20 - 6 + 1 = 15$
- **$k = 2$:** $r(2) = 1$. $c(4) = -5(15) - 6(-4) + 1 = -75 + 24 + 1 = -50$
- **$k = 3$:** $r(3) = 1$. $c(5) = -5(-50) - 6(15) + 1 = 250 - 90 + 1 = 161$

$$\boxed{c(0)=0,\; c(1)=1,\; c(2)=-4,\; c(3)=15,\; c(4)=-50,\; c(5)=161,\;\dots}$$

---

#### QUESTION B ‚Äî Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**(a)** For the closed-loop digital control system with $G(z) = \dfrac{0.5z}{z^2 - z + 0.5}$ in the forward path and unity feedback, determine the steady-state error for **(i)** unit step input and **(ii)** unit ramp input. Take $T = 0.5$ sec. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the modified Z-transfer function of $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$ and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute static error constants:**

The open-loop PTF is $G(z) = \dfrac{0.5z}{z^2 - z + 0.5}$.

**Position constant:**
$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.5z}{z^2 - z + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{1 - 1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 1$$

**Velocity constant:**
$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{Tz} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)(0.5z)}{0.5 \cdot z \cdot (z^2 - z + 0.5)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z^2 - z + 0.5}$$

Using L'H√¥pital or direct substitution: as $z \to 1$, $(z^2 - z + 0.5) \to 0.5$ and $(z-1) \to 0$.

$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z^2 - z + 0.5} = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Steady-state errors:**

**(i) For unit step:**
$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p} = \frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5}$$

**(ii) For unit ramp:** Since $K_v = 0$, the system is a **Type-0 system** in ramp input context, hence:
$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \infty}$$

The system cannot track a ramp input without unbounded error.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Apply Modified Z-transform definition:**

For $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$, the impulse response is $g(t) = e^{-at}$.

Sampled at $t = (k+m)T$:
$$g(kT + mT) = e^{-a(kT + mT)} = e^{-amT}\cdot (e^{-aT})^k$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute into modified Z-transform series:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(z, m) &= z^{-1} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} e^{-amT} (e^{-aT})^k z^{-k} \\
&= z^{-1} e^{-amT} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{e^{-aT}}{z}\right)^k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Geometric series summation (valid for $\vert e^{-aT}/z \vert < 1$):**

$$G(z, m) = \frac{z^{-1} e^{-amT}}{1 - e^{-aT} z^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{G(z, m) = \frac{e^{-amT}}{z - e^{-aT}}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Significance:**

- When $m = 0$: $G(z, 0) = \dfrac{1}{z - e^{-aT}}$ ‚Üí standard pulse transfer function (response exactly at sampling instants).
- When $m = 1$: $G(z, 1) = \dfrac{e^{-aT}}{z - e^{-aT}} = \dfrac{1}{e^{aT}}\cdot \dfrac{1}{z - e^{-aT}}$ ‚Üí response one sample late.
- For **intermediate values** $0 < m < 1$, the modified Z-transfer function captures the **inter-sample response**, which is essential for detecting **hidden oscillations** and **ripple** missed by standard PTF analysis.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$ for cascaded systems *without* a sampler between them. This is **wrong** ‚Äî you must take the Z-transform of the *entire* product as a single entity.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In partial fractions, students forget to multiply by $z/z$ before taking the inverse Z-transform. Always write $C(z)/z$ first, expand in partial fractions, then multiply by $z$ before applying $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}\right\} = a^k$.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Applying the **Final Value Theorem** without verifying that all poles of $(z-1)E(z)/z$ lie strictly inside the unit circle. If violated, the FVT gives incorrect results. Always check stability first!
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing $K_v$ and $K_a$ formulas ‚Äî they include $T$ and $T^2$ factors which many students omit, costing 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In modified Z-transform, students use $e^{mTs}$ instead of $e^{-mTs}$ in the time-shifted impulse response. The correct sign is **negative**: $g(kT+mT) = e^{-a(k+m)T}$.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [x] **Pulse Transfer Function** $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$ is the discrete analog of $G(s) = C(s)/R(s)$, valid only for sampled-data systems.
- [x] **ZOH Formula**: $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ ‚Äî appears in nearly every KTU problem involving a digital-to-analog interface.
- [x] **Block Diagram Rules R1‚ÄìR6** must be memorized; the location of samplers fundamentally changes the closed-loop PTF.
- [x] **Closed-Loop PTF**: $M(z) = G(z)/[1 + G(z)H(z)]$ ‚Äî characteristic equation is $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$.
- [x] **Modified Z-TF**: $G(z, m) = z^{-1}\mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\,e^{mTs}\}$ ‚Äî captures inter-sample behaviour for $0 \le m \le 1$.
- [x] **Difference Equation**: Direct recursive method (simple, numerical) vs. Z-transform method (closed-form symbolic) ‚Äî both tested.
- [x] **Shift Theorem**: $\mathcal{Z}\{c(k+n)\} = z^n C(z) - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(i)\,z^{n-1-i}$ ‚Äî must encode initial conditions explicitly.
- [x] **Final Value Theorem**: $e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z}\,E(z)$ ‚Äî check stability before application.
- [x] **Static Error Constants**:
  - $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ (step)
  - $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{Tz} G(z)$ (ramp)
  - $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)^2}{T^2 z} G(z)$ (parabolic)
- [x] **Type Numbering**: Type-0 system ‚Üí finite $e_{ss}$ for step only; Type-1 ‚Üí zero step error, finite ramp error; Type-2 ‚Üí zero step and ramp error, finite parabolic error.
- [x] **Sampling Period Effect**: Smaller $T$ improves accuracy but increases computational load; the choice of $T$ directly affects $K_v$, $K_a$, and stability margins.
- [x] **Initial Conditions in Z-transform**: NEVER assume zero initial conditions unless explicitly stated; the shift theorem embeds them as correction terms.
- [x] **Modified Z-TF Significance**: Critical for dead-time systems (e.g., chemical process control) and inter-sample oscillation analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples on Static Error Coefficients. Bilinear Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Examples on Static Error Coefficients. Bilinear Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Static Error Coefficients ‚Äî Formal Definition

In **discrete-time control systems**, the steady-state error of a unity feedback system driven by a deterministic test input is characterized by **Static Error Coefficients**. These are numerical constants evaluated from the open-loop pulse transfer function $G(z)$ and they completely determine how the closed-loop system "tracks" standard test inputs (Step, Ramp, Parabolic) as $k \to \infty$.

The three canonical error constants are:

$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) \quad \text{(Position Constant)}$$

$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{T}\, G(z) \quad \text{(Velocity Constant)}$$

$$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2}\, G(z) \quad \text{(Acceleration Constant)}$$

where **T** is the sampling period (in seconds), a **physical constant** of the digital system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Type Number (KTU 2024 Convention):** The Type is the number of poles of $G(z)$ located exactly at $z = 1$. Type-0 ‚áí 0 poles at $z=1$; Type-1 ‚áí 1 pole at $z=1$; Type-2 ‚áí 2 poles at $z=1$.

## 1.2 Bilinear Transformation ‚Äî Formal Definition

The **Bilinear Transformation** (also called the *Tustin transformation* or *trapezoidal rule*) is a conformal mapping from the continuous $s$-plane to the discrete $z$-plane, given by the exact algebraic substitution:

$$s = \frac{2}{T}\,\frac{z-1}{z+1} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z = \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$$

This transformation is the **workhorse of digital redesign** ‚Äî it converts any analog transfer function $G(s)$ into an equivalent discrete transfer function $G(z)$ while preserving the **stability boundary**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Car Cruise Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: Static Error Coefficients**
> Imagine a driverless car trying to maintain a target speed. 
> - $K_p$ tells you how much *position* error remains after a sudden speed command (step input).
> - $K_v$ tells you the *lag* in seconds when following a linearly increasing speed profile (ramp input).
> - $K_a$ tells you how badly the car *falls behind* when you demand a constantly accelerating profile (parabolic input).
> 
> A high $K_v$ means the car is responsive to changing speeds ‚Äî like a sports car. A low $K_v$ means a sluggish truck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: Bilinear Transformation**
> Think of it as a "mathematical lens" that warps the entire $s$-plane onto the unit circle in the $z$-plane. The imaginary axis ($j\omega$) of the $s$-plane, which marks the stability boundary in continuous systems, gets mapped exactly onto the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ in the discrete plane ‚Äî the corresponding stability boundary for digital systems. So when you transform a stable analog controller, you get a stable digital controller *automatically*.

## 1.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Static Error Coefficients**: Used in robotics (tracking precision), CNC machines (positioning accuracy), satellite attitude control (pointing error), and servo systems.
- **Bilinear Transformation**: Used in designing digital filters (DSP), digital PID controllers, simulation of analog systems on microcontrollers, and FPGA-based control implementations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework ‚Äî Static Error Coefficients

The steady-state error for standard inputs in a unity discrete feedback system is derived from the Final Value Theorem applied to $E(z)$:

$$E(z) = \frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

| Input $r(k)$ | Z-transform $R(z)$ | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit Step $u(k)$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | Finite if Type $\geq 0$ |
| Unit Ramp $kTu(k)$ | $\dfrac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | Finite if Type $\geq 1$ |
| Unit Parabolic $\dfrac{k^2T^2}{2}u(k)$ | $\dfrac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3}$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ | Finite if Type $\geq 2$ |

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. Compute the open-loop pulse transfer function $G(z)$ from the block diagram.
2. Count the number of poles of $G(z)$ at $z = 1$ ‚Üí this gives the **Type number**.
3. Compute $K_p, K_v, K_a$ using the limit definitions.
4. Substitute into the table above to obtain $e_{ss}$.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework ‚Äî Bilinear Transformation

### Mapping Properties:
| $s$-plane feature | $z$-plane image | Engineering meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Left-half plane: $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ | Inside unit circle: $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Stable analog ‚áí stable digital |
| Imaginary axis: $\text{Re}(s) = 0$ | Unit circle: $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Stability boundary preserved |
| Origin: $s = 0$ | $z = 1$ | DC gain is preserved exactly |
| $s = j\infty$ | $z = -1$ | High-frequency behavior warped |
| $\omega$ range: $[0, \infty)$ | $\theta$ range: $[0, \pi]$ | Frequency warping (pre-warping needed for accurate design) |

### Pre-Warping Equation (for matched frequency design):

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_a T}{2}\right)$$

where $\omega_a$ is the desired analog frequency and $\Omega$ is the pre-warped analog frequency used in the substitution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_p = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} G(z)$ | Position error constant |
| 2 | $K_v = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{T} G(z)$ | Velocity error constant |
| 3 | $K_a = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)^2}{T^2} G(z)$ | Acceleration error constant |
| 4 | $e_{ss}$ (step) $= \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | Steady-state error for unit step |
| 5 | $e_{ss}$ (ramp) $= \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | Steady-state error for unit ramp |
| 6 | $e_{ss}$ (parabolic) $= \dfrac{1}{K_a}$ | Steady-state error for unit parabolic |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ | Bilinear substitution |
| 8 | $z = \dfrac{2+sT}{2-sT}$ | Inverse bilinear substitution |
| 9 | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_a T}{2}\right)$ | Pre-warping formula |
| 10 | $\omega_d = \dfrac{2}{T}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega T}{2}\right)$ | Discrete-to-analog frequency mapping |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Inverted Pendulum / Robot Arms**: $K_v$ directly determines how many seconds the robot lags the desired trajectory ‚Äî critical for high-speed pick-and-place.
- **Audio Equalizers**: Bilinear transformation is the standard method to convert analog biquad filters into digital ones.
- **Hard Disk Drive Servos**: Tracking accuracy in HDD read-head positioning relies on high $K_p$ values from digital controllers designed via Tustin's method.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 ‚Äî Static Error Coefficients (Comprehensive)

**Problem:** For a sampled-data system with open-loop transfer function 
$$G(z) = \frac{0.5\,z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$$
and sampling period $T = 0.2\,\text{s}$, determine:
(a) The system type.
(b) The static error coefficients $K_p, K_v, K_a$.
(c) The steady-state errors for unit step, ramp, and parabolic inputs.

### (a) System Type Identification

The poles of $G(z)$ are found by setting the denominator to zero:
$$(z-1)(z-0.5) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; z = 1,\;\; z = 0.5$$

Number of poles at $z = 1$ is **one**. 

$$\boxed{\text{Type} = 1}$$

### (b) Static Error Coefficients

**Position Constant:**
$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.5\,z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$$

As $z \to 1$, the numerator $\to 0.5$ and the term $(z-1) \to 0$, while $(z-0.5) \to 0.5$. Denominator $\to 0$. Hence:
$$\boxed{K_p = \infty}$$

**Velocity Constant:**
$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{T}\, G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{0.2} \cdot \frac{0.5\,z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$$

Cancel the $(z-1)$ factor:
$$K_v = \frac{1}{0.2} \cdot \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.5\,z}{z-0.5} = \frac{1}{0.2} \cdot \frac{0.5 \times 1}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{1}{0.2} \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5} = \frac{1}{0.2}$$

$$\boxed{K_v = 5\;\text{sec}^{-1}}$$

**Acceleration Constant:**
$$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{T^2}\, G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{0.04} \cdot \frac{0.5\,z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$$

Cancel one $(z-1)$; the remaining $(z-1) \to 0$ in the numerator:
$$K_a = \frac{1}{0.04} \cdot \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) \cdot 0.5\,z}{z-0.5} = \frac{1}{0.04} \cdot \frac{0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1}{0.5} = \frac{0}{0.04}$$

$$\boxed{K_a = 0}$$

### (c) Steady-State Errors

| Input | Formula | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Step | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \infty}$ | $\mathbf{0}$ |
| Unit Ramp | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = \dfrac{1}{5}$ | $\mathbf{0.2\;\text{seconds}}$ |
| Unit Parabolic | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{1}{0}$ | $\boldsymbol{\infty}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Type-1 Behavior:** A Type-1 system tracks a step with **zero** error, tracks a ramp with a **finite constant lag**, and **cannot** track a parabolic input (error grows unboundedly). The result above is consistent with theory. ‚úî

---

## 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 ‚Äî Static Error Coefficients (Type-2 System)

**Problem:** For the system 
$$G(z) = \frac{0.2(z+0.8)}{(z-1)^2(z-0.5)}$$
with $T = 0.5\,\text{s}$, find the system type and compute all static error coefficients.

### Solution

**Type Identification:** The denominator has a factor $(z-1)^2$ ‚Üí two poles at $z=1$.

$$\boxed{\text{Type} = 2}$$

**Position Constant:**
$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.2(z+0.8)}{(z-1)^2(z-0.5)} = \infty \quad \text{(denominator} \to 0\text{)}$$

**Velocity Constant:**
$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{0.5} \cdot \frac{0.2(z+0.8)}{(z-1)^2(z-0.5)}$$

Cancel one $(z-1)$:
$$K_v = \frac{1}{0.5} \cdot \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.2(z+0.8)}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \infty$$

**Acceleration Constant:**
$$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{0.25} \cdot \frac{0.2(z+0.8)}{(z-1)^2(z-0.5)} = \frac{1}{0.25} \cdot \frac{0.2(1+0.8)}{1-0.5}$$

Simplify step-by-step:
$$K_a = 4 \cdot \frac{0.2 \times 1.8}{0.5} = 4 \cdot \frac{0.36}{0.5} = 4 \cdot 0.72$$

$$\boxed{K_a = 2.88\;\text{sec}^{-2}}$$

**Steady-state errors:**
- Step: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+\infty} = 0$
- Ramp: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{\infty} = 0$
- Parabolic: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{2.88} \approx 0.347\;\text{s}^2$

---

## 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 ‚Äî Bilinear Transformation

**Problem:** Convert the analog transfer function 
$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+5)}$$
to an equivalent discrete transfer function $G(z)$ using the bilinear transformation with sampling period $T = 0.2\,\text{s}$.

### Step 1: Compute the Bilinear Substitution

For $T = 0.2$:
$$s = \frac{2}{0.2} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 10 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = \frac{10(z-1)}{z+1}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into $G(s)$

$$G(z) = \frac{10}{\dfrac{10(z-1)}{z+1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{10(z-1)}{z+1} + 5\right)}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Second Bracket

$$\frac{10(z-1)}{z+1} + 5 = \frac{10(z-1) + 5(z+1)}{z+1} = \frac{10z - 10 + 5z + 5}{z+1} = \frac{15z - 5}{z+1}$$

### Step 4: Combine the Denominator

$$G(z) = \frac{10}{\dfrac{10(z-1)}{z+1} \cdot \dfrac{15z-5}{z+1}} = \frac{10(z+1)^2}{10(z-1)(15z-5)}$$

Cancel the common factor 10:
$$G(z) = \frac{(z+1)^2}{(z-1)(15z-5)} = \frac{(z+1)^2}{5(z-1)(3z-1)}$$

### Step 5: Normalize to Standard Form

Multiply numerator and denominator by appropriate factors to express in negative powers of $z$:
$$G(z) = \frac{(z+1)^2}{5(z-1)(3z-1)}$$

We can expand the numerator:
$$(z+1)^2 = z^2 + 2z + 1$$

And the denominator:
$$5(z-1)(3z-1) = 5(3z^2 - z - 3z + 1) = 5(3z^2 - 4z + 1) = 15z^2 - 20z + 5$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{z^2 + 2z + 1}{15z^2 - 20z + 5}}$$

### Step 6: Verify DC Gain Preservation

For $G(s)$ at $s = 0$: $G(0) = \dfrac{10}{0 \cdot 5} \to \infty$ (integrator present).

For $G(z)$ at $z = 1$: $G(1) = \dfrac{1 + 2 + 1}{15 - 20 + 5} = \dfrac{4}{0} \to \infty$ ‚úî

Both have an integrator ‚Äî the bilinear transformation has correctly preserved the pole at the origin (mapped to $z = 1$).

---

## 3.4 EXAMPLE 4 ‚Äî Bilinear Transformation with Pre-Warping

**Problem:** Design a digital low-pass filter equivalent to an analog filter with critical frequency $\omega_a = 10\,\text{rad/s}$, using $T = 0.1\,\text{s}$ and bilinear transformation with **pre-warping**.

### Step 1: Apply Pre-Warping Formula

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_a T}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.1}\tan\!\left(\frac{10 \times 0.1}{2}\right) = 20\tan(0.5\,\text{rad})$$

Since $\tan(0.5) \approx 0.5463$:
$$\Omega = 20 \times 0.5463 = 10.926\,\text{rad/s}$$

### Step 2: Use $\Omega$ in the Analog Filter Design

The pre-warped analog filter is designed using $\Omega$ instead of $\omega_a$. This ensures that the magnitude response of the digital filter at frequency $\omega_a$ matches the analog filter exactly at that frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pre-Warp?** The bilinear transformation causes *frequency warping* ‚Äî the discrete frequency $\omega_d$ relates to the analog frequency $\omega_a$ by a nonlinear (arctangent) law. Pre-warping compensates for this so that one specific critical frequency is matched exactly.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Worked Examples Comparison

| Example | Topic | Key Computation | Final Answer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | Type-1 system, $K_p, K_v, K_a$ | Limit evaluation with $(z-1)$ cancellation | $K_v = 5\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $e_{ss,\text{ramp}} = 0.2\,\text{s}$ |
| 3.2 | Type-2 system, parabolic tracking | Two-stage cancellation | $K_a = 2.88\,\text{s}^{-2}$, $e_{ss,\text{parabolic}} \approx 0.347\,\text{s}^2$ |
| 3.3 | Bilinear transform of 2nd order | Algebraic simplification | $G(z) = \dfrac{z^2 + 2z + 1}{15z^2 - 20z + 5}$ |
| 3.4 | Pre-warping for filter design | $\Omega = \tfrac{2}{T}\tan(\tfrac{\omega_a T}{2})$ | $\Omega = 10.926\,\text{rad/s}$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
**Define static error coefficients for a discrete-time control system. State the steady-state error expressions for unit step, unit ramp, and unit parabolic inputs.**

**Model Answer:**
Static error coefficients are numerical constants that quantify the steady-state ability of a unity feedback discrete system to track standard inputs. They are defined as:
- Position constant: $K_p = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} G(z)$
- Velocity constant: $K_v = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{T} G(z)$
- Acceleration constant: $K_a = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)^2}{T^2} G(z)$

Steady-state errors:
- Unit step: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$
- Unit ramp: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$
- Unit parabolic: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
**State the bilinear transformation relating $s$-plane and $z$-plane. Mention two advantages of this method.**

**Model Answer:**
The bilinear transformation (Tustin's method) is given by:
$$s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1} \quad \text{or} \quad z = \frac{2+sT}{2-sT}$$

**Advantages:**
1. It maps the left half of the $s$-plane into the interior of the unit circle in the $z$-plane, preserving stability.
2. It is a one-to-one mapping (bijective) with no aliasing ‚Äî every point in the $s$-plane corresponds to exactly one point in the $z$-plane.

---

## PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Module 2, Modified]**

(a) Consider the unity feedback discrete-time system with open-loop transfer function 
$$G(z) = \frac{0.368\,z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$$
and sampling period $T = 1\,\text{s}$.
**(7 Marks)**
Determine: (i) the system type, (ii) the static error coefficients $K_p, K_v, K_a$, and (iii) the steady-state error for a unit ramp input.

(b) Using the bilinear transformation with $T = 0.1\,\text{s}$, obtain the discrete-time transfer function $G(z)$ equivalent to the analog transfer function 
$$G(s) = \frac{5}{s+5}$$
**(7 Marks)**

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Static Error Coefficients

**(i) System Type:**
Poles of $G(z)$: $z = 1$ and $z = 0.368$.
One pole at $z = 1$.
$$\boxed{\text{System Type} = 1}$$

**(ii) Error Coefficients:**

$K_p = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} G(z)$: As $z \to 1$, denominator $(z-1)(z-0.368) \to 0$, numerator $\to 0.632$. Hence:
$$K_p = \infty$$

$K_v = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{T} G(z) = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{1} \cdot \dfrac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$

Cancel $(z-1)$:
$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{z - 0.368} = \frac{0.368(1) + 0.264}{1 - 0.368} = \frac{0.632}{0.632} = 1$$

$$\boxed{K_v = 1\,\text{s}^{-1}}$$

$K_a = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)^2}{T^2} G(z) = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)(0.368z + 0.264)}{1 \cdot (z - 0.368)}$

Substituting $z = 1$:
$$K_a = \frac{0 \cdot 0.632}{0.632} = 0$$

**(iii) Steady-State Error for Unit Ramp:**
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{1} = 1\,\text{second}$$

---

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Bilinear Transformation of $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+5}$

**Step 1:** Bilinear substitution for $T = 0.1$:
$$s = \frac{2}{0.1} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 20 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = \frac{20(z-1)}{z+1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:
$$G(z) = \frac{5}{\dfrac{20(z-1)}{z+1} + 5}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify denominator:
$$\frac{20(z-1)}{z+1} + 5 = \frac{20(z-1) + 5(z+1)}{z+1} = \frac{20z - 20 + 5z + 5}{z+1} = \frac{25z - 15}{z+1}$$

**Step 4:** Final expression:
$$G(z) = \frac{5(z+1)}{25z - 15} = \frac{z+1}{5z - 3} = \frac{z+1}{5(z - 0.6)}$$

$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{z+1}{5(z-0.6)}}$$

**Step 5: Verify DC Gain:**
$G(1) = \dfrac{1+1}{5(1-0.6)} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1$ 
$G(0) = \dfrac{5}{0+5} = 1$ ‚úî DC gain is preserved.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Module 2]**

(a) For the discrete-time system 
$$G(z) = \frac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.8)}$$
with $T = 0.5\,\text{s}$, compute all static error coefficients and the steady-state error for unit parabolic input. **(7 Marks)**

(b) Apply bilinear transformation with $T = 0.5\,\text{s}$ to obtain $G(z)$ for 
$$G(s) = \frac{2}{s(s+2)}$$
**(7 Marks)**

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Static Error Coefficients

**System Type:** Poles are $z = 1$ (simple) and $z = 0.8$. 
$$\text{Type} = 1$$

**Coefficients:**
$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.8)} = \infty$$

$$K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{0.5} \cdot \frac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.8)} = \frac{1}{0.5} \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.2} = 2 \cdot 2.5 = 5$$

$$\boxed{K_v = 5\,\text{s}^{-1}}$$

$$K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2}{0.25} \cdot \frac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.8)} = \frac{(z-1) \cdot 0.5}{0.25(z-0.8)}\bigg\vert_{z=1} = 0$$

**Steady-state error for unit parabolic input:**
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

> A Type-1 system **cannot** track a parabolic input ‚Äî error grows unboundedly. ‚úî

---

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Bilinear Transformation of $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s(s+2)}$

**Step 1:** For $T = 0.5$:
$$s = \frac{2}{0.5} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 4 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = \frac{4(z-1)}{z+1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:
$$G(z) = \frac{2}{\dfrac{4(z-1)}{z+1} \cdot \left[\dfrac{4(z-1)}{z+1} + 2\right]}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify inner bracket:
$$\frac{4(z-1)}{z+1} + 2 = \frac{4(z-1) + 2(z+1)}{z+1} = \frac{4z - 4 + 2z + 2}{z+1} = \frac{6z - 2}{z+1}$$

**Step 4:** Combine:
$$G(z) = \frac{2(z+1)^2}{4(z-1)(6z-2)} = \frac{(z+1)^2}{2(z-1)(6z-2)}$$

Simplify $2(6z-2) = 12z - 4 = 4(3z-1)$:
$$G(z) = \frac{(z+1)^2}{4(z-1)(3z-1)}$$

$$\boxed{G(z) = \frac{(z+1)^2}{4(z-1)(3z-1)}}$$

**Verification:** Both $G(s)$ and $G(z)$ have an integrator (pole at origin mapped to $z=1$), as required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the sampling period $T$ in $K_v$ and $K_a$ formulas:** The factors $(z-1)/T$ and $(z-1)^2/T^2$ must include $T$. Omitting $T$ leads to a unit mismatch ‚Äî students lose **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Failing to cancel $(z-1)$ before substituting $z = 1$:** Direct substitution gives $0/0$ or $\infty/0$ indeterminate forms. **Always cancel $(z-1)$ first**, then substitute.
> 3. **Confusing system type with order:** Type = number of poles at $z=1$, Order = total number of poles. A 4th-order Type-2 system is perfectly valid.
> 4. **Bilinear transformation ‚Äî losing the factor $2/T$:** Some students use only $\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ without the $\frac{2}{T}$ prefactor. This produces a wrong gain and may destabilize the system. The factor is **mandatory**.
> 5. **Algebraic sign errors when combining fractions in bilinear substitution:** Take your time to find a common denominator correctly.
> 6. **Not verifying DC gain preservation:** Always cross-check $G(s=0) = G(z=1)$ as a sanity test. Marks are awarded for verification in 14-mark questions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Static Error Coefficients**
> - [ ] Three constants: $K_p$ (no $(z-1)$ factor), $K_v$ (one $(z-1)/T$), $K_a$ (two $(z-1)^2/T^2$ factors).
> - [ ] **Always cancel $(z-1)$ before taking the limit** at $z \to 1$.
> - [ ] Type 0 ‚áí $K_p$ finite; Type 1 ‚áí $K_v$ finite; Type 2 ‚áí $K_a$ finite.
> - [ ] Steady-state error table: Step ‚Üí $\frac{1}{1+K_p}$; Ramp ‚Üí $\frac{1}{K_v}$; Parabolic ‚Üí $\frac{1}{K_a}$.
> - [ ] Ramp error has units of **seconds**; parabolic error has units of **seconds¬≤**.
> - [ ] A Type-N system tracks inputs up to order $N-1$ with zero error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Bilinear Transformation**
> - [ ] Substitution: $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ ‚Äî never forget the $\frac{2}{T}$ prefactor.
> - [ ] Maps LHP ($s$) ‚Üí inside unit circle ($z$). Stability is preserved.
> - [ ] Maps $j\omega$ axis ($s$) ‚Üí unit circle ($z$). Stability boundary preserved.
> - [ ] Pre-warping: $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\left(\frac{\omega_a T}{2}\right)$ for matched-frequency design.
> - [ ] DC gain: $G(s=0) = G(z=1)$ always (verify!).
> - [ ] No aliasing ‚Äî unique mapping for each $s$-plane point.
> - [ ] Frequency warping: discrete frequency $\omega_d = \frac{2}{T}\arctan\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$ is nonlinear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping from S-Plane to Z-Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mapping from S-Plane to Z-Plane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Digital Control Systems**, a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is converted into a discrete-time sequence $x(kT)$ by an ideal sampler. The relationship between the **s-plane** (used to analyze continuous-time/Laplace-domain systems) and the **z-plane** (used to analyze discrete-time/Z-domain systems) is established through the fundamental mapping equation:

$$z = e^{sT}$$

where:
- $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex Laplace variable,
- $T$ is the **sampling period** (in seconds), and
- $z$ is the complex Z-domain variable.

Equivalently, the inverse mapping is given by:

$$s = \frac{1}{T} \ln(z)$$

This exponential transformation is **periodic** in nature because $z$ is a periodic function of $\omega$ with period $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T}$ (the sampling frequency in rad/sec).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the *bridge* between continuous-time stability theory (Routh-Hurwitz, root locus in s-plane) and discrete-time stability theory (Jury's test, root locus in z-plane). Mastery of this mapping is mandatory for stability analysis in Module 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî "The Wrapped Ribbon of Stability"**
>
> Imagine the s-plane as an **infinite flat ribbon** stretching from left (stable) to right (unstable). The imaginary axis $j\omega$ is the central spine of this ribbon. Now, take this ribbon and **wrap it around a cylinder** (the unit circle in the z-plane).
>
> - Every full rotation of the ribbon corresponds to one **sampling frequency** $\omega_s$.
> - The **left half** of the ribbon (stable region, $\sigma < 0$) maps to the **inside** of the cylinder (interior of unit circle, $|z| < 1$).
> - The **right half** (unstable, $\sigma > 0$) maps to the **outside** of the cylinder (exterior of unit circle, $|z| > 1$).
> - The **central spine** (imaginary axis) maps exactly onto the **surface** of the cylinder (unit circle, $|z| = 1$).
>
> Because the ribbon is infinite but the cylinder is finite, the mapping **overlaps itself** ‚Äî this is called **periodicity**, and each segment of the ribbon is called a **primary strip** or **principal strip**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Period** | $T$ | **seconds (s)** | Time between two consecutive samples |
| **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s = 1/T$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Number of samples per second |
| **Sampling Angular Frequency** | $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ | **rad/sec** | Angular sampling frequency |
| **Nyquist / Folding Frequency** | $\omega_s/2 = \pi/T$ | **rad/sec** | Half the sampling frequency |
| **Damping Ratio** | $\zeta$ | dimensionless | Degree of damping |
| **Natural Frequency** | $\omega_n$ | **rad/sec** | Undamped natural frequency |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does $z = e^{sT}$ Hold?

The mapping emerges directly from the definition of the **Z-transform**:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT)\, z^{-k}$$

If $x(t) = e^{st}$, then $x(kT) = e^{s(kT)} = (e^{sT})^k$. Substituting:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (e^{sT})^k z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{e^{sT}}{z}\right)^k = \frac{1}{1 - e^{sT}/z}$$

This converges only when $\vert e^{sT}/z \vert < 1$, i.e., when $z = e^{sT}$. Thus the pole of the Z-transform in the z-plane corresponds to the pole $s$ of the Laplace transform in the s-plane, establishing the bijection $z = e^{sT}$.

## 2.2 Structured Breakdown of Mapping Properties

The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is analyzed by substituting $s = \sigma + j\omega$:

- **Step A ‚Äî Modulus Analysis:** $\vert z \vert = \vert e^{sT} \vert = e^{\sigma T}$.
  - If $\sigma < 0$ (LHP): $\vert z \vert < 1$ ‚Üí **inside** the unit circle.
  - If $\sigma = 0$ (imaginary axis): $\vert z \vert = 1$ ‚Üí **on** the unit circle.
  - If $\sigma > 0$ (RHP): $\vert z \vert > 1$ ‚Üí **outside** the unit circle.

- **Step B ‚Äî Argument Analysis:** $\angle z = \omega T$.
  - The angle in the z-plane is **directly proportional** to the imaginary part of $s$.

- **Step C ‚Äî Periodicity:**
  - If $\omega$ changes by $\frac{2\pi}{T}$, then $\angle z$ changes by $2\pi$, returning to the same $z$.
  - Thus, the infinite strip $-\frac{\pi}{T} < \omega \leq \frac{\pi}{T}$ (called the **primary strip**) uniquely maps onto the entire z-plane.
  - All other strips of width $\frac{2\pi}{T}$ overlap onto the same z-plane region.

- **Step D ‚Äî Folding Effect:**
  - Frequencies above $\omega_s/2$ (Nyquist) "fold back" into the primary strip.
  - This is the source of **aliasing** in discrete systems.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Fundamental s-to-z mapping |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{1}{T}\ln(z)$ | Inverse mapping |
| 3 | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Modulus relationship |
| 4 | $\angle z = \omega T$ | Argument relationship |
| 5 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Sampling frequency (rad/s) |
| 6 | $\omega_N = \dfrac{\pi}{T}$ | Nyquist / folding frequency |
| 7 | $z = e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$ | Mapping of imaginary axis $s = j\omega$ |
| 8 | $z + \dfrac{1}{z} = 2\cos(\omega T)$ | Symmetric real-axis identity |
| 9 | Locus of constant $\sigma$: circle of radius $e^{\sigma T}$ | Vertical line in s-plane |
| 10 | Locus of constant $\omega$: radial line at angle $\omega T$ | Horizontal line in s-plane |
| 11 | Constant-$\zeta$ line: $s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ maps to **logarithmic spiral** | Damping ratio loci |
| 12 | Constant-$\omega_n$ circle: $\vert s + \zeta\omega_n \vert = \omega_n$ maps to another circle | Natural frequency loci |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The s-to-z mapping is the analytical backbone for:
> - **Digital filter design** (converting analog Butterworth, Chebyshev filters into IIR digital filters).
> - **Discrete-time stability analysis** (since BIBO stability in discrete systems requires all poles inside the unit circle, this mapping translates s-plane stability into z-plane stability).
> - **Digital controller design** (converting PID controllers designed in continuous domain to discrete equivalents using ZOH equivalence).
> - **Anti-aliasing filter design** (the folding frequency $\omega_s/2$ sets the upper limit of representable frequencies).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Mapping $z = e^{sT}$

**Goal:** Show that the pole $s$ of a continuous-time exponential $e^{st}$ corresponds exactly to a pole $z = e^{sT}$ in the discrete domain.

**Step 1:** Consider the continuous-time signal $x(t) = e^{st}$. Its Laplace transform is:

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{s - s_0}, \quad \text{with a single pole at } s = s_0$$

**Step 2:** Sample $x(t)$ at instants $t = kT$, $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$:

$$x(kT) = e^{s_0 \cdot kT}$$

**Step 3:** Take the Z-transform of the sampled sequence:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} e^{s_0 kT} \, z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(e^{s_0 T}\right)^{k} z^{-k}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $z^{-k}$ and recognize a geometric series:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{e^{s_0 T}}{z}\right)^{k} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{e^{s_0 T}}{z}} = \frac{z}{z - e^{s_0 T}}$$

This is a valid Z-transform with a **pole at $z = e^{s_0 T}$**. The pole location in the z-plane is therefore the exponential of the corresponding s-plane pole, confirming $z = e^{sT}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mapping of the Imaginary Axis onto the Unit Circle

**Goal:** Show that $s = j\omega$ maps onto $\vert z \vert = 1$.

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ (i.e., $\sigma = 0$, pure imaginary):

$$z = e^{j\omega T}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the modulus:

$$\vert z \vert = \vert e^{j\omega T} \vert = e^{0 \cdot T} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Compute the argument:

$$\angle z = \omega T \pmod{2\pi}$$

**Step 4:** Using Euler's identity:

$$z = e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$$

Hence, as $\omega$ sweeps from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, the point $z$ traces the unit circle **infinitely many times**, with each full revolution corresponding to an increase of $\omega$ by $\frac{2\pi}{T}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Periodicity Implication:** The mapping is **not one-to-one** over the entire $j\omega$-axis. Only within the **primary strip** $-\frac{\pi}{T} \leq \omega < \frac{\pi}{T}$ is the mapping one-to-one. Beyond this strip, points overlap with corresponding points in the primary strip.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mapping of Constant Damping Lines ($\zeta$ = constant)

**Goal:** Find the z-plane locus corresponding to $s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ as $\omega_n$ varies.

**Step 1:** Express $s$ in polar form. A point on the constant-$\zeta$ line has:

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_d, \quad \omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

The angle from the negative real axis is:

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$$

**Step 2:** Write $s$ in polar form:

$$s = \omega_n e^{j(\pi - \theta)} = \omega_n e^{j\beta}, \quad \beta = \pi - \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the mapping:

$$z = e^{sT} = e^{\omega_n T \cos\beta} \cdot e^{j\omega_n T \sin\beta}$$

**Step 4:** Let $r = e^{\omega_n T \cos\beta}$ (modulus) and $\phi = \omega_n T \sin\beta$ (argument). Then:

$$\ln r = \omega_n T \cos\beta \quad \text{and} \quad \phi = \omega_n T \sin\beta$$

**Step 5:** Divide the two equations to eliminate $\omega_n$:

$$\frac{\phi}{\ln r} = \frac{\sin\beta}{\cos\beta} = \tan\beta = \text{constant}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\phi = (\tan\beta) \cdot \ln r \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = e^{\phi / \tan\beta}$$

This is the equation of a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane. The constant-$\zeta$ line in the s-plane maps to a logarithmic spiral in the z-plane.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mapping of Constant Natural Frequency Circles ($\omega_n$ = constant)

**Goal:** Find the z-plane locus corresponding to $\vert s + \zeta\omega_n \vert = \omega_n$ (circles centered at $s = -\zeta\omega_n$).

**Step 1:** Parameterize the circle. Let $\phi$ be the angle from the center. A point on the circle is:

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n + \omega_n e^{j\phi} = \omega_n(e^{j\phi} - \zeta)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into $z = e^{sT}$:

$$z = e^{\omega_n T (e^{j\phi} - \zeta)} = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T} \cdot e^{\omega_n T e^{j\phi}}$$

**Step 3:** This is a closed curve that is generally **not a perfect circle**, but for small $T$ it is approximately circular. When $\zeta = 0$ (undamped), the curve becomes:

$$z = e^{\omega_n T e^{j\phi}}$$

which traces a cardioid-like shape known as the **constant-$\omega_n$ locus**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Region-by-Region Mapping Summary

| s-Plane Region | Condition | z-Plane Image | Stability Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Left Half Plane (LHP) | $\sigma < 0$ | Interior of unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$) | **Stable** discrete system |
| Imaginary axis ($j\omega$-axis) | $\sigma = 0$ | On the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) | **Marginally stable** (oscillatory) |
| Right Half Plane (RHP) | $\sigma > 0$ | Exterior of unit circle ($\vert z \vert > 1$) | **Unstable** discrete system |
| Origin ($s = 0$) | $\sigma = 0,\ \omega = 0$ | $z = 1$ | DC component |
| Negative real axis ($\omega = 0,\ \sigma < 0$) | real, negative | Real segment $(0, 1)$ | Exponential decay |
| Positive real axis ($\omega = 0,\ \sigma > 0$) | real, positive | Real segment $(1, \infty)$ | Exponential growth |
| Infinite negative real ($\sigma \to -\infty$) | ‚Äî | $z \to 0$ | Infinitely fast decay |
| Constant-$\zeta$ line | damping fixed | **Logarithmic spiral** in z-plane | Decaying oscillation envelope |
| Constant-$\omega_n$ circle | natural freq. fixed | Closed curve (approx. circle for small $T$) | Fixed-frequency contour |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Mapping

```mermaid

    E --> F{"Check Real Part sigma"}
    F -- "sigma less than 0" --> G["Inside Unit Circle: Stable"]
    F -- "sigma equals 0" --> H["On Unit Circle: Marginally Stable"]
    F -- "sigma greater than 0" --> I["Outside Unit Circle: Unstable"]
    G --> J["z-Plane Locus at z = e power sT"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Apply Inverse Z Transform if Needed"]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Topology ‚Äî Stable / Unstable Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["s = sigma + j omega"] --> S2{"Is sigma less than 0?"}
    S2 -- "YES (Stable LHP)" --> Z1["Maps to INSIDE of unit circle |z| less than 1"]
    S2 -- "NO" --> S3{"Is sigma greater than 0?"}
    S3 -- "YES (Unstable RHP)" --> Z2["Maps to OUTSIDE of unit circle |z| greater than 1"]
    S3 -- "NO (sigma equals 0)" --> Z3["Maps ONTO the unit circle |z| equals 1"]
    Z1 --> R1["Discrete system is BIBO stable"]
    Z2 --> R2["Discrete system is UNSTABLE"]
    Z3 --> R3["Discrete system is MARGINALLY stable sustained oscillation"]
```
