## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
*State and explain the magnitude and angle conditions for the root locus of a digital control system with characteristic equation $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a point $z = z_0$ to lie on the root locus of a discrete-time system, it must satisfy the characteristic equation $1 + G(z_0)H(z_0) = 0$, which splits into two conditions:

**(1) Angle Condition:**
$$\angle G(z_0)H(z_0) = (2q + 1) \cdot 180¬∞, \quad q = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

**(2) Magnitude Condition:**
$$\vert G(z_0)H(z_0) \vert = 1$$

The angle condition determines the *shape* of the locus (independent of $K$), while the magnitude condition is used to compute the *value of gain* $K$ at any point on the locus using $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(z_0)H(z_0) / K_{\text{stripped}} \vert}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
*What is the bilinear transformation? Why is it used in the frequency-domain analysis of digital control systems?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **bilinear (w-) transformation** is the mapping:

$$w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}, \quad z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w}$$

It is used because the **unit circle** in the z-plane (the stability boundary for discrete systems) is mapped onto the **imaginary axis** of the w-plane, which is the stability boundary for continuous-time systems. This allows direct application of classical tools like the **Bode plot, Nyquist plot, and Routh-Hurwitz criterion** to digital control systems without modification.

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 ‚Äî Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

For a unity-feedback digital control system with open-loop pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{K(z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.2)(z - 0.8)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the root locus using the standard rules and identify the range of $K$ for stability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Convert $G(z)$ to the w-plane, sketch the Bode plot, and determine the gain margin and phase margin. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION A:**

**Part (a) ‚Äî Root Locus Construction (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify poles and zeros:**
Poles: $p_1 = 0.2, \; p_2 = 0.8$. Zero: $z_1 = -0.5$. So $n = 2, m = 1$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Real axis segments:**
Count real poles/zeros to the right of test points:
- Between $-0.5$ and $0.2$: one zero to right (odd) ‚Üí **on locus**.
- Between $0.2$ and $0.8$: two poles to right (even) ‚Üí **off locus**.
- Right of $0.8$: three elements to right (odd) ‚Üí **on locus**.
- Left of $-0.5$: zero elements (even) ‚Üí **off locus**.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Asymptote:**
$n - m = 2 - 1 = 1$ asymptote.

$$\sigma_a = \frac{(0.2 + 0.8) - (-0.5)}{1} = \frac{1.0 + 0.5}{1} = 1.5$$

$$\theta_0 = \frac{(2 \cdot 0 + 1)\pi}{1} = 180¬∞$$

The asymptote is a horizontal line at $180¬∞$ through $\sigma_a = 1.5$, meaning the lone branch going to infinity moves leftward from the centroid along the negative real direction.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Breakaway/break-in points:**
Characteristic equation: $(z-0.2)(z-0.8) + K(z+0.5) = 0$

$$K = -\frac{(z-0.2)(z-0.8)}{z+0.5} = -\frac{z^2 - z + 0.16}{z + 0.5}$$

Setting $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$:

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = -\frac{(2z - 1)(z + 0.5) - (z^2 - z + 0.16)(1)}{(z + 0.5)^2} = 0$$

Numerator:

$$(2z - 1)(z + 0.5) - (z^2 - z + 0.16) = 2z^2 + z - 0.5 - z^2 + z - 0.16$$

$$= z^2 + 2z - 0.66 = 0$$

$$z = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 2.64}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{6.64}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm 2.577}{2}$$

$$z_1 = 0.2885, \quad z_2 = -2.2885$$

Check validity (must lie on real-axis locus segments): $z_1 = 0.2885$ lies between $0.2$ and $0.8$ (off locus) ‚Üí invalid. $z_2 = -2.2885$ lies left of $-0.5$ (off locus) ‚Üí invalid. **No breakaway point exists on the real axis.**

**Step 5 ‚Äî Intersection with unit circle:**
Use the angle condition at $z = e^{j\omega T}$. Write:

$$\frac{K(e^{j\omega T} + 0.5)}{(e^{j\omega T} - 0.2)(e^{j\omega T} - 0.8)} = -1$$

For $z = -1$ ($\omega T = \pi$):

$$K_{\max} = \frac{\vert (-1) - 0.2 \vert \cdot \vert (-1) - 0.8 \vert}{\vert -1 + 0.5 \vert} = \frac{1.2 \cdot 1.8}{0.5} = \frac{2.16}{0.5} = 4.32$$

**Conclusion (Part a):** The locus begins at poles $0.2$ and $0.8$, moves along the real axis segments between $-0.5$ to $0.2$ and beyond $0.8$, with one branch terminating at $z = -0.5$ and the other going to infinity along $180¬∞$. **Range of stability: $0 < K < 4.32$.**

---

**Part (b) ‚Äî w-plane Bode Plot (7 Marks):**

Apply $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:

$$G(w) = \frac{K\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} + 0.5\right)}{\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.2\right)\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.8\right)}$$

Simplify step by step:

$$\frac{1+w}{1-w} + 0.5 = \frac{1+w + 0.5(1-w)}{1-w} = \frac{1.5 + 0.5w}{1-w}$$

$$\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.2 = \frac{1+w - 0.2(1-w)}{1-w} = \frac{0.8 + 1.2w}{1-w}$$

$$\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.8 = \frac{1+w - 0.8(1-w)}{1-w} = \frac{0.2 + 1.8w}{1-w}$$

Therefore:

$$G(w) = \frac{K \cdot \frac{1.5 + 0.5w}{1-w}}{\frac{(0.8 + 1.2w)(0.2 + 1.8w)}{(1-w)^2}} = \frac{K(1.5 + 0.5w)(1-w)}{(0.8 + 1.2w)(0.2 + 1.8w)}$$

Let $K = 1$ for analysis:

$$G(w) = \frac{(1.5 + 0.5w)(1-w)}{(0.8 + 1.2w)(0.2 + 1.8w)}$$

Substitute $w = j\omega_w$:

$$G(j\omega_w) = \frac{(1.5 + j0.5\omega_w)(1 - j\omega_w)}{(0.8 + j1.2\omega_w)(0.2 + j1.8\omega_w)}$$

Magnitude:

$$\vert G(j\omega_w) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1.5^2 + 0.25\omega_w^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \omega_w^2}}{\sqrt{0.8^2 + 1.44\omega_w^2} \cdot \sqrt{0.2^2 + 3.24\omega_w^2}}$$

Phase:

$$\angle G = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5\omega_w}{1.5}\right) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_w) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.2\omega_w}{0.8}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.8\omega_w}{0.2}\right)$$

**Phase crossover frequency** ($\omega_{pc}$): where $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180¬∞$.

By numerical iteration: $\omega_{pc} \approx 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$ (approx), giving $\vert G(j0.5) \vert \approx 0.42$.

$$GM = \frac{1}{0.42} = 2.38 \;\Rightarrow\; GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(2.38) \approx 7.54 \text{ dB}$$

**Gain crossover frequency** ($\omega_{gc}$): where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$.

By iteration: $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.18 \text{ rad/s}$. At this frequency:

$$\angle G(j0.18) \approx -45¬∞ - 10.2¬∞ - 15.1¬∞ - 58.4¬∞ \approx -128.7¬∞$$

$$PM = 180¬∞ + (-128.7¬∞) = 51.3¬∞$$

**Conclusion (Part b):** $GM \approx 7.5 \text{ dB}, \; PM \approx 51¬∞$. The system is **stable with good relative stability margins**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Alternate]**

A digital control system has the open-loop pulse transfer function:

$$G(z)H(z) = \frac{K}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)}$$

**(a)** Construct the complete root locus and find the critical gain $K_{\max}$ for stability using the intersection-with-unit-circle method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the bilinear transform, convert the system to w-plane, draw the Nyquist plot, and apply the Nyquist stability criterion to determine stability for $K = 2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION B:**

**Part (a) ‚Äî Root Locus (7 Marks):**

Poles: $p_1 = 0.5, \; p_2 = 1$. No finite zeros. $n = 2, m = 0$.

**Real-axis segments:** Right of $z = 1$ (two poles to right ‚Üí odd ‚Üí on locus). Between $0.5$ and $1$ (one pole to right ‚Üí odd ‚Üí on locus). Left of $0.5$ ‚Üí off locus.

**Asymptotes:** $n - m = 2$.

$$\sigma_a = \frac{0.5 + 1}{2} = 0.75$$

$$\theta_0 = 90¬∞, \quad \theta_1 = 270¬∞ \;(\text{or } -90¬∞)$$

**Breakaway point:** From Section 3.3, $z = 0.75$ (already derived).

**Intersection with unit circle:** $z = -1$:

$$K_{\max} = \vert (-1) - 0.5 \vert \cdot \vert (-1) - 1 \vert = 1.5 \times 2 = 3.0$$

**Conclusion:** **Range of stability: $0 < K < 3$.**

---

**Part (b) ‚Äî Nyquist Plot in w-plane (7 Marks):**

Apply $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:

$$G(w)H(w) = \frac{K}{\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.5\right)\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 1\right)}$$

Simplify:

$$\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 0.5 = \frac{1.5 + 0.5w}{1-w}$$

$$\frac{1+w}{1-w} - 1 = \frac{2w}{1-w}$$

Therefore:

$$G(w)H(w) = \frac{K(1-w)^2}{(1.5 + 0.5w)(2w)} = \frac{K(1-w)^2}{w(3 + w)}$$

For $K = 2$ and $w = j\omega_w$:

$$G(j\omega_w)H(j\omega_w) = \frac{2(1 - j\omega_w)^2}{j\omega_w(3 + j\omega_w)}$$

Expand numerator:

$$(1 - j\omega_w)^2 = 1 - 2j\omega_w - \omega_w^2 = (1 - \omega_w^2) - j2\omega_w$$

Denominator:

$$j\omega_w(3 + j\omega_w) = 3j\omega_w + j^2\omega_w^2 = -\omega_w^2 + j3\omega_w$$

**For Nyquist plot, sweep $\omega_w$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:**

At $\omega_w \to 0^+$:
$$G(j0^+) \approx \frac{2 \cdot 1}{j\omega_w \cdot 3} \to \infty \angle -90¬∞$$

At $\omega_w \to \infty$:
$$G(j\infty) \approx \frac{2(-j\omega_w)^2}{(j\omega_w)(j\omega_w)} = \frac{2 \cdot (-1)}{-1} = 2$$

At $\omega_w = 1$ (numerical):
$$G(j1) = \frac{2 \cdot (0 - j2)}{-1 + j3} = \frac{-j4}{-1 + j3} = \frac{-j4 \cdot (-1 - j3)}{1 + 9} = \frac{j4 - 12}{10} = -1.2 + j0.4$$

The Nyquist contour consists of:
- Imaginary axis ($w = j\omega_w$, $\omega_w: -\infty \to +\infty$) ‚Äî gives symmetric real-axis plot for $G(j\omega_w)$ when system has no RHP poles.
- Infinite semicircle (closing path in RHP).

**Poles of $G(w)H(w)$:** $w = 0$ and $w = -3$. No RHP poles, so $P = 0$.

**Encirclements $N$:** By sketching the Nyquist plot, the curve does not encircle $(-1, 0)$.

$$Z = N + P = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Number of closed-loop poles in unstable region of w-plane = 0.**

By the mapping $w \to z$, **the closed-loop system has no poles outside the unit circle** for $K = 2$, hence **stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often forget that $P$ must count only the **open-loop poles in the RHP of the w-plane** (equivalently, outside the unit circle in z-plane). Failing to identify $w = 0$ as a pole on the imaginary axis leads to an incorrect indent in the Nyquist contour, costing 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Root Locus & Frequency Domain Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong stability boundary**: Mixing up the imaginary axis (s/w-plane) with the unit circle (z-plane) ‚Äî this costs full marks in stability questions.
> 2. **Centroid formula sign error**: $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n-m}$. Subtracting in the wrong order gives the asymptotes pointing in the *opposite* direction.
> 3. **Asymptote angles**: Using $(2q+1) \cdot 180¬∞ / (n-m)$ *forgetting the half-integer multiples* ‚Äî must use $(2q+1)\pi$ (radians) or $(2q+1) \cdot 180¬∞ / (n-m)$ (degrees) for $q = 0, 1, \ldots$
> 4. **Breakaway point validity**: Students compute $dK/dz = 0$ but forget to **verify** the root lies on a valid real-axis segment of the locus. Invalid breakaway points are simply ignored.
> 5. **Bilinear transform mistakes**: A single algebraic slip in the w-transform invalidates the entire Bode plot. Always show the intermediate numerator/denominator simplification step.
> 6. **Nyquist $P$ miscount**: Forgetting to count open-loop poles on the imaginary axis (which require *indented* contours in w-plane analysis).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **characteristic equation** for a digital root locus is $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$.
- Root locus in the z-plane has **$n$ branches** (one per open-loop pole) and is **symmetric about the real axis**.
- The **angle condition** determines the shape; the **magnitude condition** gives the gain.
- **Real axis segments** are those having an **odd number of real poles + zeros to their right**.
- **Centroid** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n-m}$ is a real number; asymptote angles $\theta_q = \dfrac{(2q+1)\pi}{n-m}$.
- **Breakaway points** satisfy $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$ on the characteristic equation, then verified against valid real-axis segments.
- **Angle of departure** $\theta_d = 180¬∞ + \sum\angle(p_k - z_j) - \sum_{i \neq k}\angle(p_k - p_i)$.
- **Stability boundary** in z-plane is the **unit circle**; poles *inside* ‚áí stable, *on* ‚áí marginally stable, *outside* ‚áí unstable.
- **Bilinear transform** $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ maps unit circle $\to$ imaginary axis of w-plane, enabling classical Bode/Nyquist tools.
- After w-transform, substitute $w = j\omega_w$ for **frequency response**, where $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T / 2)$.
- **Gain Margin** $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}|G(j\omega_{pc})|$; **Phase Margin** $PM = 180¬∞ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$.
- **Nyquist criterion**: $Z = N + P$. In the w-plane, $P$ counts open-loop poles in the right half-plane.
- For a root locus to cross the unit circle, use the angle and magnitude conditions at $z = e^{j\omega T}$ to find the **critical gain** $K_{\max}$.
- Always cross-verify digital root locus results using **Routh-Hurwitz in the w-plane** for confidence.
- Remember: the w-plane is **not equivalent to the s-plane** in terms of frequency ‚Äî the relation $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ introduces **frequency warping**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bode Plots - Gain Margin and Phase Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bode Plots - Gain Margin and Phase Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bode Plot ‚Äî Formal Definition

A **Bode Plot** is a graphical frequency-response representation of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system, consisting of **two semi-logarithmic plots** drawn against the log of input frequency (log œâ on the x-axis):

1. **Magnitude Plot** ‚Äî $|G(j\omega)|_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega) \vert$ versus $\log \omega$.
2. **Phase Plot** ‚Äî $\angle G(j\omega)$ in degrees versus $\log \omega$.

The plot is built using **asymptotic straight-line approximations** (corner-frequency / Bode method), making it the most engineer-friendly tool for **stability analysis in the frequency domain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Bode plots* form the backbone of **classical control design**. They convert the algebraic Routh-Hurwitz test into a *visual relative-stability chart* (GM, PM, $\omega_{gc}$, $\omega_{pc}$).

## 1.2 Gain Margin (GM) ‚Äî Definition

> **Gain Margin (GM)** is the factor by which the open-loop gain may be raised (multiplicatively) before the closed-loop system reaches the **threshold of instability** (i.e., before a pole crosses the $j\omega$-axis).

It is measured at the **Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$)** ‚Äî the frequency at which the open-loop phase first equals $\mathbf{-180^{\circ}}$.

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \quad \text{(linear, dimensionless)} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

## 1.3 Phase Margin (PM) ‚Äî Definition

> **Phase Margin (PM)** is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that the open-loop system can tolerate at the **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)** before the closed-loop system becomes unstable.

It is measured at the **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)** ‚Äî the frequency at which the magnitude first equals **0 dB** (unity, $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 1$).

$$PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$$

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî The "Steering Wheel" Picture

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Gain Margin** | Spare **brake-pedal travel** before the brakes lock up. | How much *amplifier* gain you can add before the loop self-oscillates. |
| **Phase Margin** | Spare **steering-wheel rotation** before the car veers off the lane. | How much *extra phase delay* the plant may introduce before the loop becomes unstable. |
| **Gain Crossover $\omega_{gc}$** | Cruise-control speed at which the steering correction first responds. | The natural bandwidth of the closed-loop system. |

A car with **tiny steering margin** is twitchy and crashes; a control system with **tiny PM** is oscillatory and unstable. Likewise, **zero brake margin** means slamming the brakes = lock-up = crash = oscillation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**GM at $\omega_{pc}$, PM at $\omega_{gc}$**" ‚Äî Gain is measured where Phase hits 180¬∞; Phase is measured where Gain hits 0 dB.

## 1.5 Stability Decision Rule (Bode Criterion)

| Condition | Stability Verdict |
|-----------|-------------------|
| $GM > 0$ dB **and** $PM > 0^{\circ}$ | **Stable** (Bode-Nyquist criterion satisfied) |
| $GM = 0$ dB **or** $PM = 0^{\circ}$ | **Marginally stable** (sustained oscillation) |
| $GM < 0$ dB **or** $PM < 0^{\circ}$ | **Unstable** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Logic of a Bode Plot ‚Äî Stepwise

The Bode method converts a rational $G(s)$ into straight-line asymptotes by treating each factor separately. The rules:

1. **Convert to Bode (time-constant) form:**
$$G(j\omega) = K \frac{\prod (1+j\omega\tau_z)^{m}}{\prod (1+j\omega\tau_p)^{n}} \cdot \frac{1}{(j\omega)^{N}}$$
where $N$ is the **type number** (number of pure integrations, i.e., poles at the origin).

2. **Identify corner (break) frequencies:**
   - For each factor $(1+j\omega\tau)$ ‚Üí corner at $\omega_c = 1/\tau$.
   - For each quadratic $\left[1 + 2\zeta(j\omega)/\omega_n + (j\omega)^2/\omega_n^2\right]$ ‚Üí corner at $\omega_n$.

3. **Initial slope of magnitude plot:**
$$\text{Initial slope} = -20N \text{ dB/decade}$$
where $N$ is the number of integrators (poles at origin).

4. **Slope changes at each corner:**
   - Each simple pole ‚Üí slope becomes **more negative by 20 dB/dec**.
   - Each simple zero ‚Üí slope becomes **more positive by 20 dB/dec**.
   - Each real pole/zero contributes an asymptotic $\pm 20\log\sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^2}$ correction (only the *change* in slope matters).

5. **Phase plot rules:**
   - Each pure integrator $1/(j\omega)$ ‚Üí constant $-90^{\circ}$.
   - Each factor $(1+j\omega\tau)$ ‚Üí phase goes from $0^{\circ}$ to $-90^{\circ}$ (pole) or $+90^{\circ}$ (zero), with a **0¬∞ ‚Üí ¬±90¬∞ transition centred at $\omega_c$** over a $\pm$ decade window (one decade below, one decade above).
   - Quadratic underdamped: $\phi = -\tan^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)}{1-(\omega/\omega_n)^2}\right]$.

6. **Cross-reference points:**
   - $\omega_{gc}$: where magnitude plot **crosses 0 dB** (magnitude = 1).
   - $\omega_{pc}$: where phase plot **crosses $-180^{\circ}$**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner may reward marks for in this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Magnitude (dB) | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | dB |
| 2 | Gain Crossover | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert_{dB} = 0$ dB | rad/s |
| 3 | Phase Crossover | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^{\circ}$ | rad/s |
| 4 | Gain Margin (linear) | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Gain Margin (dB) | $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | dB |
| 6 | Phase Margin | $PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | degrees |
| 7 | Slope‚Äìfrequency relation | $\Delta \text{slope} = \dfrac{\Delta M_{dB}}{\Delta \log_{10}\omega}$ | dB/decade |
| 8 | Asymptotic correction at $\omega_c$ | $\pm 3$ dB for simple pole/zero | at $\omega = \omega_c$ |
| 9 | Phase of a pole $(1+j\omega\tau)^{-1}$ | $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$ | degrees |
| 10 | Phase of a pure integrator | $\phi = -90^{\circ}$ | constant |
| 11 | Stability (Bode) | $GM > 0$ dB **and** $PM > 0^{\circ}$ | necessary & sufficient for minimum-phase open-loop |
| 12 | Closed-loop bandwidth approximation | $\omega_B \approx \omega_{gc}$ | for $PM \in [30^{\circ}, 70^{\circ}]$ |
| 13 | Damping ratio from PM | $\zeta \approx \dfrac{PM(\text{deg})}{100}$ | approximate (valid $PM \le 70^{\circ}$) |
| 14 | Overshoot from PM | $M_p \approx 0.16 + 0.4(M_r - 1)$ where $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(PM)}$ | approximate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule on sign convention:** A *positive* $GM_{dB}$ (e.g., +10 dB) means stable. If the magnitude plot at $\omega_{pc}$ is **above** 0 dB, then $GM_{dB}$ is **negative** and the system is **unstable**.

## 2.3 Physical & Engineering Utility

- **Lead compensators** are designed primarily to **increase PM** (raise $\angle G$ near $\omega_{gc}$).
- **Lag compensators** reduce steady-state error while **preserving PM** (they reshape low-frequency gain only).
- **PID tuning** in practice: increase $K_p$ (loop gain) until desired $PM \approx 45^{\circ} \text{‚Äì} 60^{\circ}$ is reached on the Bode plot.
- **Audio amplifiers, op-amp loops, switching power supplies** all use Bode-plot analysis to guarantee **conditional stability** (no oscillation under any operating point).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Phase-Margin Formula

We start from the closed-loop characteristic equation of a unity feedback system:

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad G(s) = -1$$

On the Nyquist / Bode plane, the **critical instability point is** $\mathbf{-1 + j0}$ (magnitude 1, phase $-180^{\circ}$). The closed-loop system is stable iff the open-loop $G(j\omega)$ locus does **not encircle** this point.

The **distance from the $G(j\omega)$ curve to the critical point $-1$** has two orthogonal components:

- *Radial distance* = how far the gain is from 1 ‚Üí quantified by **GM**.
- *Angular distance* = how far the phase is from $-180^{\circ}$ ‚Üí quantified by **PM**.

### Step-by-step Derivation of PM

At $\omega = \omega_{gc}$, the magnitude is exactly unity:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \quad \text{(definition of } \omega_{gc}\text{)}$$

The phase angle at this point is $\phi_{gc} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$, which is *negative* for stable systems. The angular distance to the $-180^{\circ}$ line is:

$$\boxed{\,PM = 180^{\circ} + \phi_{gc} = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})\,}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \phi_{gc} &= -90^{\circ} \;\Rightarrow\; PM = 90^{\circ} \quad \text{(very stable, sluggish)} \\
\text{If } \phi_{gc} &= -135^{\circ} \;\Rightarrow\; PM = 45^{\circ} \quad \text{(well-damped, industry standard)} \\
\text{If } \phi_{gc} &= -180^{\circ} \;\Rightarrow\; PM = 0^{\circ} \quad \text{(marginally stable, oscillation)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step-by-step Derivation of GM

At $\omega = \omega_{pc}$, the phase is exactly $-180^{\circ}$:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^{\circ}$$

The *gain factor* by which the loop can be multiplied before the $G(j\omega)$ curve passes through the critical point $-1$ is the reciprocal of its current magnitude at this frequency:

$$\boxed{\,GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \;\equiv\; GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert\,}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert < 1 \;(0 \text{ dB}) &\;\Rightarrow\; GM_{dB} > 0 \quad \text{(stable)} \\
\text{If } \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1 \;(0 \text{ dB}) &\;\Rightarrow\; GM_{dB} = 0 \quad \text{(marginally stable)} \\
\text{If } \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert > 1 \;(0 \text{ dB}) &\;\Rightarrow\; GM_{dB} < 0 \quad \text{(unstable)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example ‚Äî KTU Pattern (14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A unity-feedback control system has the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s\,(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$$
Sketch the Bode plot and determine **(i) Gain Margin**, **(ii) Phase Margin**. Comment on the **stability** of the closed-loop system.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify Bode Form & Factors

$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{2}{j\omega\,(1+j0.5\omega)(1+j0.2\omega)}$$

| Factor | Corner Frequency $\omega_c$ | Slope Contribution | Phase Contribution |
|--------|-----------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| $1/(j\omega)$ | ‚Äî (integrator) | $-20$ dB/dec from start | $-90^{\circ}$ |
| $1/(1+j0.5\omega)$ | $\omega_1 = 1/0.5 = 2$ rad/s | $-20$ dB/dec after $\omega_1$ | $0^{\circ} \to -90^{\circ}$ about $\omega_1$ |
| $1/(1+j0.2\omega)$ | $\omega_2 = 1/0.2 = 5$ rad/s | $-20$ dB/dec after $\omega_2$ | $0^{\circ} \to -90^{\circ}$ about $\omega_2$ |

**Initial slope:** $-20$ dB/decade.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Magnitude Asymptotes (Table of Values)

$$M_{dB}(\omega) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{1+0.25\omega^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04\omega^{2}}}\right)$$

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | Asymptote calculation | Asymptote $M_{dB}$ (dB) | Slope |
|------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 0.1 | $20\log(2/0.1) = 20\log 20$ | $\mathbf{+26.0}$ | $-20$ dB/dec |
| 0.5 | $20\log(2/0.5) = 20\log 4$ | $\mathbf{+12.0}$ | $-20$ dB/dec |
| 1.0 | $20\log(2/1)$ | $\mathbf{+6.0}$ | $-20$ dB/dec |
| 2.0 | Just after $\omega_1$; use $6 - 20\log(2/1) + 3$ dB correction? ‚Äî asymptote: $6 - 20\log(2/1) = 6$ | $\mathbf{+6.0}$ (start of $-40$ dB/dec) | $-40$ dB/dec |
| 3.0 | $6 - 40\log(3/2) = 6 - 7.04$ | $\mathbf{-1.04}$ | $-40$ dB/dec |
| 5.0 | $6 - 40\log(5/2) = 6 - 15.96$ | $\mathbf{-9.96}$ | start of $-60$ dB/dec |
| 10 | $-9.96 - 60\log(10/5) = -9.96 - 18.06$ | $\mathbf{-28.0}$ | $-60$ dB/dec |

### Step 3 ‚Äî Find Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$ (0 dB Crossing)

In the region $\omega \in [2,\,5]$ (slope $-40$ dB/dec), the asymptote equation is:

$$M_{dB}(\omega) = 6 - 40\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{1}\right) \quad \text{[starting from } M_{dB}(1)=6\text{ dB]}$$

Wait ‚Äî the slope changed to $-40$ dB/dec at $\omega_1 = 2$. So reference at $\omega = 2$ is $6$ dB. Solving $M_{dB}(\omega_{gc}) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
6 - 40\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{gc}}{2}\right) &= 0 \\
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{gc}}{2}\right) &= \dfrac{6}{40} = 0.15 \\
\dfrac{\omega_{gc}}{2} &= 10^{0.15} \approx 1.413 \\
\boxed{\omega_{gc} \approx 2.83 \text{ rad/s}}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $2 < 2.83 < 5$, the assumption is consistent. ‚úì

### Step 4 ‚Äî Compute Phase at $\omega_{gc}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) &= -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(0.5\,\omega_{gc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\,\omega_{gc}) \\
&= -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \times 2.83) - \tan^{-1}(0.2 \times 2.83) \\
&= -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(1.414) - \tan^{-1}(0.566) \\
&= -90^{\circ} - 54.74^{\circ} - 29.50^{\circ} \\
&= -174.24^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Phase Margin

$$\begin{aligned}
PM &= 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) \\
&= 180^{\circ} - 174.24^{\circ} \\
\boxed{PM \approx 5.76^{\circ}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A PM of only **5.76¬∞** is **dangerously small** ‚Äî the closed-loop response will have **overshoot > 70%** and severe ringing. Industry design target: $PM \in [40^{\circ}, 60^{\circ}]$.

### Step 6 ‚Äî Find Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$

Solve $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^{\circ}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
-90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(0.5\,\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\,\omega_{pc}) &= -180^{\circ} \\
\tan^{-1}(0.5\,\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\,\omega_{pc}) &= 90^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1}(a) + \tan^{-1}(b) = 90^{\circ} \iff ab = 1$ (when $a, b > 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.5\,\omega_{pc})(0.2\,\omega_{pc}) &= 1 \\
0.1\,\omega_{pc}^{2} &= 1 \\
\omega_{pc}^{2} &= 10 \\
\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \text{ rad/s}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Compute Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert &= \dfrac{2}{\omega_{pc} \cdot \sqrt{1+(0.5\,\omega_{pc})^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1+(0.2\,\omega_{pc})^{2}}} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.25 \times 10} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04 \times 10}} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{3.5} \cdot \sqrt{1.4}} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{3.1623 \times 1.8708 \times 1.1832} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{6.997} \\
&\approx 0.2859
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 ‚Äî Compute Gain Margin

$$\begin{aligned}
GM_{dB} &= -20\log_{10}(0.2859) \\
&= -20 \times (-0.5437) \\
\boxed{GM_{dB} \approx +10.87 \text{ dB}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9 ‚Äî Stability Verdict

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|--------|-------|---------|
| $GM_{dB}$ | $+10.87$ dB | $> 0$ dB ‚Üí **Stable sign** ‚úì |
| $PM$ | $+5.76^{\circ}$ | $> 0^{\circ}$ ‚Üí **Stable sign** ‚úì |
| **Joint conclusion** | Both positive | **Closed-loop is stable**, but with very *poor relative stability* |

**Recommendation:** Increase gain or add a **lead compensator** to raise $PM$ to at least $45^{\circ}$.

## 3.3 Derivation ‚Äî Range of $K$ for Stability

For $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$, $GM = 10.87$ dB at $K = 2$. Since $GM$ scales **inversely with $K$** (raising $K$ by $X$ dB lowers $GM$ by $X$ dB), the limiting $K$ is:

$$20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{\max}}{K_{\text{current}}}\right) = GM_{dB} = 10.87$$

$$K_{\max} = K_{\text{current}} \times 10^{10.87/20} = 2 \times 10^{0.5435} \approx 2 \times 3.495 \approx 6.99$$

$$\boxed{\;0 < K < 6.99\; \quad \text{for closed-loop stability}\;}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bode Plot Construction Flow (Procedural)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start with G of s] --> convA[Convert to Bode Time Constant Form]
    convA --> idCorners[Identify Corner Frequencies omegaC]
    idCorners --> initSlope[Set Initial Slope equals minus 20 N dB per decade]
    initSlope --> plotMag[Plot Magnitude Asymptote on semi log axes]
    plotMag --> applyCorr[Apply plus or minus 3 dB correction at each corner]
    applyCorr --> plotPhase[Plot Phase Curve using tan inverse sum of factors]
    plotPhase --> findWGC[Mark Gain Crossover omegaGC where magnitude equals 0 dB]
    plotPhase --> findWPC[Mark Phase Crossover omegaPC where phase equals minus 180 deg]
    findWGC --> measPM[Measure Phase Margin PM at omegaGC]
    findWPC --> measGM[Measure Gain Margin GM at omegaPC]
    measPM --> verd{PM greater than 0 AND GM greater than 0}
    measGM --> verd
    verd -->|Yes| stable[System is Stable]
    verd -->|No| unstab[System is Unstable or Marginally Stable]
```

## 4.2 Visual Mapping of GM and PM on Bode Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAGAX["MAGNITUDE PLOT dB versus log omega"]
        M1[0 dB horizontal reference line] --- M2[Magnitude curve descending]
        M2 --- M3A[Point A at omegaGC where curve hits 0 dB]
    end
    subgraph PHAX["PHASE PLOT deg versus log omega"]
        P1[Minus 180 deg horizontal reference] --- P2[Phase curve descending]
        P2 --- P3A[Point B at omegaGC where phase angle equals phiGC]
        P2 --- P4A[Point C at omegaPC where phase hits minus 180 deg]
    end
    GMREG[Gain Margin GM equals vertical distance from Point C to 0 dB line] --- MAGAX
    GMREG --- PHAX
    PMREG[Phase Margin PM equals vertical distance from Point B to minus 180 deg line] --- MAGAX
    PMREG --- PHAX
```

## 4.3 Stability Region Map (PM vs GM Quadrants)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1["Quadrant I: PM greater than 0 deg AND GM greater than 0 dB --> STABLE well damped"]
    q2["Quadrant II: PM less than 0 deg AND GM greater than 0 dB --> UNSTABLE via phase lag"]
    q3["Quadrant III: PM less than 0 deg AND GM less than 0 dB --> UNSTABLE both"]
    q4["Quadrant IV: PM greater than 0 deg AND GM less than 0 dB --> UNSTABLE via excess gain"]
    bord["On Axis PM equals 0 or GM equals 0 --> MARGINALLY STABLE sustained oscillation"]
    q1 --- bord
    q2 --- bord
    q3 --- bord
    q4 --- bord
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of Bode-Based Stability Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputR[Reference Input r of t] --> sumJ[Comparator Summing Junction]
    sumJ --> errSig[Error Signal e of t]
    errSig --> ctrlC[Controller C of s]
    ctrlC --> plantP[Plant G of s]
    plantP --> outY[Output y of t]
    outY --> fbPath[Feedback Path H of s equals 1]
    fbPath --> fbSig[Feedback Signal]
    fbSig --> sumJ
    plantP --> bodeInject[Inject j omega sweep]
    bodeInject --> bodeCompute[Bode Plot Engine magnitude and phase]
    bodeCompute --> metricExtract[Extract omegaGC omegaPC]
    metricExtract --> gmCalc[Compute GM at omegaPC]
    metricExtract --> pmCalc[Compute PM at omegaGC]
    gmCalc --> verdict[Stability Verdict Function of GM PM]
    pmCalc --> verdict
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]`
**Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. State the frequency at which each is measured.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain of a system can be increased before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. It is measured at the **phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$**, which is the frequency at which the open-loop phase angle equals $\mathbf{-180^{\circ}}$.
> $$\boxed{GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$
>
> **Phase Margin (PM):** It is defined as the additional phase angle (in degrees) that can be added to the open-loop system at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. It is measured at the **gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$**, where the magnitude of $G(j\omega)$ equals unity (0 dB).
> $$\boxed{PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})}$$
>
> *(1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for frequencies)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`
**How is the Bode plot used to assess the relative stability of a control system? What are the desirable values of GM and PM for a well-designed system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bode plot provides a *visual* measure of relative stability through **Gain Margin** and **Phase Margin**. A system is **stable** if both $GM > 0$ dB and $PM > 0^{\circ}$. The *magnitude* of these margins indicates the *degree* of stability ‚Äî larger margins imply a more robust system, less sensitive to parameter variations and noise.

- **Desirable GM:** typically **6 dB to 12 dB** (i.e., gain can be at least doubled before instability).
- **Desirable PM:** typically **$40^{\circ}$ to $60^{\circ}$** (gives a damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.4 \text{‚Äì} 0.6$ and overshoot $M_p \approx 25\% \text{‚Äì} 10\%$).

*(1 mark Bode interpretation + 1 mark stability rule + 1 mark typical design values)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A ‚Äî 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** The open-loop transfer function of a unity-feedback system is
$$G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s\,(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$$
Sketch the Bode plot and determine the **gain margin** and **phase margin**. Comment on the stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same system, if the gain is increased by a factor of 3 (i.e., $K = 6$ instead of 2), determine the new gain margin and phase margin. Is the system still stable? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Bode form & corner frequencies:**

$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{2}{j\omega\,(1+j0.5\omega)(1+j0.2\omega)}$; corner frequencies: $\omega_1 = 2$ rad/s, $\omega_2 = 5$ rad/s; initial slope = $-20$ dB/dec.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Asymptotic magnitude table (as derived in Section 3.2):**

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | 0.1 | 0.5 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 10 |
|------------------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| $M_{dB}$ (dB) | +26 | +12 | +6 | +6 | $-1$ | $-10$ | $-28$ |
| Slope | $-20$ | $-20$ | $-20$ | $-40$ | $-40$ | $-60$ | $-60$ |

**Step 3 ‚Äî Phase at corners:**

| $\omega$ | 0.5 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 |
|-----------|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| $\phi$ (deg) | $-97.1$ | $-110.7$ | $-132.8$ | $-167.7$ | $-202.6$ | $-226.4$ |

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute $\omega_{gc}$** (between 2 and 5, slope $-40$ dB/dec):
$$6 - 40\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}/2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{gc} = 2 \times 10^{0.15} \approx 2.83 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:**
$$\angle G(j2.83) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(1.414) - \tan^{-1}(0.566) = -174.24^{\circ}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Phase Margin:**
$$PM = 180^{\circ} - 174.24^{\circ} \approx \mathbf{+5.76^{\circ}}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Compute $\omega_{pc}$** (using $\tan^{-1}a + \tan^{-1}b = 90^{\circ} \Rightarrow ab = 1$):
$$0.1\,\omega_{pc}^{2} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**
$$\vert G(j3.16) \vert = \dfrac{2}{3.162 \times 1.871 \times 1.183} \approx 0.286$$

**Step 9 ‚Äî Gain Margin:**
$$GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(0.286) \approx \mathbf{+10.87 \text{ dB}}$$

**Stability comment:** Both $GM$ and $PM$ are positive, so the **closed-loop system is stable**, but margins are small ‚Üí *poor relative stability* (high overshoot expected). *Sketch the Bode plot on semi-log paper: 0.1 ‚Üí 1 dec horizontal axis; mark corners at 2 and 5; phase crosses $-180^{\circ}$ near $\omega_{pc} \approx 3.16$ where magnitude is below 0 dB (10.87 dB below).* ‚úì

---

#### Solution to Question A(b)

When the gain is increased by a factor of 3, the magnitude plot shifts **upward by $20\log_{10}(3) = 9.54$ dB** at every frequency. The phase plot is **unchanged**.

| Effect on Bode plot | Consequence |
|---------------------|-------------|
| Magnitude plot up by 9.54 dB | New $\omega_{gc}$ shifts to a higher frequency (where original magnitude was $-9.54$ dB) |
| Phase plot unchanged | $\omega_{pc}$ remains at 3.16 rad/s |
| New $GM_{dB}$ at same $\omega_{pc}$ | $GM_{dB,\text{new}} = 10.87 - 9.54 = +1.33$ dB |
| New $\omega_{gc}$: where original $M_{dB} = -9.54$ dB on $-60$ dB/dec segment (i.e., $\omega > 5$) | $M_{dB}(5) \approx -10$ dB; solve $-10 - 60\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}/5) = -9.54$ ‚Üí $\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}/5) = -7.67 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0$ ‚Üí $\omega_{gc,\text{new}} \approx 5$ rad/s |

**Phase at new $\omega_{gc} = 5$ rad/s:**
$$\angle G(j5) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(2.5) - \tan^{-1}(1) = -90^{\circ} - 68.2^{\circ} - 45^{\circ} = -203.2^{\circ}$$

**New Phase Margin:**
$$PM_{\text{new}} = 180^{\circ} - 203.2^{\circ} = \mathbf{-23.2^{\circ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often *forget to shift the magnitude plot* and just re-use the old $\omega_{gc}$. That loses **3 marks** in 14-mark questions. Always re-compute $\omega_{gc}$ after a gain change!
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A *negative* PM means the phase curve has crossed $-180^{\circ}$ **before** the magnitude reaches 0 dB ‚Äî the closed-loop is **unstable**. Do not write "barely stable" ‚Äî the verdict is **unstable**.

**Final Verdict:** The system with $K = 6$ is **unstable** (negative PM and tiny positive GM). $K$ must be reduced below $K_{\max} = 2 \times 10^{0.5435} \approx 7$ for stability.

---

### Question B ‚Äî 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for sketching the Bode magnitude and phase plots of a transfer function. Illustrate with the example
$$G(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+2)(s+5)}$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$
Find the value of $K$ that yields a **phase margin of $30^{\circ}$**. Also find the corresponding gain margin. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Step-by-step procedure (general):**

1. **Substitute** $s = j\omega$ to obtain $G(j\omega)$.
2. **Convert** to **Bode (time-constant) form**: factor out DC gain and express each factor as $(1+j\omega\tau)^{\pm 1}$.
3. **Identify** corner frequencies $\omega_c = 1/\tau$ for each first-order factor.
4. **Plot magnitude asymptotes**: initial slope is $-20N$ dB/dec (where $N$ is type number); slope changes by $\pm 20$ dB/dec at each corner.
5. **Plot phase**: integrate contributions; integrator = constant $-90^{\circ}$; each first-order factor = $0^\circ$ to $\mp 90^\circ$ across the corner (with $\pm 1$ decade transition window).
6. **Mark** $\omega_{gc}$ (0 dB crossing) and $\omega_{pc}$ ($-180^\circ$ phase crossing).
7. **Compute** $GM$ and $PM$ from these crossover points.

**Illustration for $G(s) = 10 / [(s+2)(s+5)]$:**

$$G(s) = \dfrac{10}{2 \cdot 5 \,(1+s/2)(1+s/5)} = \dfrac{1}{(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$$

- Corners: $\omega_1 = 2$, $\omega_2 = 5$ rad/s; DC gain = $20\log 1 = 0$ dB.
- Magnitude slopes: $0$ dB/dec up to $\omega_1$; $-20$ dB/dec from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$; $-40$ dB/dec beyond $\omega_2$.
- Phase: $0^\circ$ initially; $\to -90^\circ$ around $\omega_1$; $\to -180^\circ$ around $\omega_2$.
- (Note: phase **never** reaches $-180^\circ$ within the *asymptotic* flat region in the strict limit; full $-180^\circ$ is reached asymptotically at high frequency.)

---

#### Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write in Bode form:**

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \cdot (1+s/2)(1+s/5)} = \dfrac{K/10}{s\,(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$$

Let $K_1 = K/10$. So $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K_1}{j\omega\,(1+j0.5\omega)(1+j0.2\omega)}$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express magnitude and phase:**

$$M_{dB} = 20\log_{10} K_1 - 20\log_{10}\omega - 10\log_{10}(1+0.25\omega^2) - 10\log_{10}(1+0.04\omega^2)$$

$$\phi(\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply PM condition at $\omega = \omega_{gc}$:**

$$PM = 180^\circ + \phi(\omega_{gc}) = 30^\circ \;\Rightarrow\; \phi(\omega_{gc}) = -150^\circ$$

So: $\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{gc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{gc}) = 60^\circ$

Using the tangent addition formula:
$$\tan(60^\circ) = \sqrt{3} = \dfrac{0.5\omega_{gc} + 0.2\omega_{gc}}{1 - (0.5\omega_{gc})(0.2\omega_{gc})} = \dfrac{0.7\omega_{gc}}{1 - 0.1\omega_{gc}^{2}}$$

$$\sqrt{3}\,(1 - 0.1\omega_{gc}^{2}) = 0.7\omega_{gc}$$

$$1.732 - 0.1732\,\omega_{gc}^{2} = 0.7\omega_{gc}$$

$$0.1732\,\omega_{gc}^{2} + 0.7\,\omega_{gc} - 1.732 = 0$$

$$\omega_{gc}^{2} + 4.041\,\omega_{gc} - 10 = 0$$

$$\omega_{gc} = \dfrac{-4.041 + \sqrt{4.041^{2} + 40}}{2} = \dfrac{-4.041 + \sqrt{56.33}}{2} = \dfrac{-4.041 + 7.505}{2} \approx 1.732 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî At $\omega_{gc} = 1.732$ rad/s, magnitude must be 0 dB:**

$$M_{dB}(\omega_{gc}) = 20\log_{10}K_1 - 20\log_{10}(1.732) - 10\log_{10}(1+0.25 \cdot 3) - 10\log_{10}(1+0.04 \cdot 3) = 0$$

$$20\log_{10}K_1 = 20\log_{10}(1.732) + 10\log_{10}(1.75) + 10\log_{10}(1.12)$$

$$20\log_{10}K_1 = 4.77 + 2.43 + 0.49 = 7.69 \text{ dB}$$

$$K_1 = 10^{7.69/20} = 10^{0.3845} \approx 2.425$$

$$\boxed{K = 10 \cdot K_1 \approx 24.25}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute corresponding Gain Margin at $K = 24.25$:**

The original $GM_{dB}$ at $K = 10$ is found by computing $\omega_{pc}$ and $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ for $K_1 = 1$:

- $\omega_{pc}$: solve $0.1\,\omega_{pc}^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ rad/s
- $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{K_1=1} = \dfrac{1}{3.162 \times 1.871 \times 1.183} \approx 0.143$
- $GM_{dB,\,K=10} = -20\log(0.143) = +16.9$ dB

For $K = 24.25$, the gain has increased by $20\log_{10}(24.25/10) = 20\log_{10}(2.425) = 7.69$ dB.

$$GM_{dB,\,K=24.25} = 16.9 - 7.69 \approx \mathbf{+9.21 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** When using the tan-addition formula $\tan(A+B)$, students often forget the **denominator term** $(1 - ab)$ and write $(a+b) = \tan(60¬∞) = \sqrt{3}$. The correct step is $(a+b)/(1-ab) = \sqrt{3}$. This mistake costs **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #4:** Many students confuse the type-number "N" of the system with the number of poles ‚Äî they forget that **only the poles at the origin** count as integrators contributing to the initial slope of the magnitude plot.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bode Plots ‚Äî GM & PM**

- ‚òê **Bode plot = Magnitude (dB) plot + Phase plot** against $\log \omega$.
- ‚òê **Initial slope** = $-20N$ dB/decade, where $N$ = number of poles at origin.
- ‚òê **Corner frequencies** = reciprocals of time constants: $\omega_c = 1/\tau$.
- ‚òê **Slope change** = $\mp 20$ dB/dec per simple pole/zero at each corner.
- ‚òê **Phase contribution**: integrator ‚Üí $-90¬∞$ (constant); first-order pole ‚Üí $0¬∞$ to $-90¬∞$ across corner; first-order zero ‚Üí $0¬∞$ to $+90¬∞$.
- ‚òê **$\omega_{gc}$** = frequency where magnitude curve **crosses 0 dB**.
- ‚òê **$\omega_{pc}$** = frequency where phase curve **crosses $-180¬∞$**.
- ‚òê **$GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$**; positive GM ‚Üí stable.
- ‚òê **$PM = 180¬∞ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$**; positive PM ‚Üí stable.
- ‚òê **Both GM and PM must be positive** for closed-loop stability (minimum-phase systems).
- ‚òê **Design target:** $PM \in [40¬∞, 60¬∞]$, $GM \in [6, 12]$ dB.
- ‚òê **Approximate damping** from PM: $\zeta \approx PM/100$ (valid for $PM \le 70¬∞$).
- ‚òê **Increasing $K$** shifts magnitude plot *up* by $20\log K$ dB ‚Üí reduces both $GM$ and $PM$ (potentially destabilises).
- ‚òê **Lead compensator** ‚Üí boosts PM at $\omega_{gc}$ (adds positive phase).
- ‚òê **Lag compensator** ‚Üí lowers magnitude at $\omega_{gc}$ (reduces GM usage for steady-state error).
- ‚òê **For 14-mark Bode questions:** always tabulate $\omega, M_{dB}, \phi$ explicitly; show $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$ computations; never skip the stability comment.
- ‚òê **Sign convention pitfall:** magnitude *below* 0 dB at $\omega_{pc}$ ‚áí $GM_{dB} > 0$ ‚áí stable; magnitude *above* 0 dB at $\omega_{pc}$ ‚áí $GM_{dB} < 0$ ‚áí unstable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of State Space Techniques to Continuous Data Systems, State Space Representation of Discrete Time Systems - Transfer Function from State Space Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Review of State Space Techniques to Continuous Data Systems, State Space Representation of Discrete Time Systems - Transfer Function from State Space Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# üìò SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Review of State Space Techniques ‚Äî Continuous Data Systems

In classical control theory, a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** system is described by a **transfer function** $G(s)$, which is a ratio of polynomials in the Laplace variable $s$. While compact, the transfer function gives only the **input‚Äìoutput** relationship and hides the internal workings of the system. For **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)** systems, or when internal variables must be observed (e.g., in observer design or optimal control), the transfer function approach becomes cumbersome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> *State Space Representation* of a continuous-time LTI system is a set of **first-order matrix differential equations** of the form $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ and an algebraic output equation $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$, where $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **state vector**, $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the **input vector**, and $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the **output vector**.

**Where:**
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ ‚Äî **System (State) Matrix** ‚Äî governs the internal dynamics.
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ ‚Äî **Input Matrix** ‚Äî couples external inputs to the states.
- $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ ‚Äî **Output Matrix** ‚Äî picks the measurable outputs from the states.
- $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ ‚Äî **Direct Feed-through (Transmission) Matrix**.

The term **"State"** is rigorously defined as the *minimum set of variables* (along with the input) that is sufficient to uniquely determine the future behaviour of the system for $t \ge t_0$.

### üåê Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Vehicle Dashboard" View

Imagine driving a car. The **classical transfer function** is like knowing only the speed of the car at the steering wheel ‚Äî it tells you how fast you go when you press the accelerator, but tells you nothing about the engine RPM, fuel flow, or wheel torque.

The **state space view**, in contrast, is the *full dashboard*: it exposes every internal variable (engine RPM = $x_1$, fuel rate = $x_2$, coolant temperature = $x_3$) that, together with the pedal position ($u$), uniquely determines the car's speed ($y$). For a digital control engineer designing cruise control, this internal visibility is non-negotiable.

---

## 1.2 State Space Representation ‚Äî Discrete-Time Systems

A digital controller or sampled-data system only "sees" the world at discrete sampling instants $t = kT$, where $T$ is the **sampling period** (a critical design parameter, typically chosen by the **Shannon/Nyquist criterion**: $T < \pi/\omega_{\max}$). The continuous derivative $\dot{x}$ is no longer defined; instead we work with differences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition ‚Äî Discrete-Time State Space Model**
> An LTI discrete system is represented by the **first-order matrix difference equations**:
> $$x(k+1) = \Phi \, x(k) + \Gamma \, u(k)$$
> $$y(k) = C \, x(k) + D \, u(k)$$
> where $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ is the sample index.

**Where:**
- $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ ‚Äî **State Transition Matrix** (discrete-time system matrix).
- $\Gamma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ ‚Äî **Discrete Input Matrix**.

The two matrix pairs $(\Phi, \Gamma)$ and $(A, B)$ are related through the matrix exponential and integral, which we shall derive rigorously in Section 3.

### üåê Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Stop-Motion Movie"

If continuous-time state space is a *cinematic video* (every instant in time matters), then discrete-time state space is a *stop-motion animation* ‚Äî the picture is captured only at specific instants (frames) $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. Each frame records the current state of every puppet, and the next frame's state is *predicted* from the current frame and the puppeteer's inputs. The **state transition matrix $\Phi$** is the "physics engine" of the animation: it tells us how each puppet's pose evolves from frame $k$ to frame $k+1$.

---

## 1.3 Transfer Function from the State Space Model

A transfer function is essentially the Laplace (or Z-) transform of the system's input‚Äìoutput relationship, **assuming zero initial conditions**. From the state space model, we can algebraically eliminate the state vector $x$ to obtain a compact scalar/vector transfer function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Result ‚Äî Continuous-Time Transfer Function**
> $$G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B + D$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Result ‚Äî Discrete-Time Transfer Function (Pulse Transfer Function)**
> $$G(z) = C (zI - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D$$

The term $(sI - A)^{-1}$ is the **resolvent** of $A$, whose inverse Laplace transform gives the **state transition matrix** $e^{At}$. Analogously, $(zI - \Phi)^{-1}$ is the **resolvent** of $\Phi$ in the Z-domain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# üìò SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuous-Time State Equation ‚Äî Step-by-Step Logic

The continuous-time state equation $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ is derived as follows:

1. **Start with an $n$-th order linear ODE** describing the physical plant.
2. **Define $n$ state variables** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ ‚Äî typically the output and its first $(n-1)$ derivatives.
3. **Convert the $n$-th order ODE** into $n$ first-order ODEs (a standard "phase-variable" trick).
4. **Write the first-order equations in matrix form** to obtain $\dot{x} = A x + B u$.
5. **Express the output** as a linear combination of states and inputs ‚Üí $y = C x + D u$.

### 2.1.1 The Solution of the Continuous State Equation

The state equation is a **first-order linear matrix ODE**. Its complete, closed-form solution for a given initial state $x(0)$ and input $u(t)$ is:

$$x(t) = e^{A t} \, x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{A (t - \tau)} B \, u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

The matrix exponential $e^{A t}$ (sometimes denoted $\Phi(t)$) is defined by the convergent power series:
$$e^{A t} = I + A t + \frac{A^{2} t^{2}}{2!} + \frac{A^{3} t^{3}}{3!} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(A t)^{k}}{k!}$$

**Key properties of $e^{At}$:**
- $\Phi(0) = e^{A \cdot 0} = I$
- $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \cdot \Phi(t_2)$ ‚Äî *semigroup property*
- $\frac{d}{dt} e^{A t} = A \, e^{A t} = e^{A t} A$
- $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t) = e^{-A t}$

The Laplace-domain link is **the most exam-relevant identity**:
$$e^{A t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} (s I - A)^{-1}$$

---

## 2.2 Discretization ‚Äî From Continuous to Discrete State Space

Given the continuous LTI system $\dot{x} = A x + B u$, holding the input $u$ constant over a sampling interval $T$ (a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** assumption ‚Äî standard in digital control), the *exact* discrete-time equivalents are:

$$\Phi = e^{A T}$$

$$\Gamma = \left( \int_{0}^{T} e^{A \tau} \, d\tau \right) B = \left( \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^{k} T^{k+1}}{(k+1)!} \right) B = A^{-1} \left( e^{A T} - I \right) B$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The formula $\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I)B$ is valid **only when $A$ is invertible**. If $A$ is singular, the integral $\int_0^T e^{A\tau} d\tau$ must be evaluated by other means (series expansion or Sylvester's expansion).

---

## 2.3 The Discrete-Time State Equation ‚Äî Solution

Iterating the difference equation $x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$:

$$x(1) = \Phi x(0) + \Gamma u(0)$$
$$x(2) = \Phi x(1) + \Gamma u(1) = \Phi^{2} x(0) + \Phi \Gamma u(0) + \Gamma u(1)$$

By induction, the general solution is:
$$x(k) = \Phi^{k} x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \Phi^{k-j-1} \Gamma \, u(j)$$

The Z-domain version (assuming zero initial state):
$$X(z) = (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma \, U(z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Slick Trick** ‚Äî the discrete matrix $z$-transform of $\Phi^{k-1}$ for $k \ge 1$ is $\mathcal{Z}\{\Phi^{k-1}\} = (zI - \Phi)^{-1} z$ (one-sided). Combining with the $u(j)$ convolution produces the $X(z) = (zI-\Phi)^{-1} \Gamma U(z)$ result.

---

## 2.4 Deriving the Transfer Function ‚Äî The Heart of the Module

### 2.4.1 Continuous-Time Case

Take the Laplace transform of both state and output equations (zero initial conditions):
$$s X(s) = A X(s) + B U(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad X(s) = (s I - A)^{-1} B U(s)$$

$$Y(s) = C X(s) + D U(s) = \left[ C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D \right] U(s)$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D\,}$$

### 2.4.2 Discrete-Time Case (Pulse Transfer Function)

Taking the one-sided Z-transform (zero initial conditions):
$$z X(z) = \Phi X(z) + \Gamma U(z) \quad \Rightarrow \quad X(z) = (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma U(z)$$

$$Y(z) = C X(z) + D U(z) = \left[ C (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D \right] U(z)$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\,G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility** ‚Äî The formula $G(z) = C(zI-\Phi)^{-1}\Gamma + D$ is the *workhorse* of digital control. Once the discrete TF is in hand, classical z-plane tools (root locus, Nyquist, Bode) can be applied. The expression also enables **controllability/observability** tests (via $\Phi, \Gamma, C$ matrices) ‚Äî topics for Module 5.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet üìù

| # | Topic | Formula | Remarks / Units |
|---|-------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Continuous State Equation | $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | $A$: $n \times n$ |
| 2 | Continuous Output Equation | $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$ | $C$: $p \times n$ |
| 3 | Matrix Exponential | $e^{A t} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}$ | Convergent for all $A$ |
| 4 | Laplace ‚Üî State Transition | $\mathcal{L}\{e^{A t}\} = (s I - A)^{-1}$ | Identity (KEY) |
| 5 | State Solution (continuous) | $x(t) = e^{A t} x(0) + \int_0^t e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau) d\tau$ | Initial cond. included |
| 6 | Discretized State Matrix | $\Phi = e^{A T}$ | $T$ = sampling period (s) |
| 7 | Discretized Input Matrix | $\Gamma = A^{-1}(e^{A T} - I) B$ | Requires $\det(A) \neq 0$ |
| 8 | Discrete State Equation | $x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$ | $k$ = sample index |
| 9 | State Solution (discrete) | $x(k) = \Phi^{k} x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \Phi^{k-j-1} \Gamma u(j)$ | Iterated form |
| 10 | Continuous Transfer Function | $G(s) = C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D$ | SISO / MIMO |
| 11 | Discrete Pulse Transfer Function | $G(z) = C (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D$ | ZOH-based |
| 12 | Eigenvalue Link (SISO) | Poles of $G(s)$ = eigenvalues of $A$ | $\det(sI - A) = 0$ |
| 13 | Eigenvalue Link (Discrete) | Poles of $G(z)$ = eigenvalues of $\Phi$ | $\det(zI - \Phi) = 0$ |
| 14 | Characteristic Polynomial | $\Delta(s) = \det(s I - A)$ | Order = $n$ |
| 15 | Cayley‚ÄìHamilton (auxiliary) | $A^{n} = -a_{n-1} A^{n-1} - \cdots - a_{0} I$ | Used to reduce $e^{At}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight** ‚Äî In SISO systems, the denominator polynomial of $G(s)$ is exactly $\det(sI - A)$ and the denominator of $G(z)$ is exactly $\det(zI - \Phi)$. Hence, the **poles of the transfer function = eigenvalues of the state matrix**. This is a high-yield link tested in KTU examinations.

# üìò SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Continuous-Time State Equation Solution

We seek $x(t)$ that satisfies $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ with $x(0)$ given.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Move all state terms to one side.**
$$\dot{x}(t) - A x(t) = B u(t)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Multiply both sides by the integrating factor $e^{-A t}$.**
$$e^{-A t} \dot{x}(t) - e^{-A t} A x(t) = e^{-A t} B u(t)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Recognize the left side as a perfect derivative.**
Recall that $\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{-A t} x(t)\right) = -A e^{-A t} x(t) + e^{-A t} \dot{x}(t)$ (since $A$ commutes with $e^{-At}$). Therefore:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{-A t} x(t)\right) = e^{-A t} B u(t)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Integrate from $0$ to $t$.**
$$e^{-A t} x(t) - e^{-A \cdot 0} x(0) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-A \tau} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for $x(t)$ by left-multiplying by $e^{A t}$.**
$$x(t) = e^{A t} x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{A (t - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> **Conversion logic:** The term $e^{At}x(0)$ is the **zero-input response** (natural evolution), and the integral is the **zero-state response** (forced evolution). This separation is foundational in linear systems.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D$

We have the Laplace-transformed equations (zero initial conditions):

**Step 1 ‚Äî Transform the state equation.**
$$\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = s X(s) - x(0) \xrightarrow{\,x(0)=0\,} s X(s)$$

So: $s X(s) = A X(s) + B U(s)$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Group the $X(s)$ terms.**
$$s X(s) - A X(s) = B U(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad (s I - A) X(s) = B U(s)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for $X(s)$ by left-multiplying by $(sI - A)^{-1}$.**
$$X(s) = (s I - A)^{-1} B U(s)$$

> The matrix $(sI-A)^{-1}$ exists wherever $\det(sI - A) \neq 0$, i.e., away from the eigenvalues of $A$.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute into the output equation $Y(s) = C X(s) + D U(s)$.**
$$Y(s) = C (s I - A)^{-1} B U(s) + D U(s) = \left[ C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D \right] U(s)$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Form the transfer function as the ratio $Y(s)/U(s)$.**
$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Discretization of the State Equation

Given $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ and assuming $u(t)$ is held constant over each sampling interval (ZOH), we want to find $x((k+1)T)$ in terms of $x(kT)$ and $u(kT)$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Express the solution over one interval $[kT, (k+1)T]$.**
From Section 3.1, with $t_0 = kT$ and $t = (k+1)T$:
$$x((k+1)T) = e^{A T} x(kT) + \int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A((k+1)T - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply ZOH: $u(\tau) = u(kT)$ for $\tau \in [kT, (k+1)T]$.**
$$x((k+1)T) = e^{A T} x(kT) + \left( \int_{0}^{T} e^{A \lambda} \, d\lambda \right) B u(kT)$$

(substitution $\lambda = (k+1)T - \tau$)

**Step 3 ‚Äî Define the discrete matrices.**
$$\Phi \triangleq e^{A T}, \quad \Gamma \triangleq \left( \int_{0}^{T} e^{A \lambda} \, d\lambda \right) B$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Closed-form expression for $\Gamma$ (when $A$ is non-singular).**
$$\Gamma = \left( \int_{0}^{T} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(A \lambda)^k}{k!} d\lambda \right) B = \left( \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k T^{k+1}}{(k+1)!} \right) B = A^{-1}(e^{A T} - I) B = A^{-1}(\Phi - I) B$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Write the discrete model (dropping the explicit $T$ notation).**
$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Discrete Pulse Transfer Function $G(z) = C(zI - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Take the one-sided Z-transform of $x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$.**

Using $\mathcal{Z}\{x(k+1)\} = z X(z) - z x(0)$ and zero initial conditions:
$$z X(z) = \Phi X(z) + \Gamma U(z)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Group the $X(z)$ terms.**
$$(z I - \Phi) X(z) = \Gamma U(z)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for $X(z)$.**
$$X(z) = (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma U(z)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute into the output equation.**
$$Y(z) = C (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma U(z) + D U(z)$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Form the transfer function.**
$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C (z I - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example ‚Äî From State Space to Transfer Function

**Problem:** A SISO system is given by
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

Find $G(s)$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Form $(sI - A)$.**
$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the inverse using the adjugate formula.**
$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^{2} + 3s + 2$$

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $(sI - A)^{-1} B$.**
$$(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute $G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B + D$.**
$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^{2} + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 3s + 2}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Factor the denominator and final form.**
$$G(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Notice that the poles $\{s = -1, s = -2\}$ are exactly the eigenvalues of $A$ (solve $\det(sI - A) = 0$), confirming the pole-eigenvalue duality.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example ‚Äî From Continuous to Discrete State Space

**Problem:** Given
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T = 0.1 \, \text{s}$$

Compute $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $e^{AT}$ via series or Sylvester's method.** We use the auxiliary polynomial $A^2$:
$$A^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $A^2 + A = 0$, i.e., $A^2 = -A$. By Cayley‚ÄìHamilton, $e^{At} = \alpha_0 I + \alpha_1 A$, where $e^{\lambda_i t} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \lambda_i$ for each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 = -1$.
- $e^{0} = 1 = \alpha_0 + 0 \cdot \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \alpha_0 = 1$.
- $e^{-T} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1(-1) = 1 - \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 = 1 - e^{-T}$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Form $\Phi$.**
$$\Phi = e^{A T} = I + (1 - e^{-T}) A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + (1 - e^{-0.1}) \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

For $T = 0.1$, $1 - e^{-0.1} \approx 0.0952$:
$$\Phi \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.0952 \\ 0 & 0.9048 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Form $\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I) B$.**

First, $A^{-1}$: since $A$ is upper triangular with $A_{22} = -1 \neq 0$, we can solve. For this $2 \times 2$:
$$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{computation} \end{bmatrix}$$

But since $A_{11} = 0$, the formula $\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I)B$ has a singular $A$ issue. We use the series:
$$\Gamma = \int_0^T e^{A\lambda} d\lambda \cdot B = \int_0^T \left[ I + (1 - e^{-\lambda}) A \right] d\lambda \cdot B$$

$$\int_0^T I d\lambda = T I, \quad \int_0^T (1 - e^{-\lambda}) d\lambda = T + e^{-T} - 1$$

So $\Gamma = \left[ T I + (T + e^{-T} - 1) A \right] B$.

For $T = 0.1$, $T + e^{-T} - 1 \approx 0.00484$:
$$\Gamma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.00484 \\ 0 & 0.1 - 0.00484 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00484 \\ 0.09516 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The result $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$ matrices together fully describe the **equivalent discrete-time system** with sampling period $0.1$ s.

# üìò SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Continuous-Time State Space Realisation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical **Direct Programming realisation** of the continuous-time state space model $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$, with integrators as the core dynamic elements.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeU(["Input u(t)"]) --> nodeSum1(("+"))
    nodeU --> nodeMatD["Matrix D"]
    nodeSum1 --> nodeMatA["Matrix A (n x n)"]
    nodeMatA --> nodeInt1[/"‚à´ dt (x1)"/]
    nodeInt1 --> nodeInt2[/"‚à´ dt (x2)"/]
    nodeInt1 --> nodeIntN[/"...‚à´ dt (xn)"/]
    nodeInt2 --> nodeMatC["Matrix C (p x n)"]
    nodeIntN --> nodeMatC
    nodeMatC --> nodeSum2(("+"))
    nodeMatD --> nodeSum2
    nodeSum2 --> nodeY(["Output y(t)"])
    nodeInt1 -.feedback.-> nodeSum1
    nodeInt2 -.feedback.-> nodeSum1
    nodeIntN -.feedback.-> nodeSum1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The state vector $x(t)$ is the **output of the integrators**.
- It is fed back through the $A$ matrix and added to the input (multiplied by $B$) to form $\dot{x}$.
- The output $y$ is formed by combining $Cx$ and $Du$.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram ‚Äî Discrete-Time State Space Realisation

For the discrete system, the integrators are replaced by **unit-delay elements** $z^{-1}$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeU2(["Input u(k)"]) --> nodeSum3(("+"))
    nodeU2 --> nodeMatD2["Matrix D"]
    nodeSum3 --> nodeMatPhi["Matrix Œ¶ (n x n)"]
    nodeMatPhi --> nodeDelay1[">1 sample delay<br/>z‚Åª¬π (x1)"]
    nodeDelay1 --> nodeDelay2[">1 sample delay<br/>z‚Åª¬π (x2)"]
    nodeDelay1 --> nodeDelayN["... delay z‚Åª¬π (xn)"]
    nodeDelay2 --> nodeMatC2["Matrix C (p x n)"]
    nodeDelayN --> nodeMatC2
    nodeMatC2 --> nodeSum4(("+"))
    nodeMatD2 --> nodeSum4
    nodeSum4 --> nodeY2(["Output y(k)"])
    nodeDelay1 -.feedback.-> nodeSum3
    nodeDelay2 -.feedback.-> nodeSum3
    nodeDelayN -.feedback.-> nodeSum3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The state $x(k)$ is stored in delay elements.
- It is fed back through the $\Phi$ matrix and added to $\Gamma u(k)$ to form $x(k+1)$.
- The output is $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow ‚Äî Converting Between Representations

The diagram below shows the **two-way conversion** between state space and transfer function:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SSBlock["State Space Domain"]
        direction TB
        SS1["·∫ã = A x + B u<br/>y = C x + D u"] 
        SS2["x(k+1) = Œ¶ x(k) + Œì u(k)<br/>y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)"]
    end
    subgraph TFBlock["Transfer Function Domain"]
        direction TB
        TF1["G(s) = C(sI-A)‚Åª¬πB + D"]
        TF2["G(z) = C(zI-Œ¶)‚Åª¬πŒì + D"]
    end
    SS1 -- "Resolvent +<br/>matrix inverse" --> TF1
    SS2 -- "Resolvent +<br/>matrix inverse" --> TF2
    TF1 -- "Coefficient matching<br/>(controllable canonical)" --> SS1
    TF2 -- "Coefficient matching<br/>(controllable canonical)" --> SS2
    SS1 -- "Discretisation<br/>Œ¶=e^{AT}, Œì=A‚Åª¬π(Œ¶-I)B" --> SS2
    SS2 -- "Inverse sampling" --> SS1
```

---

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Graph ‚Äî SISO 2nd-Order State Space (Controllable Canonical Form)

For a 2nd-order SISO system, the controllable canonical state space representation corresponds to the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{b_0 s^2 + b_1 s + b_2}{s^2 + a_1 s + a_2}$. The matrices are:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} b_2 & b_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = b_0$$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode(["u(t)"]) --> sumS1(("+"))
    sumS1 --> x1Int[/"x1"/]
    x1Int -- "1" --> x2Int[/"x2"/]
    x2Int -- "-a2" --> sumS1
    x2Int -- "-a1" --> sumS1
    x1Int -- "b2" --> sumS2(("+"))
    x2Int -- "b1" --> sumS2
    inNode -- "b0" --> sumS2
    sumS2 --> outNode(["y(t)"])
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note** ‚Äî The **controllable canonical form** is the most common starting point when one has a transfer function and needs a state space model. It guarantees the pair $(A, B)$ is **controllable** by construction, which is essential for pole-placement design (covered later in the syllabus).
