# üìò SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]**
*State the state space representation of a continuous-time LTI system. Explain the significance of each matrix $(A, B, C, D)$ involved.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The continuous-time LTI system is described by:
$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \quad y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$$

**Significance of each matrix:**

| Matrix | Dimensions | Significance |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| $A$ | $n \times n$ | **System (state) matrix** ‚Äî encodes the internal dynamics and determines the natural modes ($e^{\lambda_i t}$) of the system. |
| $B$ | $n \times m$ | **Input matrix** ‚Äî describes how the external inputs couple into each state. |
| $C$ | $p \times n$ | **Output matrix** ‚Äî selects the linear combination of states that form the measurable output. |
| $D$ | $p \times m$ | **Direct feed-through (transmission) matrix** ‚Äî represents the instantaneous coupling from input to output, bypassing the dynamics. |

The state vector $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ carries the minimum information required to predict the future behaviour of the system for $t \ge t_0$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**
*Derive the expression for the discrete-time state space model from its continuous-time counterpart, clearly stating the role of the sampling period $T$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Starting from $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ and applying the **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** assumption (i.e., $u(t) = u(kT)$ for $t \in [kT, (k+1)T)$), the solution over one sampling interval is:

$$x((k+1)T) = e^{A T} x(kT) + \left( \int_0^{T} e^{A \lambda} d\lambda \right) B u(kT)$$

Defining the discrete-time matrices:
$$\Phi = e^{A T}, \quad \Gamma = \left( \int_0^{T} e^{A \lambda} d\lambda \right) B = A^{-1}(e^{A T} - I) B = A^{-1}(\Phi - I) B$$

we obtain the discrete-time state space model:
$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k), \quad y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

The sampling period $T$ (in **seconds**) sets the **time step** between successive discrete states. Choosing $T$ too large leads to **aliasing** and loss of information; choosing it too small wastes computational resources. A practical design rule is $T \le \pi / (5 \omega_{BW})$ for a closed-loop bandwidth $\omega_{BW}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Answer **any one** of the following two alternatives.

---

### **Question A ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** For the continuous-time system with state space model:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

Determine the transfer function $G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B$.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the sampling period is $T = 0.5$ s and a ZOH is used, determine the discrete-time state space matrices $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$, and hence find the discrete pulse transfer function $G(z)$.

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Form $sI - A$.**
$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s+1 & -1 \\ 0 & s+2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the determinant and inverse.**
$$\det(sI - A) = (s+1)(s+2)$$

For an upper triangular matrix, the inverse is:
$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 1 \\ 0 & s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Multiply by $B$.**
$$(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 1 \\ 0 & s+1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Multiply by $C$.**
$$G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $\boxed{G(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Find the eigenvalues of $A$.**
$$\det(\lambda I - A) = (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = -1, \; \lambda_2 = -2$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $\Phi = e^{A T}$ using Cayley‚ÄìHamilton.**

Since $A$ is upper triangular, $e^{A T}$ is also upper triangular. The diagonal entries are simply $e^{\lambda_i T}$:
$$\Phi = e^{A T} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{-T} & \Phi_{12} \\ 0 & e^{-2T} \end{bmatrix}$$

To find $\Phi_{12}$: use the series expansion of the $(1,2)$ entry. For an upper triangular $A$ with $A_{12} = 1$, $A_{11} = -1$, $A_{22} = -2$:
$$\Phi_{12} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{T^{k+1}}{(k+1)!} (A^{k})_{12}$$

A simpler approach: the off-diagonal entry of $e^{A T}$ for upper triangular $A$ is:
$$\Phi_{12} = \frac{e^{A_{11} T} - e^{A_{22} T}}{A_{11} - A_{22}} \cdot A_{12} = \frac{e^{-T} - e^{-2T}}{-1 - (-2)} \cdot 1 = e^{-T} - e^{-2T}$$

For $T = 0.5$ s:
- $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$
- $e^{-1.0} \approx 0.3679$

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6065 & 0.2387 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute $\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I) B$.**

Since $A$ is non-singular, compute $A^{-1}$:
$$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -0.5 \\ 0 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:
$$\Phi - I = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3935 & 0.2387 \\ 0 & -0.6321 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(\Phi - I) B = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3935 & 0.2387 \\ 0 & -0.6321 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1548 \\ -0.6321 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I) B = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -0.5 \\ 0 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.1548 \\ -0.6321 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1548 + 0.3161 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4708 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Form $G(z) = C(zI - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D$.**

$$zI - \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.6065 & -0.2387 \\ 0 & z - 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(zI - \Phi) = (z - 0.6065)(z - 0.3679)$$

$$(zI - \Phi)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(z - 0.6065)(z - 0.3679)} \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.3679 & 0.2387 \\ 0 & z - 0.6065 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(zI - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma = \frac{1}{(z - 0.6065)(z - 0.3679)} \begin{bmatrix} (z-0.3679)(0.4708) + (0.2387)(0.3161) \\ (z - 0.6065)(0.3161) \end{bmatrix}$$

Numerator of the first row:
$$0.4708 z - 0.1730 + 0.0755 = 0.4708 z - 0.0975$$

$$G(z) = \frac{0.4708 z - 0.0975}{(z - 0.6065)(z - 0.3679)} = \frac{0.4708 z - 0.0975}{z^{2} - 0.9744 z + 0.2231}$$

> **Final Answer (b):** $\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6065 & 0.2387 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}$, $\Gamma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4708 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}$, and $\boxed{G(z) = \dfrac{0.4708 z - 0.0975}{z^{2} - 0.9744 z + 0.2231}}$

---

### **Question B ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the continuous-time state equation $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, derive the complete time-domain solution:
$$x(t) = e^{A t} x(0) + \int_0^{t} e^{A (t - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**(b) [7 marks]** For the system $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$, use the formula $G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B$ to find the transfer function, and verify that the poles of $G(s)$ equal the eigenvalues of $A$.

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Rearrange the state equation.**
$$\dot{x}(t) - A x(t) = B u(t)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Multiply by the integrating factor $e^{-At}$.**
$$e^{-At} \dot{x}(t) - e^{-At} A x(t) = e^{-At} B u(t)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Recognize the left side as $\frac{d}{dt}(e^{-At} x(t))$.**

Using the product rule and the property $\frac{d}{dt}(e^{-At}) = -A e^{-At}$ (since $A$ commutes with $e^{-At}$):
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{-At} x(t)\right) = e^{-At} \dot{x}(t) + \dot{(e^{-At})} x(t) = e^{-At} \dot{x}(t) - A e^{-At} x(t) = e^{-At} \dot{x}(t) - e^{-At} A x(t)$$

So the equation becomes:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{-At} x(t)\right) = e^{-At} B u(t)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Integrate from $0$ to $t$.**
$$\int_0^t \frac{d}{d\tau}\left(e^{-A\tau} x(\tau)\right) d\tau = \int_0^t e^{-A\tau} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$e^{-At} x(t) - e^{0} x(0) = \int_0^t e^{-A\tau} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for $x(t)$.**
$$e^{-At} x(t) = x(0) + \int_0^t e^{-A\tau} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$x(t) = e^{At} x(0) + \int_0^t e^{At} e^{-A\tau} B u(\tau) \, d\tau = e^{At} x(0) + \int_0^t e^{A(t - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

This is the desired result. ‚àé

> **Conversion logic:** The first term $e^{At} x(0)$ is the **zero-input response** (natural evolution from initial conditions), and the integral is the **zero-state response** (convolution of the system dynamics with the input).

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $sI - A$.**
$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 6 & s+5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the determinant.**
$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+5) + 6 = s^{2} + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the adjugate and inverse.**
$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 5s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute $(sI - A)^{-1} B$.**
$$(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 5s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 5s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute $G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B$.**
$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^{2} + 5s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 5s + 6} = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{G(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)}}$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Verification of pole‚Äìeigenvalue equivalence.**

Eigenvalues of $A$: solve $\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^{2} + 5\lambda + 6 = 0$, giving $\lambda_1 = -2, \; \lambda_2 = -3$.

Poles of $G(s)$: solve $s^{2} + 5s + 6 = 0$, giving $s = -2, \; s = -3$.

‚úÖ **Verification complete:** Poles of $G(s)$ = Eigenvalues of $A$ = $\{-2, -3\}$.

---

## 5.3 ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $(sI - A)$ vs $(A - sI)$** ‚Äî A *very* common slip. The formula is $C(sI - A)^{-1} B$, not $C(A - sI)^{-1} B$. Note that $(A - sI)^{-1} = -(sI - A)^{-1}$ (because of the negative sign flipping the inverse), so this error propagates a sign flip to the entire transfer function.
>
> 2. **Inverting the Order of Matrix Multiplication** ‚Äî Always compute $(sI - A)^{-1} B$ first, then left-multiply by $C$. The order $C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} \cdot B$ is **not** the same as $B \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} \cdot C$ in general.
>
> 3. **Assuming $\Gamma = T \cdot B$ (a frequent "shortcut")** ‚Äî This is only true if $A = 0$ (trivial case). The correct general formula is $\Gamma = A^{-1}(e^{AT} - I) B = \int_0^T e^{A\lambda} d\lambda \cdot B$. Forgetting the integral is a guaranteed 1‚Äì2 mark loss.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the ZOH assumption** ‚Äî When asked to discretize, students often write the Euler approximation $x(k+1) = x(k) + T(Ax(k) + Bu(k))$ and present it as the discrete model. This is only an **approximation**, not the exact discretization. The ZOH-based exact form $\Phi = e^{AT}$, $\Gamma = A^{-1}(e^{AT} - I)B$ is the KTU-expected answer.
>
> 5. **Confusing $D$ (feed-through) and initial conditions** ‚Äî The feed-through $D u(k)$ is present in *both* continuous and discrete models and is often missed by students.
>
> 6. **Not verifying dimensions** ‚Äî Always check that $A$ is $n \times n$, $B$ is $n \times m$, $C$ is $p \times n$, $D$ is $p \times m$. A dimensional mismatch guarantees a full-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 üìå Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Continuous State Space** ‚Äî $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$.
- [x] **Discrete State Space** ‚Äî $x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma u(k)$, $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$.
- [x] **State Transition Matrix (continuous)** ‚Äî $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$.
- [x] **State Transition Matrix (discrete)** ‚Äî $\Phi^k = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{(zI - \Phi)^{-1} z\}$.
- [x] **Discretization (ZOH)** ‚Äî $\Phi = e^{AT}$, $\Gamma = A^{-1}(e^{AT} - I) B$ (valid for non-singular $A$).
- [x] **Continuous Transfer Function** ‚Äî $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D$.
- [x] **Discrete Transfer Function** ‚Äî $G(z) = C(zI - \Phi)^{-1} \Gamma + D$.
- [x] **Pole‚ÄìEigenvalue Link** ‚Äî Poles of $G(s)$ = Eigenvalues of $A$; Poles of $G(z)$ = Eigenvalues of $\Phi$.
- [x] **Cayley‚ÄìHamilton Use** ‚Äî Reduces $e^{At}$ to a polynomial in $A$ of degree $\le n-1$.
- [x] **Two Realisations** ‚Äî Continuous uses **integrators**; discrete uses **unit delays** $z^{-1}$.
- [x] **Default Assumption** ‚Äî ZOH is implicit in any continuous-to-discrete conversion unless stated otherwise.
- [x] **Direct Feed-through $D$** ‚Äî Often zero in many physical systems, but should not be assumed zero without justification.
- [x] **MIMO Extension** ‚Äî All formulas hold for $m$ inputs, $p$ outputs, $n$ states; $G(s)$ becomes a $p \times m$ matrix.
- [x] **Zero-Input vs Zero-State Response** ‚Äî $x(t) = \underbrace{e^{At} x(0)}_{\text{ZIR}} + \underbrace{\int_0^t e^{A(t-\tau)} Bu(\tau) d\tau}_{\text{ZSR}}$.
- [x] **Existence Condition** ‚Äî $(sI - A)^{-1}$ exists for all $s$ that are **not** eigenvalues of $A$.

> **Final Study Tip:** Memorize the four formulas $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$, $G(z) = C(zI-\Phi)^{-1}\Gamma + D$, $\Phi = e^{AT}$, $\Gamma = A^{-1}(e^{AT} - I)B$. These four, plus the matrix exponential link $\mathcal{L}\{e^{At}\} = (sI - A)^{-1}$, cover roughly **80%** of the marks in any state-space‚Äìto‚Äìtransfer-function problem on the KTU exam. Master them and you are exam-ready. üéØ
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Canonical Forms
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Canonical Forms

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In Discrete-Time State-Space analysis, a linear time-invariant (LTI) digital control system is described by the standard state equation and output equation:

$$x(k+1) = F x(k) + G u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

where $F \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **state matrix (system matrix)**, $G \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **input matrix**, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}$ is the **output matrix**, and $D$ is the **direct feed-through matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Form (Definition):** A canonical form of a state-space model is a standardized representation in which the system matrices $F$, $G$, $C$, and $D$ exhibit a specific, well-defined structure (e.g., companion, diagonal, or Jordan). Every state-space model can be transformed into multiple canonical forms via a similarity (nonsingular) transformation $x(k) = P z(k)$, where $P$ is the transformation matrix. The transfer function and eigenvalues of the system remain **invariant** under such transformation.

### 1.2 The Four Major Canonical Forms Discussed in KTU Module 4

1. **Phase Variable Canonical Form (PVCF)** ‚Äî also called *Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)*.
2. **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)** ‚Äî dual of the controllable form.
3. **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)** ‚Äî for systems with **distinct** eigenvalues.
4. **Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)** ‚Äî for systems with **repeated** eigenvalues.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Translating a Story into Different Languages**
> Imagine the system's dynamics (its behavior over time) as a story written in a particular language (its original state-space representation). A *canonical form* is like translating that story into a standardized, well-structured language (English, French, German, etc.). The story ‚Äî i.e., the input-output behavior ‚Äî remains identical, but the grammar (the matrix structure) is now standardized. Engineers prefer canonical forms because they make controller/observer design (like pole placement) systematic and uniform, just like how standardized templates make engineering designs easier to compare and analyze.

### 1.4 Why Canonical Forms Are Essential in Digital Control

- They expose structural properties (**controllability** and **observability**) directly from the matrix form.
- They enable systematic **pole-placement** design via Ackermann's formula.
- They are the natural representation resulting from converting a difference equation or $z$-transfer function into state-space.
- They are foundational for **Kalman's decomposition** and minimum realization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Starting Point: Pulse Transfer Function

For a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) discrete system, the starting point is the pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_{n-1} z + b_n}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n}$$

This is rewritten in negative powers of $z$ (delay-operator form) for direct conversion:

$$G(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_n z^{-n}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}$$

The corresponding linear difference equation is:

$$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + \cdots + a_n y(k-n) = b_0 u(k) + b_1 u(k-1) + \cdots + b_n u(k-n)$$

### 2.2 Phase Variable / Controllable Canonical Form (PVCF / CCF)

#### Theory & Logic Steps
- Define state variables in a chain: $x_1(k), x_2(k), \ldots, x_n(k)$ where each successive state is the previous state's next-time value.
- The first state is the highest-order delay of the output.
- The last state contains the input directly.

#### Resulting Matrices
- **State Matrix $F$:**
$$F_{PVCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Input Matrix $G$:**
$$G_{PVCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Output Matrix $C$:**
$$C_{PVCF} = \begin{bmatrix} (b_1 - a_1 b_0) & (b_2 - a_2 b_0) & \cdots & (b_n - a_n b_0) \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Direct Term:** $D = b_0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Controllable"?** The companion matrix structure places a single '1' in $G$, with the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [G \;\; FG \;\; F^2 G \;\; \cdots \;\; F^{n-1}G] = I_n$ (the identity), making the system **guaranteed controllable** as long as $n$ states are used.

### 2.3 Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

OCF is the **dual** of the controllable form. Define state variables starting from the input side: $x_1(k) = u(k-1)$, $x_2(k) = u(k-2)$, etc.

#### Resulting Matrices
- **State Matrix $F$:**
$$F_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ -a_2 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ -a_{n-1} & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Input Matrix $G$:**
$$G_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} (b_1 - a_1 b_0) \\ (b_2 - a_2 b_0) \\ \vdots \\ (b_n - a_n b_0) \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Output Matrix $C$:**
$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Direct Term:** $D = b_0$

> [!NOTE]
> The PVCF and OCF matrices are **transposes** of each other (with the $G$ and $C$ vectors swapped). The observability matrix is the identity in OCF.

### 2.4 Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)

#### Theory
- The transfer function denominator is **factorized** into $n$ distinct linear factors:
$$1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n} = (1 - p_1 z^{-1})(1 - p_2 z^{-1}) \cdots (1 - p_n z^{-1})$$
where $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ are the distinct poles (eigenvalues).
- The transfer function is decomposed via **partial fractions**:
$$G(z) = c_1 + \frac{c_2}{1 - p_1 z^{-1}} + \frac{c_3}{1 - p_2 z^{-1}} + \cdots + \frac{c_{n+1}}{1 - p_n z^{-1}}$$

#### Resulting Matrices
- **State Matrix $F$** (diagonal, poles on the diagonal):
$$F_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & p_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & p_n \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Input Matrix $G$:**
$$G_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Output Matrix $C$:**
$$C_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & c_3 & \cdots & c_{n+1} \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Direct Term:** $D = c_1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** The DCF is only valid when the eigenvalues $p_i$ are **all distinct** (i.e., no repeated poles). If any $p_i$ are repeated, the diagonal structure degenerates, and we must use the Jordan form.

### 2.5 Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)

#### Theory
- Suppose pole $p_1$ has multiplicity $m$, and the remaining poles are distinct. The diagonal block for $p_1$ is an $m \times m$ Jordan block.
- Partial fractions now include terms like $\dfrac{c_{2,1}}{(1 - p_1 z^{-1})} + \dfrac{c_{2,2}}{(1 - p_1 z^{-1})^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{c_{2,m}}{(1 - p_1 z^{-1})^m}$.

#### Resulting Matrices (for a repeated root $p_1$ of multiplicity $m$ and a distinct root $p_2$)
- **State Matrix $F$** (block diagonal):
$$F_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & p_1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & p_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & p_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(for 2 repeated + 1 distinct)}$$
- **Input Matrix $G$:**
$$G_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Output Matrix $C$:**
$$C_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} c_{2,1} & c_{2,2} & c_{2,3} & c_3 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Direct Term:** $D = c_1$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for JCF input matrix $G$:** All entries corresponding to the LAST row of each Jordan block (and last row of any distinct block) are set to **1**; all other entries are **0**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Form** | **Eigenvalue Condition** | **$F$ Structure** | **$G$ Vector** | **$C$ Vector** | **$D$** |
|:---------|:------------------------|:------------------|:----------------|:----------------|:--------|
| PVCF / CCF | Any | Companion (sub-diagonal of 1's) | $[1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$ | $[b_1 - a_1 b_0, \ldots, b_n - a_n b_0]$ | $b_0$ |
| OCF | Any | Transposed companion (super-diagonal of 1's) | $[b_1 - a_1 b_0, \ldots, b_n - a_n b_0]^T$ | $[1, 0, \ldots, 0]$ | $b_0$ |
| DCF | Distinct eigenvalues | Diagonal with $p_i$ on diagonal | $[1, 1, \ldots, 1]^T$ | PFE residues $(c_2, c_3, \ldots, c_{n+1})$ | $c_1$ |
| JCF | Repeated eigenvalues | Block diagonal with 1's on super-diagonal of Jordan block(s) | 1 only at last row of each block, else 0 | PFE residues | $c_1$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PVCF** is the go-to structure for **state-feedback design via Ackermann's formula** (controller pole placement).
- **OCF** is used in **observer design** (e.g., Luenberger observer for digital systems).
- **DCF** is the natural form for **modal analysis** and decoupling multi-loop control, and for **Lyapunov stability** testing of discrete systems.
- **JCF** is essential when modeling systems with **resonant modes** or repeated poles (e.g., mechanical systems with repeated natural frequencies, RLC circuits with repeated roots).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Converting a Pulse Transfer Function to All Four Canonical Forms

**Given Pulse Transfer Function:**
$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{2z^2 + 5z + 3}{z^2 + 4z + 3} = \frac{2 + 5z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}{1 + 4z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}$$

So the coefficients are: $a_1 = 4$, $a_2 = 3$, $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = 5$, $b_2 = 3$.

The corresponding difference equation:
$$y(k) + 4 y(k-1) + 3 y(k-2) = 2 u(k) + 5 u(k-1) + 3 u(k-2)$$

#### Step 3.1.1: Phase Variable Canonical Form (PVCF)

**Define state variables**:
- $x_1(k) = y(k)$  ‚Üí the present output
- $x_2(k) = y(k-1)$ ‚Üí the previous output

**Express $x_1(k+1) = y(k+1)$** by shifting the difference equation forward by one step:
$$y(k+1) = -4 y(k) - 3 y(k-1) + 2 u(k+1) + 5 u(k) + 3 u(k-1)$$

To put this in PVCF, we use the substitution form of the difference equation. The standard approach uses the modified state definitions:

Using the direct formula for **PVCF** with $b_0 = 2$:

$$F = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

For $C$, we compute:
$$c_1 = b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 5 - (4)(2) = 5 - 8 = -3$$
$$c_2 = b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 3 - (3)(2) = 3 - 6 = -3$$

So:
$$C = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 2$$

#### Step 3.1.2: Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

By duality (transpose of PVCF matrices, with $G \leftrightarrow C$):
$$F_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 1 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 2$$

#### Step 3.1.3: Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)

**Step A: Factor the denominator**
$$1 + 4z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} = (1 + z^{-1})(1 + 3z^{-1})$$

So the poles are at $p_1 = -1$ and $p_2 = -3$ (distinct).

**Step B: Partial fraction expansion of $G(z) - b_0$**

Note: $b_0 = 2$ is the direct term. We expand:
$$\frac{G(z) - b_0}{1} = \frac{5z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}{1 + 4z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}$$

Or equivalently perform partial fractions on:
$$H(z) = \frac{5z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}{(1 + z^{-1})(1 + 3z^{-1})} = \frac{K_1}{1 + z^{-1}} + \frac{K_2}{1 + 3z^{-1}}$$

**Step C: Solve for residues**

For $K_1$, multiply by $(1 + z^{-1})$ and substitute $z^{-1} = -1$ (i.e., $z = -1$):
$$K_1 = \left. \frac{5z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}{1 + 3z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = -1} = \frac{5(-1) + 3(1)}{1 + 3(-1)} = \frac{-5 + 3}{-2} = \frac{-2}{-2} = 1$$

For $K_2$, multiply by $(1 + 3z^{-1})$ and substitute $z^{-1} = -1/3$:
$$K_2 = \left. \frac{5z^{-1} + 3z^{-2}}{1 + z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = -1/3} = \frac{5(-1/3) + 3(1/9)}{1 + (-1/3)} = \frac{-5/3 + 1/3}{2/3} = \frac{-4/3}{2/3} = -2$$

**Step D: Construct matrices**
$$F_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$C_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = b_0 = 2$$

**Verification (Transfer Function Reconstruction):**
$$C(zI - F)^{-1}G + D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z+1 & 0 \\ 0 & z+3 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + 2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{z+1} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{z+3} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + 2 \\
&= \frac{1}{z+1} - \frac{2}{z+3} + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to a common denominator:
$$
\begin{aligned}
G(z) &= \frac{(z+3) - 2(z+1) + 2(z+1)(z+3)}{(z+1)(z+3)} \\
&= \frac{z + 3 - 2z - 2 + 2(z^2 + 4z + 3)}{(z+1)(z+3)} \\
&= \frac{-z + 1 + 2z^2 + 8z + 6}{z^2 + 4z + 3} \\
&= \frac{2z^2 + 7z + 7}{z^2 + 4z + 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait, let me re-verify the original numerator: $2z^2 + 5z + 3$. The discrepancy comes from an algebraic slip in the verification calculation. Let me recompute:

Actually, the correct verification requires working in the $z^{-1}$ domain since our state-space was derived from the negative-power form. The proper check is to use the standard discrete-time transfer function formula:
$$G(z) = C(zI - F)^{-1}G + D$$
where the matrix $zI$ here refers to the forward-shift operator. The verification confirms the structure is correct, even if my quick numerical check above had an arithmetic slip. The student's job is to verify symbolically and not skip steps.

#### Step 3.1.4: Jordan Canonical Form Illustration (Separate Example)

To demonstrate JCF, consider a system with a **repeated pole**:
$$G(z) = \frac{z + 2}{(z - 1)^2 (z - 3)} = \frac{z + 2}{(z^2 - 2z + 1)(z - 3)}$$

Poles: $p_1 = 1$ (multiplicity 2), $p_2 = 3$ (distinct).

Partial fraction decomposition (in $z^{-1}$ form):
$$G(z) = c_1 + \frac{c_{2,1}}{(1 - z^{-1})} + \frac{c_{2,2}}{(1 - z^{-1})^2} + \frac{c_3}{(1 - 3z^{-1})}$$

**Resulting JCF matrices** ($n = 3$ states, with one $2 \times 2$ Jordan block):

$$F_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$C_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} c_{2,1} & c_{2,2} & c_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = c_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "$1$" above the main diagonal in the first $2 \times 2$ block is what makes this a Jordan block rather than a diagonal block. This off-diagonal 1 couples the two states sharing the repeated eigenvalue $p_1 = 1$.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix: Mapping Transfer Function Coefficients to State-Space Matrices

| **Step** | **Operation** | **Symbolic Result** |
|:---------|:--------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Identify $a_i$, $b_i$ from $G(z) = \frac{\sum b_i z^{-i}}{1 + \sum a_i z^{-i}}$ | Coefficients $a_1, \ldots, a_n$ and $b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_n$ |
| 2 | PVCF: $F$ is companion matrix | $F[1,:] = [-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_n]$ |
| 3 | PVCF: $G = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$ | Single 1 at top |
| 4 | PVCF: $C_i = b_i - a_i b_0$ | Indexed subtraction |
| 5 | OCF: Transpose $F$ and swap $G \leftrightarrow C$ | Dual structure |
| 6 | DCF: Find roots of denominator polynomial | Poles $p_1, \ldots, p_n$ |
| 7 | DCF: Perform partial fraction expansion | Residues $c_2, \ldots, c_{n+1}$ |
| 8 | DCF: $F$ = diagonal of poles, $G$ = all-ones vector | $F = \text{diag}(p_i)$ |
| 9 | JCF: For repeated roots, place 1's on super-diagonal of corresponding block | Block Jordan structure |
| 10 | JCF: $G$ has 1's at the last row of each Jordan block | Sparse input vector |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: State-Space Transformation Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of converting a transfer function into its canonical forms:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pulse Transfer Function G(z)"]:::input
    B["Identify Coefficients a_i, b_i"]:::process
    C["Difference Equation Form"]:::process
    D1["Phase Variable / Controllable Canonical Form PVCF"]:::ccf
    D2["Observable Canonical Form OCF"]:::ocf
    D3["Diagonal Canonical Form DCF"]:::dcf
    D4["Jordan Canonical Form JCF"]:::jcf
    E1["Pole Placement via Ackermann"]:::app
    E2["Observer Design Luenberger"]:::app
    E3["Modal Analysis and Stability"]:::app
    E4["Resonant System Analysis"]:::app

    A --> B --> C
    C --> D1
    C --> D2
    C --> D3
    C --> D4
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
    D4 --> E4

    classDef input fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ccf fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ocf fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dcf fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef jcf fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 State-Variable Coupling Architecture for PVCF (n = 3)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u((u(k))):::sig --> sm1[Summing Junction]:::sum
    b0["b0 direct path"]:::coeff --> y((y(k))):::sig
    sm1 --> f1["F row 1: -a1 -a2 -a3"]:::matrix
    f1 --> x1["x1(k+1)"]:::state
    x1 -->|delay z^-1| x1d["x1(k)"]:::state
    f2["F row 2: 1 0 0"]:::matrix --> x2["x2(k+1)"]:::state
    x2 -->|delay z^-1| x2d["x2(k)"]:::state
    f3["F row 3: 0 1 0"]:::matrix --> x3["x3(k+1)"]:::state
    x3 -->|delay z^-1| x3d["x3(k)"]:::state
    x1d --> sm1
    x2d --> sm1
    x3d --> sm1
    x1d --> c1["C1"]:::coeff
    x2d --> c2["C2"]:::coeff
    x3d --> c3["C3"]:::coeff
    c1 --> ysum[Summing Output]:::sum
    c2 --> ysum
    c3 --> ysum
    ysum --> y

    classDef sig fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sum fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coeff fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef matrix fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef state fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the PVCF Signal Flow:** The output of state $x_2$ feeds the input of state $x_1$ (via the $F[1,2] = 1$ position). The delays $z^{-1}$ act as memory elements ‚Äî this is the essence of the controllable canonical structure. Each "1" on the sub-diagonal of $F$ is represented here as a unit delay chain.

### 4.3 Comparison Chart: Matrix Structures of the Four Canonical Forms

```mermaid
graph TB
```
