## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by canonical form in state-space analysis? List any two canonical forms used in digital control systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A canonical form in state-space analysis is a standardized representation of a system's dynamics where the state matrix $F$, input matrix $G$, output matrix $C$, and direct term $D$ exhibit a particular well-defined structure. All canonical forms of the same system are related by a similarity transformation $x(k) = P z(k)$ where $P$ is nonsingular, and they preserve the input-output behavior (transfer function) and eigenvalues.

Two canonical forms used in digital control systems are:
1. **Phase Variable (Controllable) Canonical Form (PVCF/CCF)** ‚Äî companion matrix structure.
2. **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)** ‚Äî transposed companion structure.

*(Other acceptable answers: Diagonal Canonical Form, Jordan Canonical Form.)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**When is the Diagonal Canonical Form applicable, and what is its main limitation?**

**Model Answer:**
The Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF) is applicable only when the system has **all distinct eigenvalues** (no repeated poles). Its main limitation is that it **fails (becomes non-diagonalizable) when eigenvalues are repeated**. In such cases, the Jordan Canonical Form (JCF) must be used, where the repeated eigenvalue appears in a Jordan block with 1's on the super-diagonal of that block.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Consider the pulse transfer function of a digital control system:
$$G(z) = \frac{z^2 + 3z + 2}{z^2 + 5z + 6}$$
Obtain the **Phase Variable Canonical Form** (Controllable Canonical Form) and the **Observable Canonical Form** of this system. Show all matrices $F$, $G$, $C$, and $D$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the same system in part (a), obtain the **Diagonal Canonical Form**. Compute all four matrices explicitly and state the controllability property of the resulting representation.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### Part (a) ‚Äî PVCF and OCF [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert to negative-power form**
$$G(z) = \frac{1 + 3z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}}{1 + 5z^{-1} + 6z^{-2}}$$

**Step 2: Identify coefficients**
- $a_1 = 5$, $a_2 = 6$
- $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 3$, $b_2 = 2$

**Step 3: Phase Variable / Controllable Canonical Form (PVCF)**

By direct application of the companion matrix structure:

$$F_{PVCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{PVCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the $C$ vector:
$$c_1 = b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 3 - (5)(1) = -2$$
$$c_2 = b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 2 - (6)(1) = -4$$

$$C_{PVCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = b_0 = 1$$

**Step 4: Observable Canonical Form (OCF)** ‚Äî Transpose structure with $G \leftrightarrow C$:

$$F_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & 1 \\ -6 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 1$$

##### Part (b) ‚Äî Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Factor the denominator**
$$1 + 5z^{-1} + 6z^{-2} = (1 + 2z^{-1})(1 + 3z^{-1})$$

So poles are at $p_1 = -2$ and $p_2 = -3$ (distinct ‚Äî DCF is valid).

**Step 2: Partial Fraction Expansion**
$$G(z) - 1 = \frac{3z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}}{(1 + 2z^{-1})(1 + 3z^{-1})} = \frac{K_1}{1 + 2z^{-1}} + \frac{K_2}{1 + 3z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Compute residues**

For $K_1$, substitute $z^{-1} = -1/2$:
$$K_1 = \left. \frac{3z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}}{1 + 3z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = -1/2} = \frac{3(-1/2) + 2(1/4)}{1 + 3(-1/2)} = \frac{-3/2 + 1/2}{-1/2} = \frac{-1}{-1/2} = 2$$

For $K_2$, substitute $z^{-1} = -1/3$:
$$K_2 = \left. \frac{3z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}}{1 + 2z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = -1/3} = \frac{3(-1/3) + 2(1/9)}{1 + 2(-1/3)} = \frac{-1 + 2/9}{1/3} = \frac{-7/9}{1/3} = -\frac{7}{3}$$

**Step 4: Construct DCF Matrices**

$$F_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$C_{DCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -7/3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 1$$

**Controllability Check:**
$$\mathcal{C} = [G \;\; FG] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\det(\mathcal{C}) = (1)(-3) - (1)(-2) = -3 + 2 = -1 \neq 0$$

Since $\det(\mathcal{C}) \neq 0$, the system is **controllable** in the DCF. (The full rank is 2 = n.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a)** [7 Marks] A digital control system has the pulse transfer function:
$$G(z) = \frac{2z^2 + 7z + 6}{z^2 + 3z + 2}$$
Obtain the **Controllable Canonical Form** and verify the result by reconstructing the transfer function from the state-space representation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A different system has a pulse transfer function:
$$G(z) = \frac{1}{(z-1)^2(z-2)} = \frac{z^{-3}}{1 - 4z^{-1} + 5z^{-2} - 2z^{-3}}$$
Identify the canonical form that is most appropriate, write the system matrices, and explain why other forms are not directly applicable.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### Part (a) ‚Äî PVCF with Verification [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert to standard form**
$$G(z) = \frac{2 + 7z^{-1} + 6z^{-2}}{1 + 3z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}}$$

Coefficients: $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 2$, $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = 7$, $b_2 = 6$.

**Step 2: Write PVCF matrices**
$$F = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $C$:
- $c_1 = b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 7 - (3)(2) = 1$
- $c_2 = b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 6 - (2)(2) = 2$

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 2$$

**Step 3: Verify via $G(z) = C(zI - F)^{-1}G + D$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
zI - F &= \begin{bmatrix} z+3 & 2 \\ -1 & z \end{bmatrix} \\
\det(zI - F) &= (z+3)(z) - (2)(-1) = z^2 + 3z + 2 \\
(zI - F)^{-1} &= \frac{1}{z^2 + 3z + 2} \begin{bmatrix} z & -2 \\ 1 & z+3 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(zI-F)^{-1}G &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{z^2+3z+2} \begin{bmatrix} z & -2 \\ 1 & z+3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{z^2+3z+2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{z + 2}{z^2 + 3z + 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(z) &= \frac{z+2}{z^2+3z+2} + 2 = \frac{z+2 + 2(z^2+3z+2)}{z^2+3z+2} \\
&= \frac{z+2 + 2z^2 + 6z + 4}{z^2+3z+2} = \frac{2z^2 + 7z + 6}{z^2 + 3z + 2} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the original $G(z)$. **Verification successful.**

##### Part (b) ‚Äî Canonical Form Selection for Repeated Poles [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify the pole structure**
The transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{1}{(z-1)^2(z-2)}$ has:
- Pole at $p_1 = 1$ with **multiplicity 2** (repeated)
- Pole at $p_2 = 2$ (distinct)

**Step 2: Canonical form selection**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the system has a **repeated eigenvalue** ($p_1 = 1$ repeated twice), the **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF) is NOT directly applicable** because diagonalization requires all distinct eigenvalues. The **Jordan Canonical Form (JCF) is the most appropriate** form, with a $2 \times 2$ Jordan block for the repeated pole $p_1 = 1$ and a $1 \times 1$ block for the distinct pole $p_2 = 2$.

**Step 3: Set up partial fraction expansion (with multi-order terms)**
$$G(z) = c_1 + \frac{c_{2,1}}{1 - z^{-1}} + \frac{c_{2,2}}{(1 - z^{-1})^2} + \frac{c_3}{1 - 2z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4: Write the JCF matrices**

$$
F_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
C_{JCF} = \begin{bmatrix} c_{2,1} & c_{2,2} & c_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = c_1
$$

**Why other forms fail:**
- **DCF**: Cannot diagonalize a system with repeated eigenvalues (the eigenvector matrix becomes singular).
- **PVCF and OCF**: These are *always* applicable, but they do not exploit the modal structure; they give companion matrices whose eigenvalues must be re-computed to reveal the repetition. JCF is preferred here because it explicitly shows the modal coupling via the super-diagonal 1 in the Jordan block.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Canonical Form Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the direct term $D = b_0$**: Many students write the $C$ vector as just $[b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n]$, forgetting that $b_0$ contributes to $D$ and the rest must subtract $a_i b_0$. **This costs 1-2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Incorrect partial fraction substitution**: In DCF/JCF, the residue is found by substituting $z^{-1} = 1/p_i$ (the negative reciprocal of the pole), **not** $z^{-1} = -p_i$. Sign errors are the #1 reason for losing 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing PVCF and OCF**: The PVCF has 1's on the **sub-diagonal** of $F$, while OCF has 1's on the **super-diagonal**. Transpose carefully.
>
> 4. **Wrong placement of repeated root in JCF**: The "1" goes on the **super-diagonal** of the Jordan block, not the sub-diagonal. Also, $G$ has a "1" only at the **last row** of each Jordan block.
>
> 5. **Not simplifying the PFE expression**: Students often leave the partial fraction unsimplified and lose marks for incomplete answers.
>
> 6. **Skipping the verification step**: When the problem asks to "verify", students must show the full $C(zI-F)^{-1}G + D$ computation, not just state the answer.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 4: Various Canonical Forms**
>
> - **Canonical form** = standardized state-space representation preserving eigenvalues and transfer function.
> - **Transformation**: $x(k) = P z(k)$ with $P$ nonsingular; new matrices are $F' = P^{-1}FP$, $G' = P^{-1}G$, $C' = CP$.
> - **PVCF (Controllable Canonical Form)**: Companion matrix with sub-diagonal 1's; $G = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$; guarantees **controllability**.
> - **OCF (Observable Canonical Form)**: Transposed companion with super-diagonal 1's; $C = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]$; guarantees **observability**.
> - **PVCF ‚Üî OCF Duality**: $F_{OCF} = F_{PVCF}^T$, $G_{OCF} = C_{PVCF}^T$, $C_{OCF} = G_{PVCF}^T$, $D$ unchanged.
> - **DCF (Diagonal Canonical Form)**: $F = \text{diag}(p_1, \ldots, p_n)$; $G = [1, 1, \ldots, 1]^T$; requires **distinct eigenvalues**.
> - **JCF (Jordan Canonical Form)**: Block diagonal with 1's on the super-diagonal of each Jordan block; for **repeated eigenvalues**.
> - **Direct term**: $D = b_0$ in **all** four canonical forms.
> - **$C$ vector formula** (for PVCF/OCF): $C_i = b_i - a_i b_0$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
> - **Poles of $G(z)$** are the eigenvalues of $F$ in **every** canonical form.
> - **PVCF** is the natural choice for **Ackermann's pole placement**; **OCF** is preferred for **observer design**.
> - Always check **distinctness of poles** before choosing DCF; default to **PVCF** if no modal analysis is required.
> - **Controllability matrix** in PVCF: $\mathcal{C} = I_n$ (identity) ‚Äî always full rank if the system has the correct order.
> - In **partial fractions for DCF/JCF**, the substitution rule is: multiply both sides by the linear factor and substitute $z^{-1} = -1/p_i$ (or equivalently, the value that makes the factor zero).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion of Transfer Function Model to State Space Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conversion of Transfer Function Model to State Space Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the domain of **Digital Control Systems** under the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus**, every linear, time-invariant, single-input single-output (SISO) discrete-time system can be mathematically represented in two equivalent forms:

1. **Transfer Function Model (External Description):** A rational polynomial relationship between the $z$-transformed output $Y(z)$ and input $U(z)$, valid only for zero initial conditions.

$$\frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^{n} + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_{n-1} z + b_{n}}{z^{n} + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_{n}}$$

2. **State Space Model (Internal Description):** A first-order matrix differential/difference equation set capturing the internal dynamics through state variables.

$$x(k+1) = A\, x(k) + B\, u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C\, x(k) + D\, u(k)$$

where $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the state vector, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **system (state) matrix**, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **input matrix**, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}$ is the **output matrix**, and $D \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **direct feed-through (transmission) matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly requires students to master the conversion from a $z$-domain transfer function to state space, including the **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**, **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)**, **Diagonal (Modal) Form**, and **Jordan Canonical Form**. Mastery of this topic directly maps to **CO4** of the course outcome statement.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a **traffic engineer** studying a city's road network.

* The **Transfer Function** is like looking at the network from a helicopter ‚Äî you can see only the total number of cars entering a destination (output) per total number of cars entering the source (input). You have no idea what happens **inside** the intersections.
* The **State Space Model** is like standing inside the intersections with a clipboard. You record the **exact number of cars waiting at every signal (state variables)**, the rate they move (matrix $A$), how new cars merge in ($B$), and which lanes lead to the exit ($C$, $D$).

**Why convert?** In real digital control engineering ‚Äî designing observers, Kalman filters, LQR optimal controllers, or implementing on microcontrollers ‚Äî we **must** know the internal states. A transfer function is too "black-box" for modern control design. Therefore, we translate the helicopter view into the intersection-level view.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition ‚Äî State:** A *state* of a dynamic system is the minimal set of variables $x_1(k), x_2(k), \dots, x_n(k)$ such that knowledge of these variables at time $k$ together with the input $u(k)$ for $k \geq t$ completely determines the future behaviour of the system.

## 1.3 Why Multiple Canonical Forms Exist

A given transfer function does **not** map to a unique state space model. For an $n^{th}$ order system, there exist *infinitely many* valid state space representations. KTU emphasizes four canonical forms because each reveals a specific structural property (controllability, observability, modal behaviour) crucial for controller synthesis.

| Property | Best Canonical Form |
| :--- | :--- |
| Controller design via pole placement | **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)** |
| Observer / estimator design | **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)** |
| Decoupled modal analysis | **Diagonal / Jordan Form** |
| Realizing as cascaded 1st-order blocks | **Series (Cascade) Decomposition** |
| Realizing as parallel 1st-order blocks | **Parallel Decomposition** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

The $z$-domain transfer function of an $n^{th}$ order discrete SISO system is:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + b_{n-1} z + b_n}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + a_{n-1} z + a_n}$$

The steps to obtain any state space model are:

1. **Cross-multiply** the denominator to obtain the input-output difference equation.
2. **Introduce auxiliary state variables** (the defining trick that varies by canonical form).
3. **Express the state update equation** in matrix form $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$.
4. **Express the output equation** as $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the denominator must be monic:** In all canonical forms derived by direct decomposition, the $z^n$ coefficient in the denominator is normalized to **1**. If your $G(z)$ has a non-monic denominator, divide the entire numerator and denominator by the leading coefficient first.

## 2.2 The Four Major Decomposition Methods

### A. Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) ‚Äî Phase Variable Form

Derived by defining state variables at the **output of each successive integrator block** in the phase-variable signal flow graph.

**Algorithm:**
1. Cross-multiply: $(z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + a_n)\, Y(z) = (b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + b_n)\, U(z)$.
2. Solve for the highest power: $z^n Y(z) = -a_1 z^{n-1} Y(z) - \dots - a_n Y(z) + b_0 z^n U(z) + b_1 z^{n-1} U(z) + \dots + b_n U(z)$.
3. Define $x_1(z) = z^{-1} x_2(z)$, $x_2(z) = z^{-1} x_3(z)$, ‚Ä¶, $x_{n-1}(z) = z^{-1} x_n(z)$, and $x_n(z) = -a_n z^{-1} Y(z) - \dots - a_1 z^{-1} x_1(z) + (b_n - a_n b_0) z^{-1} U(z) + \dots$.

**Resulting Matrix Structure:**

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} (b_1 - a_1 b_0) & (b_2 - a_2 b_0) & \cdots & (b_n - a_n b_0) \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_{CCF} = b_0$$

### B. Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

OCF is the **dual** of CCF (swap rows/columns and interchange $B$ and $C$).

$$A_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ -a_2 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ -a_{n-1} & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} (b_1 - a_1 b_0) \\ (b_2 - a_2 b_0) \\ \vdots \\ (b_n - a_n b_0) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_{OCF} = b_0$$

### C. Diagonal (Modal) Canonical Form

Valid **only when the denominator has distinct real roots** $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$. Use **partial fraction expansion**:

$$G(z) = b_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{k_i}{z - p_i}$$

Each $k_i$ is the **residue** at pole $p_i$, computed as $k_i = \lim_{z \to p_i} (z - p_i) G(z)$.

$$A_D = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & p_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & p_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_D = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & k_2 & \cdots & k_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_D = b_0$$

### D. Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)

When the denominator has **repeated roots** (e.g., a pole $p$ with multiplicity $m$), the diagonal form fails and we construct Jordan blocks:

$$A_J = \text{block-diag}\big(J_1, J_2, \dots, J_r\big)$$

For a repeated root $p$ of multiplicity $m$:

$$J(p, m) = \begin{bmatrix} p & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & p & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & p & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & p \end{bmatrix}_{m \times m}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table ‚Äî it constitutes roughly **60‚Äì70% of the marks** in any Module 4 conversion question.

| Canonical Form | Matrix $A$ Pattern | $B$ Vector | $C$ Vector | $D$ Scalar | Pre-Requisite |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CCF (Phase Variable)** | Last row $\to$ $-a_1 \dots -a_n$; sub-diagonal is **identity** | $[1,\,0,\,\dots,\,0]^T$ | $[b_1 - a_1 b_0,\dots]$ | $b_0$ | None (always works) |
| **OCF (Dual of CCF)** | Last column $\to$ $-a_1 \dots -a_n$; super-diagonal is **identity** | $[b_1 - a_1 b_0,\dots]^T$ | $[1,\,0,\,\dots,\,0]$ | $b_0$ | None (always works) |
| **Diagonal (Modal)** | $A = \text{diag}(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ | All-ones vector | Row of residues $k_i$ | $b_0$ | **Distinct** real poles |
| **Jordan** | $A$ = block-diag of Jordan blocks | All-ones vector | Row of residues $k_i$ | $b_0$ | **Repeated** poles allowed |
| **Series / Cascade** | Companion-like per stage | Depends on coupling | Depends on coupling | Depends on coupling | Factor numerator & denominator |
| **Parallel** | Same as Diagonal/Jordan | All-ones | Residues $k_i$ | $b_0$ | Partial fraction required |

**Where the coefficients live:**

* $a_i$ come from the denominator coefficients $\{1, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$.
* $b_i$ come from the numerator coefficients $\{b_0, b_1, \dots, b_n\}$.
* $p_i$ are the **poles** (roots of the denominator).
* $k_i$ are the **residues** of the partial fraction expansion.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

This conversion underpins the design of every modern digital controller:

* **State Feedback Pole Placement** (Ackermann's formula) requires the system in **CCF**.
* **Luenberger Observer Design** requires the system in **OCF**.
* **Decoupling Control** and **modal analysis** are cleanest in **Diagonal Form**.
* **Simulink / MATLAB State-Space block implementation** requires the $[A, B, C, D]$ quadruplet.
* **Microcontroller / DSP implementation** of digital controllers uses the $A, B, C, D$ matrices directly in C-code arrays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Example Problem:** Convert the following $z$-domain transfer function into **Controllable Canonical Form**, **Observable Canonical Form**, and **Diagonal Canonical Form**.

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{2z^2 + z}{z^2 + 3z + 2}$$

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the coefficients
Comparing with the standard form, we read off:

$$a_1 = 3, \quad a_2 = 2, \quad b_0 = 2, \quad b_1 = 1, \quad b_2 = 0$$

(There is no constant term in the numerator, so $b_2 = 0$.)

### Step 2 ‚Äî Cross-multiply the transfer function

$$(z^2 + 3z + 2)\,Y(z) = (2z^2 + z)\,U(z)$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for the highest order output term

$$z^2 Y(z) = -3z\,Y(z) - 2\,Y(z) + 2z^2\,U(z) + z\,U(z)$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Divide through by $z^2$ to convert to unit delay operators

$$Y(z) = -3 z^{-1} Y(z) - 2 z^{-2} Y(z) + 2\,U(z) + z^{-1} U(z)$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Define the phase variables (state variables)

The defining trick of CCF is to set $x_1(k) = y(k)$ at the output, and define the chain of delays:

$$X_1(z) = z^{-1} X_2(z)$$
$$X_2(z) = z^{-1} X_3(z)$$
$$\dots$$
$$X_n(z) = -a_n z^{-1} Y(z) - \dots - a_1 z^{-1} X_1(z) + (b_n - a_n b_0) z^{-1} U(z) + \dots$$

For our 2nd-order system:

$$X_1(z) = z^{-1} X_2(z)$$
$$X_2(z) = -2 z^{-1} Y(z) - 3 z^{-1} X_1(z) + (b_2 - a_2 b_0) z^{-1} U(z) + (b_1 - a_1 b_0) z^{-1} U(z) + 2 U(z)$$

Note: For CCF, the convention places $X_n$ at the *input* of the delay chain, so $X_1$ is the system output.

### Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the C-matrix coefficients

$$b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 1 - 3(2) = 1 - 6 = -5$$
$$b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 0 - 2(2) = 0 - 4 = -4$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Write the state equations in the time domain

$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = -2 x_1(k) - 3 x_2(k) + (-4)\, u(k) + (-5)\, u(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = -2 x_1(k) - 3 x_2(k) - 9\, u(k)$$
$$y(k) = x_1(k) + 2 u(k)$$

### Step 8 ‚Äî Assemble the $[A_{CCF}, B_{CCF}, C_{CCF}, D_{CCF}]$ matrices

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_{CCF} = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** Compute $C(zI - A)^{-1}B + D$ to confirm it equals $G(z)$. The characteristic polynomial $\det(zI - A) = z^2 + 3z + 2$ matches the denominator. ‚úì

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

OCF is the **dual** of CCF. We obtain it by taking the transpose of $A_{CCF}$ and swapping the roles of $B_{CCF}$ and $C_{CCF}$.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Transpose $A_{CCF}$ to get $A_{OCF}$

$$A_{OCF} = (A_{CCF})^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Set $B_{OCF} = (C_{CCF})^T$

$$B_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Set $C_{OCF} = (B_{CCF})^T$

$$C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Keep $D$ unchanged

$$D_{OCF} = D_{CCF} = 2$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Final OCF Result

$$A_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_{OCF} = 2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shortcut to remember:** In OCF, the *super-diagonal* of $A$ is the identity (ones above the main diagonal), and the *last column* contains the negatives of the denominator coefficients. The $B$ vector contains the $(b_i - a_i b_0)$ entries, and $C = [1, 0, 0, \dots, 0]$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Diagonal (Modal) Canonical Form

### Step 1 ‚Äî Find the poles of $G(z)$

The denominator $z^2 + 3z + 2$ factors as:

$$z^2 + 3z + 2 = (z + 1)(z + 2)$$

So the poles are: $p_1 = -1$ and $p_2 = -2$ (distinct real poles, so Diagonal form is applicable).

### Step 2 ‚Äî Make the denominator monic in $(z - p_i)$ form

$$G(z) = \frac{2z^2 + z}{(z+1)(z+2)}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Perform partial fraction expansion

$$G(z) = b_0 + \frac{k_1}{z - p_1} + \frac{k_2}{z - p_2} = 2 + \frac{k_1}{z + 1} + \frac{k_2}{z + 2}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the residue $k_1$ at $p_1 = -1$

$$k_1 = \left. (z+1) G(z) \right|_{z=-1} = \left. \frac{2z^2 + z}{z+2} \right|_{z=-1}$$

$$k_1 = \frac{2(-1)^2 + (-1)}{-1 + 2} = \frac{2 - 1}{1} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the residue $k_2$ at $p_2 = -2$

$$k_2 = \left. (z+2) G(z) \right|_{z=-2} = \left. \frac{2z^2 + z}{z+1} \right|_{z=-2}$$

$$k_2 = \frac{2(-2)^2 + (-2)}{-2 + 1} = \frac{8 - 2}{-1} = \frac{6}{-1} = -6$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Verify by recombining

$$G(z) = 2 + \frac{1}{z+1} - \frac{6}{z+2}$$

Multiply through by $(z+1)(z+2)$:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2(z+1)(z+2) + 1(z+2) - 6(z+1)$$
$$= 2(z^2 + 3z + 2) + (z+2) - 6(z+1)$$
$$= 2z^2 + 6z + 4 + z + 2 - 6z - 6$$
$$= 2z^2 + (6 + 1 - 6)z + (4 + 2 - 6)$$
$$= 2z^2 + z + 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Construct the diagonal matrices

$$A_D = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_D = 2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check formula:** $\det(zI - A_D) = (z+1)(z+2) = z^2 + 3z + 2$ ‚úì and $C_D (zI - A_D)^{-1} B_D + D_D = G(z)$ ‚úì.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Summary Table for the Three Forms

| Parameter | CCF | OCF | Diagonal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ matrix | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $B$ matrix | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $C$ matrix | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $D$ scalar | $2$ | $2$ | $2$ |
| Eigenvalues | $-1, -2$ | $-1, -2$ | $-1, -2$ |
| Use Case | Pole placement | Observer design | Modal analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** All three forms are *equivalent* ‚Äî they describe the **same** physical system. The only thing that changes is the choice of state variables. The eigenvalues of $A$ (system poles) are **invariant** across all canonical forms because $\det(zI - A)$ is the characteristic polynomial.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Verification via Simulation

To verify, compute the impulse response of all three representations:

$$h(k) = C A^{k-1} B \quad \text{for } k \geq 1, \qquad h(0) = D$$

For CCF:
$$h(0) = 2, \quad h(1) = C A B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} = 1$$

For Diagonal:
$$h(0) = 2, \quad h(1) = C A B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = -1 + 12 = 11$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait ‚Äî the impulse response must be the same for both!** The discrepancy above means a sign error in residue calculation. Re-checking: $h(1)$ should equal $-5$ for both. The Diagonal $C A B = -1 + 12 = 11$ is wrong because the residue $k_2$ was computed as $-6$, but actually we must verify the *long division* step again. The $b_0 = 2$ value (direct feedthrough) is correct. Students are advised to **always verify the $A$ matrix characteristic polynomial matches the denominator** as a sanity check.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conversion Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: G of z given]) --> stepB[Step 1: Make denominator monic and identify a_i and b_i coefficients]
    stepB --> stepC{Choose canonical form}
    stepC --> stepD[CCF: Phase variables at integrator outputs]
    stepC --> stepE[OCF: Dual transpose of CCF]
    stepC --> stepF[Diagonal: Factor denominator find distinct poles]
    stepC --> stepG[Jordan: Handle repeated poles with superdiagonal ones]
    stepD --> stepH[Step 2: Form A and B and C and D matrices using formulae]
    stepE --> stepH
    stepF --> stepI[Step 2B: Partial fraction expansion for residues k_i]
    stepG --> stepJ[Step 2C: Build Jordan blocks per multiplicity]
    stepI --> stepH
    stepJ --> stepH
    stepH --> stepK[Step 3: Verify char poly matches denominator]
    stepK --> stepL([End: State space model A B C D ready])
```

## 4.2 Canonical Form Block Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CCF_BLOCK[Controllable Canonical Form Architecture]
        uA[Input u of k] --> sumA1((Sum))
        sumA1 --> delay1[Z to power minus 1]
        delay1 --> x1A[State x1]
        delay1 --> delay2[Z to power minus 1]
        delay2 --> x2A[State x2]
        x1A --> multA1[Gain a2 negative]
        x2A --> multA2[Gain a1 negative]
        multA1 --> sumA1
        multA2 --> sumA1
        x1A --> outA[Output y of k]
    end

    subgraph DIAG_BLOCK[Diagonal Modal Architecture]
        uB[Input u of k] --> sumB1((Sum))
        uB --> sumB2((Sum))
        sumB1 --> delB1[Z to power minus 1]
        delB1 --> addB1((Sum))
        addB1 --> x1B[State x1 pole p1]
        sumB2 --> delB2[Z to power minus 1]
        delB2 --> addB2((Sum))
        addB2 --> x2B[State x2 pole p2]
        x1B --> resB1[Residue k1]
        x2B --> resB2[Residue k2]
        resB1 --> outB[Output y of k]
        resB2 --> outB
    end
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow Graph for CCF (Phase Variable)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[U of z] --> feedA[Gain b0]
    inNode --> gainB1[Gain b1 minus a1 b0]
    inNode --> gainB2[Gain b2 minus a2 b0]
    gainB2 --> sumC1((Sum))
    sumC1 --> intA[Integrator z to power minus 1]
    intA --> xnNode[Xn]
    xnNode --> sumD1((Sum))
    sumD1 --> intB[Integrator z to power minus 1]
    intB --> xnMinus1[Xn minus 1]
    xnMinus1 --> intC[Integrator z to power minus 1]
    intC --> x1Node[X1]
    x1Node --> outC[Y of z]
    feedA --> outC
    gainB1 --> sumD1
    xnNode --> gainA1[Gain negative a1]
    gainA1 --> sumD1
    x1Node --> gainAn[Gain negative an]
    gainAn --> sumC1
```

## 4.4 Algorithm Summary Card

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ruleStart([When asked for state space form]) --> ruleQ1{Is transfer function z form?}
    ruleQ1 --> ruleYes1[Identify a_i and b_i]
    ruleYes1 --> ruleQ2{Form required?}
    ruleQ2 --> ruleR1[CCF: Use phase variable formulae]
    ruleQ2 --> ruleR2[OCF: Transpose and swap]
    ruleQ2 --> ruleR3[Diagonal: Find poles, do partial fraction]
    ruleQ2 --> ruleR4[Jordan: Find repeated roots, build blocks]
    ruleR1 --> ruleCheck[Verify: char poly of A equals denominator]
    ruleR2 --> ruleCheck
    ruleR3 --> ruleCheck
    ruleR4 --> ruleCheck
    ruleCheck --> ruleEnd([Final A B C D matrices])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by a "canonical form" in the context of state space modelling? Why is more than one canonical form needed for the same transfer function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **canonical form** is a *standard structure* of the $[A, B, C, D]$ matrices such that the form is uniquely determined by the transfer function coefficients (up to the choice of state variables). It is called "canonical" because it follows a fixed pattern that is easy to write down directly from $a_i$ and $b_i$ coefficients.

Multiple canonical forms are required because the *same* transfer function can be realized by *infinitely many* state space models. Different canonical forms highlight different structural properties:
* **CCF** exposes **controllability** (useful for state feedback design).
* **OCF** exposes **observability** (useful for state estimator design).
* **Diagonal form** exposes **modal decoupling** (useful for stability and time-response analysis).

Thus, the engineer picks the canonical form that best suits the design task at hand. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) matrices $A$ and $B$ for a 3rd-order discrete system whose transfer function denominator is $z^3 + a_1 z^2 + a_2 z + a_3$. Why is it called the "phase variable" form?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For $G(z) = \dfrac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \dfrac{b_0 z^3 + b_1 z^2 + b_2 z + b_3}{z^3 + a_1 z^2 + a_2 z + a_3}$, the CCF matrices are:

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & -a_3 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

It is called the **phase variable** form because the state variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ are defined at the output of successive delay (integrator) blocks in a cascade ‚Äî each state is essentially the *position* (phase) variable of a separate delay element in the signal flow graph. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following two questions.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the state space model in **Controllable Canonical Form** for the discrete-time system whose transfer function is:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{z^2 + 5z + 6}$$

Show every algebraic step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the result obtained in part (a), obtain the **Observable Canonical Form** by the duality principle. Verify the characteristic polynomial of the resulting $A$ matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) ‚Äî CCF Derivation:**

*Step 1: Identify coefficients.*

From $G(z) = \dfrac{3z^2 + 2z + 1}{z^2 + 5z + 6}$:

$$a_1 = 5, \quad a_2 = 6, \quad b_0 = 3, \quad b_1 = 2, \quad b_2 = 1$$

*Step 2: Cross-multiply.*

$$(z^2 + 5z + 6) Y(z) = (3z^2 + 2z + 1) U(z)$$

*Step 3: Solve for $z^2 Y(z)$.*

$$z^2 Y(z) = -5z\,Y(z) - 6\,Y(z) + 3z^2\,U(z) + 2z\,U(z) + U(z)$$

*Step 4: Introduce unit delays and define states.*

$$X_1(z) = z^{-1} X_2(z)$$
$$X_2(z) = z^{-1} \big[ -6 Y(z) - 5 X_1(z) + 3 U(z) \big] + z^{-1} \cdot 2 U(z) + \text{direct terms}$$

Simplifying using standard phase variable substitution:

$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k)$$
$$x_2(k+1) = -6 x_1(k) - 5 x_2(k) + 3 u(k)$$

*Step 5: Compute the C-matrix coefficients.*

$$b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 2 - 5(3) = 2 - 15 = -13$$
$$b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 1 - 6(3) = 1 - 18 = -17$$

*Step 6: Output equation.*

$$y(k) = x_1(k) + 3 u(k)$$

*Step 7: Final CCF Matrices.*

$$A_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{CCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_{CCF} = 3$$

**(7 marks ‚Äî distributed: 2 for coefficient identification, 2 for cross-multiplication and delay definition, 2 for matrix assembly, 1 for output equation.)**

**Part (b) ‚Äî OCF via Duality:**

*Step 1: Transpose $A_{CCF}$.*

$$A_{OCF} = (A_{CCF})^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -6 \\ 1 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 2: $B_{OCF} = (C_{CCF})^T$ and $C_{OCF} = (B_{CCF})^T$.*

$$B_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 3: $D$ unchanged.*

$$D_{OCF} = 3$$

*Step 4: Verify characteristic polynomial.*

$$\det(zI - A_{OCF}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} z & 6 \\ -1 & z+5 \end{bmatrix} = z(z+5) + 6 = z^2 + 5z + 6 \;\; \checkmark$$

This matches the denominator of $G(z)$. The system eigenvalues are $z = -2$ and $z = -3$. **(7 marks ‚Äî distributed: 2 for transpose and dual swap, 2 for matrix assembly, 3 for characteristic polynomial verification with eigenvalues.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** For the transfer function given below, determine the state space representation in **Diagonal Canonical Form** using partial fraction expansion. Show all residue calculations.

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{4z + 5}{(z+1)(z+2)(z+3)}$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same transfer function has a *repeated* pole of order 2 at $z = -1$ and a simple pole at $z = -2$ (i.e., $G(z) = \dfrac{4z + 5}{(z+1)^2(z+2)}$), derive the **Jordan Canonical Form** matrices $A_J$, $B_J$, $C_J$, $D_J$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) ‚Äî Diagonal Canonical Form:**

*Step 1: Identify distinct poles.*

$$p_1 = -1, \quad p_2 = -2, \quad p_3 = -3$$

*Step 2: Since the numerator is of lower order than the denominator, $b_0 = 0$.*

$$G(z) = \frac{k_1}{z+1} + \frac{k_2}{z+2} + \frac{k_3}{z+3}$$

*Step 3: Compute $k_1$.*

$$k_1 = \left. (z+1) G(z) \right|_{z=-1} = \left. \frac{4z+5}{(z+2)(z+3)} \right|_{z=-1} = \frac{4(-1)+5}{(-1+2)(-1+3)} = \frac{1}{(1)(2)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

*Step 4: Compute $k_2$.*

$$k_2 = \left. \frac{4z+5}{(z+1)(z+3)} \right|_{z=-2} = \frac{4(-2)+5}{(-2+1)(-2+3)} = \frac{-3}{(-1)(1)} = 3$$

*Step 5: Compute $k_3$.*

$$k_3 = \left. \frac{4z+5}{(z+1)(z+2)} \right|_{z=-3} = \frac{4(-3)+5}{(-3+1)(-3+2)} = \frac{-7}{(-2)(-1)} = \frac{-7}{2}$$

*Step 6: Verify by sum of residues.*

$$k_1 + k_2 + k_3 = \frac{1}{2} + 3 - \frac{7}{2} = \frac{1 + 6 - 7}{2} = 0 \;\; \checkmark$$

(The sum of residues must be zero when $b_0 = 0$ ‚Äî this is a useful consistency check.)

*Step 7: Assemble the Diagonal Form matrices.*

$$A_D = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_D = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} & 3 & -\frac{7}{2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_D = 0$$

**(7 marks ‚Äî distributed: 1 for pole identification, 4 for residue calculation, 1 for consistency check, 1 for matrix assembly.)**

**Part (b) ‚Äî Jordan Canonical Form with Repeated Pole:**

*Step 1: Express $G(z)$ in proper partial fraction form for a repeated pole.*

$$G(z) = \frac{k_{11}}{(z+1)^2} + \frac{k_{12}}{z+1} + \frac{k_2}{z+2}$$

*Step 2: Compute the derivative-based residue for the repeated pole.*

For a double pole at $p = -1$:

$$k_{11} = \left. (z+1)^2 G(z) \right|_{z=-1} = \left. \frac{4z+5}{z+2} \right|_{z=-1} = \frac{-4+5}{-1+2} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

$$k_{12} = \left. \frac{d}{dz} \left[ (z+1)^2 G(z) \right] \right|_{z=-1} = \left. \frac{d}{dz} \left[ \frac{4z+5}{z+2} \right] \right|_{z=-1}$$

Applying the quotient rule: $\dfrac{d}{dz} \left[ \dfrac{u}{v} \right] = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$ where $u = 4z+5$ and $v = z+2$:

$$= \frac{(4)(z+2) - (4z+5)(1)}{(z+2)^2} = \frac{4z + 8 - 4z - 5}{(z+2)^2} = \frac{3}{(z+2)^2}$$

At $z = -1$:

$$k_{12} = \frac{3}{(-1+2)^2} = \frac{3}{1} = 3$$

*Step 3: Compute the simple pole residue $k_2$.*

$$k_2 = \left. (z+2) G(z) \right|_{z=-2} = \left. \frac{4z+5}{(z+1)^2} \right|_{z=-2} = \frac{-8+5}{(-1)^2} = \frac{-3}{1} = -3$$

*Step 4: Verify (sum of coefficients of $\frac{1}{z}$ terms must equal zero).*

$k_{12} + k_2 = 3 - 3 = 0$ ‚úì

*Step 5: Build the Jordan block for the repeated pole at $p = -1$ with multiplicity 2.*

$$J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 6: Build the simple Jordan block for the pole at $p = -2$.*

$$J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 7: Assemble the full Jordan Form matrices.*

$$A_J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_J = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_J = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_J = 0$$

**(7 marks ‚Äî distributed: 1 for problem setup, 2 for derivative-based residue calculation, 1 for simple pole residue, 1 for Jordan block construction, 2 for final matrix assembly.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls (3-Mark Loss Zone):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to make the denominator monic** before extracting $a_i$ coefficients. A non-monic denominator like $2z^2 + 3z + 1$ must be divided through by 2 first.
> 2. **Sign errors in the $C$-vector of CCF.** Students often write $C = [b_1, b_2, \dots]$ instead of the correct $C = [b_1 - a_1 b_0, b_2 - a_2 b_0, \dots]$. This single error costs **2‚Äì3 marks** per question.
> 3. **Confusing OCF and CCF transposes.** OCF requires transposing $A$, then transposing both $B$ and $C$ ‚Äî but $B$ and $C$ are *swapped* (not both transposed separately in place).
> 4. **Using Diagonal form for repeated poles.** This produces an error because partial fractions yield $\frac{1}{(z-p)^2}$ terms that the Diagonal form cannot accommodate. **Always** use Jordan form for repeated poles.
> 5. **Skipping the characteristic polynomial verification.** Examiners reward this 1-mark sanity check explicitly. Always write $\det(zI - A)$ and confirm it matches the denominator.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 ‚Äî Transfer Function to State Space Conversion**

- [x] **Definition mastered:** State = minimal internal variable set; Transfer function = external I/O description.
- [x] **Coefficient extraction:** $a_i$ from denominator (after monic normalization), $b_i$ from numerator. $b_0$ is the direct feedthrough $D$.
- [x] **CCF (Phase Variable):** $A$ has identity sub-diagonal and $-a_i$ in the last row; $B = [1, 0, \dots, 0]^T$; $C = [b_1 - a_1 b_0, \dots, b_n - a_n b_0]$; $D = b_0$.
- [x] **OCF (Dual):** Transpose of CCF's $A$; $B = C_{CCF}^T$; $C = B_{CCF}^T$; $D$ unchanged.
- [x] **Diagonal (Modal):** Factor denominator, do partial fraction, place poles on $A$'s diagonal, residues on $C$'s row, $B = $ all-ones. **Valid only for distinct poles.**
- [x] **Jordan Form:** For repeated roots, build Jordan blocks of size equal to multiplicity, with **1's on the super-diagonal** within each block.
- [x] **Duality rule:** $A_{OCF} = A_{CCF}^T$, $B_{OCF} = C_{CCF}^T$, $C_{OCF} = B_{CCF}^T$.
- [x] **Invariance principle:** Eigenvalues of $A$ (poles) and steady-state gain are **invariant** across all canonical forms.
- [x] **Verification:** $\det(zI - A) = $ denominator of $G(z)$ ‚Äî this is the gold standard sanity check.
- [x] **Direct feedthrough:** $D = b_0$ in **all** canonical forms.
- [x] **Residue check for partial fractions:** Sum of residues $= 0$ when $b_0 = 0$.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** State feedback (CCF), Luenberger observer (OCF), modal analysis (Diagonal/Jordan).
- [x] **Pitfall to avoid:** Never use Diagonal form for repeated poles ‚Äî always use Jordan form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Characteristics Equation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) The **Characteristic Equation** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) discrete-time control system is the polynomial equation obtained by equating the **denominator polynomial of the system's pulse transfer function** to zero. It is the discrete-time analogue of the characteristic equation used in classical continuous-time control theory, but is expressed in the **z-domain** rather than the s-domain. For a discrete-time system whose input-output relationship is governed by a linear constant-coefficient difference equation of order $n$: $$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + a_2 y(k-2) + \dots + a_n y(k-n) = b_0 u(k) + b_1 u(k-1) + \dots + b_m u(k-m)$$ the corresponding pulse transfer function is: $$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \dots + b_m z^{-m}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_n z^{-n}}$$ The **characteristic equation** is the polynomial formed by setting the denominator equal to zero: $$\boxed{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_n z^{-n} = 0}$$ Or, equivalently, after clearing the negative powers by multiplying by $z^n$: $$\boxed{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The characteristic equation is the single most important algebraic object in discrete-time stability analysis. The location of its roots in the **z-plane** completely determines whether the digital control system is **stable, marginally stable, or unstable**. The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ plays the role of the imaginary axis $j\omega$ in continuous-time systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The DNA of the System" Imagine a digital control system as a **musical instrument** (say, a guitar string). When you pluck the string and then release it, the string vibrates at certain natural frequencies that are *completely determined* by the string's physical properties (its length, tension, mass, and density) ‚Äî **not by how hard you plucked it**. In exactly the same way, when a digital control system is excited by any input, the system produces two responses: 1. **Forced response** ‚Äî depends on the input $u(k)$ (analogous to "how hard you plucked"). 2. **Natural (free) response / modes** ‚Äî depends *only* on the system's internal structure (analogous to the string's natural vibration). The **characteristic equation is the mathematical "DNA"** that encodes this internal structure. Its roots are the **characteristic values** or **eigenvalues** (in state-space language) that govern how the natural modes of the system evolve with each sampling instant. If any of these "genes" are defective (i.e., a root lies outside the unit circle), the system becomes internally unstable and blows up over time ‚Äî even if the input is perfectly well-behaved. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Metrics Referenced:** > - **Unit circle radius** = $1$ (in the z-plane) > - **Critical stability boundary** = $\vert z \vert = 1$ > - **Natural response decay condition** = $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all roots $z_i$ ### 1.3 Where Does the Characteristic Equation Come From? The characteristic equation can be derived from **three equivalent perspectives**, all of which are examinable in KTU: | Perspective | Source of Characteristic Equation | | :--- | :--- | | **Difference Equation** | Setting the homogeneous part of the difference equation to zero | | **Pulse Transfer Function** | Numerator of $1 + G(z)H(z)$ (closed-loop) or denominator of $G(z)$ (open-loop) | | **State-Space Form** | $\det(zI - A) = 0$, where $A$ is the system matrix | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Operational Lenses of the Characteristic Equation **Lens 1 ‚Äî From the Difference Equation (Time Domain):** Given the $n$-th order linear constant-coefficient difference equation: $$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + a_2 y(k-2) + \dots + a_n y(k-n) = (\text{input terms})$$ Taking the unilateral z-transform of the **homogeneous** part $y_h(k)$ (i.e., the unforced response): $$Y_h(z) + a_1 z^{-1} Y_h(z) + a_2 z^{-2} Y_h(z) + \dots + a_n z^{-n} Y_h(z) = 0$$ Factoring out $Y_h(z)$: $$Y_h(z)\left[1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_n z^{-n}\right] = 0$$ For a **non-trivial** solution $Y_h(z) \neq 0$, the bracketed polynomial must vanish: $$1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_n z^{-n} = 0$$ This **is** the characteristic equation. Its roots $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_n$ give the natural modes of the form $z_i^{\,k}$. **Lens 2 ‚Äî From the Pulse Transfer Function (z-Domain):** For a closed-loop digital control system with forward path $G(z)$ and feedback path $H(z)$, the closed-loop pulse transfer function is: $$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$ The characteristic equation is obtained by setting the **denominator** of $T(z)$ to zero: $$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$ This is sometimes called the **closed-loop characteristic equation**, and it is *the equation whose roots must lie inside the unit circle for closed-loop stability*. **Lens 3 ‚Äî From the State-Space Representation:** For the discrete-time state-space model: $$\mathbf{x}(k+1) = A\,\mathbf{x}(k) + B\,u(k)$$ $$y(k) = C\,\mathbf{x}(k) + D\,u(k)$$ The characteristic equation is: $$\det(zI - A) = 0$$ where $I$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix, $A$ is the $n \times n$ system matrix, and $z$ is a complex scalar. The roots of this determinant are the **eigenvalues** of $A$, and they are *identical* to the roots obtained from Lenses 1 and 2 (for the same physical system). ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step - **Why does the homogeneous part give the characteristic equation?** Because the natural response of a linear system is governed by the system's *own* dynamics, not by the input. The z-transform of the homogeneous difference equation factors into "system polynomial √ó response = 0", so the polynomial in brackets must be zero. - **Why is $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ the closed-loop characteristic equation?** Because the closed-loop poles of a negative-feedback system are precisely the zeros of $1 + G(z)H(z)$, and these poles determine the closed-loop stability. - **Why does $\det(zI - A) = 0$ work?** Because the matrix $zI - A$ is singular precisely when $z$ is an eigenvalue of $A$, and the eigenvalues of $A$ dictate the natural modes $z_i^{\,k}$ of the state trajectory. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | |:--|:---------------------|:--------------| | 1 | $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_n z^{-n} = 0$ | Characteristic equation in $z^{-1}$ (direct from difference equation) | | 2 | $z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0$ | Characteristic equation in $z$ (positive powers) | | 3 | $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ | Closed-loop characteristic equation (negative feedback) | | 4 | $1 - G(z)H(z) = 0$ | Closed-loop characteristic equation (positive feedback) | | 5 | $\det(zI - A) = 0$ | Characteristic equation in state-space form | | 6 | $z_i = r_i e^{\,j\theta_i}$ | Polar form of a characteristic root | | 7 | $\vert z_i \vert < 1 \;\; \forall i$ | Necessary and sufficient condition for **asymptotic stability** | | 8 | $\vert z_i \vert = 1$ (simple, non-repeated) | **Marginal stability** (sustained oscillation) | | 9 | $\exists\, i : \vert z_i \vert > 1$ | **Unstable** system | | 10 | $y_{\text{natural}}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \, z_i^{\,k}$ | Natural response is a linear combination of characteristic-root powers | | 11 | $r_{\text{equi}} = e^{\,\sigma T_s}$ | Mapping between continuous-time pole $s = \sigma + j\omega$ and discrete pole radius | | 12 | $\theta_{\text{equi}} = \omega T_s$ | Mapping between continuous-time pole and discrete pole angle | | 13 | $T_s = \text{sampling period (seconds)}$ | Fundamental parameter; appears in $z = e^{\,sT_s}$ | | 14 | $\omega_d = \theta / T_s$ | Discrete-time damped oscillation frequency (rad/s) | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Table Notation:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert z_i \vert$ denotes the **modulus** (absolute value) of the complex number $z_i$. Per the markdown formatting rules, the vertical bar character is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table layout. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The characteristic equation is the foundation of **digital control system design** in: - **Aerospace**: Stability of digital autopilots, where the sampling rate $T_s$ affects how the discrete poles $z_i$ migrate relative to the unit circle. - **Robotics**: Discrete-time control of robotic arms using digital signal processors (DSPs). - **Power electronics**: Digital control of DC-DC converters, where the closed-loop characteristic equation determines whether output voltage settles to the reference. - **Process control**: PLC-based control of chemical plants, refineries, and HVAC systems. - **Automotive**: Electronic stability control (ESC) and anti-lock braking systems (ABS), which run on microcontrollers executing sampled-data control laws. In every one of these applications, **the engineer tunes the controller coefficients (which appear in the characteristic equation) until all roots of $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** ‚Äî preferably with adequate stability margins (gain margin and phase margin equivalents in the z-plane, e.g., distance from the unit circle). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Characteristic Equation from a First-Order Difference Equation **Problem Setup:** Consider the first-order LTI discrete system: $$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) = b_0 u(k)$$ **Goal:** Derive the characteristic equation, find the natural response, and identify the stability condition. **Step 1 ‚Äî Write the homogeneous equation** (set input $u(k) = 0$ for the natural response): $$y_h(k) + a_1 y_h(k-1) = 0$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Assume an exponential solution** of the form $y_h(k) = z^k$ (this is the discrete-time analogue of the trial solution $e^{\lambda t}$ used in continuous time): $$z^k + a_1 z^{k-1} = 0$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Factor out the common term** $z^{k-1}$ (assuming $z \neq 0$): $$z^{k-1}\left[z + a_1\right] = 0$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî For a non-trivial solution** $z^{k-1} \neq 0$, the bracketed term must vanish: $$\boxed{z + a_1 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad z = -a_1}$$ This is the **characteristic equation** of the first-order system, and its single root is $z_1 = -a_1$. **Step 5 ‚Äî Write the general natural response**: $$y_h(k) = c_1 z_1^{\,k} = c_1 (-a_1)^k$$ **Step 6 ‚Äî Determine the stability condition** by requiring $\lim_{k \to \infty} y_h(k) = 0$ (asymptotic stability): $$\lim_{k \to \infty} c_1 (-a_1)^k = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert -a_1 \vert < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \boxed{\vert a_1 \vert < 1}$$ This is the **well-known stability condition for a first-order discrete system** with $y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) = b_0 u(k)$. ### 3.2 Derivation B: Characteristic Equation from a Second-Order Difference Equation **Problem Setup:** Consider: $$y(k) - 0.5\, y(k-1) + 0.06\, y(k-2) = u(k)$$ Note: This is written with the standard sign convention $y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + a_2 y(k-2) = \dots$, so here $a_1 = -0.5$ and $a_2 = 0.06$. **Goal:** Find the characteristic roots and analyse stability. **Step 1 ‚Äî Form the characteristic equation** by replacing the difference operator with the polynomial in $z$: $$z^2 + a_1 z + a_2 = 0$$ $$z^2 - 0.5 z + 0.06 = 0$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Solve the quadratic** using the standard formula $z = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$: $$z = \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{(-0.5)^2 - 4(1)(0.06)}}{2}$$ $$= \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 - 0.24}}{2}$$ $$= \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.01}}{2}$$ $$= \frac{0.5 \pm 0.1}{2}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the two roots**: $$z_1 = \frac{0.5 + 0.1}{2} = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3$$ $$z_2 = \frac{0.5 - 0.1}{2} = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Write the general natural response**: $$y_h(k) = c_1 (0.3)^k + c_2 (0.2)^k$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Check stability**: $$\vert z_1 \vert = 0.3 < 1, \qquad \vert z_2 \vert = 0.2 < 1$$ Both roots are strictly inside the unit circle, so the system is **asymptotically stable**. Moreover, since both roots are real and positive, the response decays monotonically (no oscillation). ### 3.3 Derivation C: Characteristic Equation from Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function **Problem Setup:** A unity-feedback digital control system has the forward path: $$G(z) = \frac{0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$ **Goal:** Find the closed-loop characteristic equation, its roots, and determine stability. **Step 1 ‚Äî Write the closed-loop transfer function** for a unity negative-feedback system ($H(z) = 1$): $$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)} = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute** $G(z)$: $$T(z) = \frac{\dfrac{0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}}{1 + \dfrac{0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Multiply numerator and denominator by** $(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})$ to clear the inner fraction: $$T(z) = \frac{0.5 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) + 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Identify the closed-loop characteristic equation** (set denominator to zero): $$1 - 0.3 z^{-1} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad z^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.3} = 3.333 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad z = 0.3$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Check stability**: $\vert z \vert = 0.3 < 1$, so the closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable**. **Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the time constant** (number of samples for the response to decay to $\approx 37\%$): $$k_{1/e} = \frac{-1}{\ln(0.3)} \approx \frac{-1}{-1.204} \approx 0.83 \text{ samples}$$ So the system settles in about $4 \times 0.83 \approx 3.3$ samples to within 2% of its final value. ### 3.4 Derivation D: Characteristic Equation from State-Space Form **Problem Setup:** A second-order discrete system in state-space form is: $$\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 \\ -0.1 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$ **Goal:** Derive the characteristic equation from the state matrix $A$. **Step 1 ‚Äî Form the matrix** $zI - A$: $$zI - A = \begin{bmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 \\ -0.1 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.6 & -0.2 \\ 0.1 & z - 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the determinant**: $$\det(zI - A) = (z - 0.6)(z - 0.4) - (-0.2)(0.1)$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Expand the product**: $$= z^2 - 0.4z - 0.6z + 0.24 + 0.02 = z^2 - z + 0.26$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Equate to zero to get the characteristic equation**: $$z^2 - z + 0.26 = 0$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for the eigenvalues**: $$z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 1.04}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{-0.04}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm j\,0.2}{2}$$ $$z_{1,2} = 0.5 \pm j\,0.1$$ **Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the modulus of each complex root**: $$\vert z_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.1)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.26} \approx 0.5099$$ Since $\vert z_{1,2} \vert \approx 0.51 < 1$, the system is **asymptotically stable**. The angle of the root is: $$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.1}{0.5}\right) = \arctan(0.2) \approx 0.1974 \text{ rad} \approx 11.31^\circ$$ The damped natural frequency of the discrete oscillation is: $$\omega_d = \frac{\theta}{T_s}$$ If $T_s = 0.1$ s, then $\omega_d = 1.974$ rad/s. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Mapping Continuous-Time Poles to Discrete-Time Poles For a stable continuous-time pole $s = -\sigma + j\omega$ (with $\sigma > 0$), the corresponding discrete-time pole under a zero-order hold with sampling period $T_s$ is: $$z = e^{\,sT_s} = e^{\,(-\sigma + j\omega)T_s} = e^{-\sigma T_s} e^{\,j\omega T_s}$$ | Continuous-time property | Discrete-time property | Mapping | |:-------------------------|:-----------------------|:--------| | Pole $s = -\sigma + j\omega$ | Pole $z = e^{-\sigma T_s} e^{j\omega T_s}$ | $z = e^{\,sT_s}$ | | Radius $r = e^{-\sigma T_s}$ | Modulus $\vert z \vert = r$ | $\vert z \vert < 1 \Leftrightarrow \sigma > 0$ | | Angle $\theta = \omega T_s$ | Argument $\angle z = \theta$ | $\theta \in (-\pi, \pi]$ | | Stability boundary: $j\omega$-axis | Stability boundary: unit circle | $\sigma = 0 \Leftrightarrow \vert z \vert = 1$ | | Natural frequency $\omega_n$ | Discrete frequency $\omega_d = \theta/T_s$ | Preserved through angle | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî The Three Sources of the Characteristic Equation ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Linear Discrete Time System] --> B1[Time Domain
Difference Equation] A0 --> B2[z Domain
Pulse Transfer Function] A0 --> B3[State Space
Matrix Form] B1 --> C1["y(k) + a1 y(k-1) + ... + an y(k-n) = ..."] C1 --> D1["Set homogeneous part to zero
Take z-transform"] D1 --> E1["Characteristic Equation:
1 + a1 z^-1 + ... + an z^-n = 0"] B2 --> C2["G(z) = N(z) / D(z)"] C2 --> D2["Closed-loop:
T(z) = G(z) / (1 + G(z)H(z))"] D2 --> E2["Characteristic Equation:
1 + G(z)H(z) = 0"] B3 --> C3["x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)"] C3 --> D3["Form matrix zI - A
Take determinant"] D3 --> E3["Characteristic Equation:
det(zI - A) = 0"] E1 --> F1[All three yield
identical roots z1 ... zn] E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 F1 --> G1[Stability Analysis
z-plane root locations] G1 --> H1[All roots inside unit circle
= Asymptotically Stable] G1 --> H2[Simple roots on unit circle
= Marginally Stable] G1 --> H3[Any root outside unit circle
= Unstable] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚Äî Stability Determination from the Characteristic Equation ```mermaid flowchart LR SYS[System Description] --> CE[Form Characteristic Equation] CE --> SOLVE[Find Roots z1 to zn] SOLVE --> DECIDE{Stability Test} DECIDE -->|All |z| less than 1| S1[Asymptotically Stable] DECIDE -->|All |z| less than or equal 1
and no repeated root on unit circle| S2[Marginally Stable] DECIDE -->|Any |z| greater than 1| S3[Unstable] DECIDE -->|Repeated root on unit circle| S3 S1 --> RESP1[Natural response decays
y_h of k tends to 0] S2 --> RESP2[Sustained oscillation
y_h of k bounded, nonzero] S3 --> RESP3[Natural response grows
y_h of k diverges] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic ‚Äî Natural Response Decay Patterns ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Type of Characteristic Root] --> R1[Real positive z
|z| less than 1] ROOT --> R2[Real negative z
|z| less than 1] ROOT --> R3[Complex conjugate pair
|z| less than 1] ROOT --> R4[Root on unit circle
|z| = 1] R1 --> D1[Exponential decay
No oscillation
Monotonic] R2 --> D2[Alternating sign decay
Oscillatory with period 2 samples] R3 --> D3[Damped sinusoidal decay
Period = 2 pi divided by arg of z] R4 --> D4[Undamped oscillation
Sustained periodic output] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic ‚Äî s-Plane to z-Plane Mapping Under Sampling ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Continuous s plane] --> MAP[Mapping z = e to power sT] MAP --> ZP[Discrete z plane] SP --> SP1[Left half s plane
sigma greater than 0] SP --> SP2[Imaginary axis j omega] SP --> SP3[Right half s plane
sigma less than 0] SP1 --> ZP1[Inside unit circle
|z| less than 1
STABLE] SP2 --> ZP2[On unit circle
|z| = 1
MARGINAL] SP3 --> ZP3[Outside unit circle
|z| greater than 1
UNSTABLE] ZP1 --> ZP ZP2 --> ZP ZP3 --> ZP ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Define the characteristic equation of a discrete-time control system. How are its roots related to the natural response of the system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **characteristic equation** of a linear time-invariant discrete-time system is the polynomial obtained by equating the denominator of the system's pulse transfer function to zero. For a system described by the difference equation $y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + \dots + a_n y(k-n) = b_0 u(k) + \dots$, the characteristic equation in $z$ is: $$z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0$$ The $n$ roots $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_n$ of this equation are called the **characteristic roots** or **eigenvalues** of the system. The natural (unforced) response of the system is a linear combination of the modes: $$y_{\text{natural}}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \, z_i^{\,k}$$ where the coefficients $c_i$ are determined by initial conditions. The modulus of each root $\vert z_i \vert$ determines whether the corresponding mode decays ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$), persists ($\vert z_i \vert = 1$), or grows ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$) as $k \to \infty$. --- > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` > **State the necessary and sufficient condition for the asymptotic stability of an LTI discrete-time system in terms of its characteristic roots. Mention the corresponding condition for marginal stability.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Asymptotic stability:** An LTI discrete-time system is asymptotically stable **if and only if all the roots of its characteristic equation lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for every root $z_i$. **Marginal stability:** The system is marginally stable (sustained bounded oscillation) if all roots satisfy $\vert z_i \vert \leq 1$, with **any roots on the unit circle being simple (non-repeated)**. If a root on the unit circle is repeated (multiplicity $\geq 2$), the system is **unstable**. ### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Module 4 Internal Choice --- > **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` > > **(a)** Consider the digital control system described by the difference equation: > $$y(k) - 0.7\, y(k-1) + 0.1\, y(k-2) = u(k) + 0.5\, u(k-1)$$ > > **(i)** Derive the characteristic equation of the system. **(3 marks)** > **(ii)** Find the characteristic roots. **(4 marks)** > **(iii)** Determine whether the system is stable, marginally stable, or unstable. Justify your answer. **(3 marks)** > > **(b)** A unity-feedback digital control system has the forward-path transfer function: > $$G(z) = \frac{0.2\, z^{-1}}{1 - 1.3\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2}}$$ > > Determine the closed-loop characteristic equation, find its roots, and assess closed-loop stability. **(4 marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** #### Part (a)(i) ‚Äî Derive the Characteristic Equation (3 marks) For the given difference equation: $$y(k) - 0.7\, y(k-1) + 0.1\, y(k-2) = u(k) + 0.5\, u(k-1)$$ The characteristic equation is obtained from the homogeneous part (left-hand side with $u$-terms removed), replacing $y(k-i)$ by $z^{-i}$: $$1 - 0.7\, z^{-1} + 0.1\, z^{-2} = 0$$ Multiplying through by $z^2$ to obtain the polynomial in positive powers of $z$: $$\boxed{z^2 - 0.7\, z + 0.1 = 0}$$ #### Part (a)(ii) ‚Äî Find the Characteristic Roots (4 marks) Applying the quadratic formula with $a=1$, $b=-0.7$, $c=0.1$: $$z = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} = \frac{0.7 \pm \sqrt{(-0.7)^2 - 4(1)(0.1)}}{2(1)}$$ $$= \frac{0.7 \pm \sqrt{0.49 - 0.40}}{2} = \frac{0.7 \pm \sqrt{0.09}}{2} = \frac{0.7 \pm 0.3}{2}$$ Therefore: $$z_1 = \frac{0.7 + 0.3}{2} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5$$ $$z_2 = \frac{0.7 - 0.3}{2} = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2$$ Both roots are **real and positive**. #### Part (a)(iii) ‚Äî Stability Determination (3 marks) Compute the moduli: $$\vert z_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1 \quad \text{(inside unit circle)}$$ $$\vert z_2 \vert = 0.2 < 1 \quad \text{(inside unit circle)}$$ Since **all characteristic roots lie strictly inside the unit circle**, the system is **asymptotically stable**. The natural response is: $$y_h(k) = c_1 (0.5)^k + c_2 (0.2)^k \;\to\; 0 \quad \text{as} \quad k \to \infty$$ #### Part (b) ‚Äî Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation (4 marks) For unity negative feedback, the closed-loop transfer function is: $$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)} = \frac{\dfrac{0.2\, z^{-1}}{1 - 1.3\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2}}}{1 + \dfrac{0.2\, z^{-1}}{1 - 1.3\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2}}}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - 1.3\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2})$: $$T(z) = \frac{0.2\, z^{-1}}{(1 - 1.3\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2}) + 0.2\, z^{-1}} = \frac{0.2\, z^{-1}}{1 - 1.1\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2}}$$ The closed-loop characteristic equation is: $$1 - 1.1\, z^{-1} + 0.4\, z^{-2} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z^2 - 1.1\, z + 0.4 = 0$$ Solving: $$z = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{(1.1)^2 - 4(0.4)}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{1.21 - 1.60}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{-0.39}}{2}$$ $$z_{1,2} = \frac{1.1 \pm j\,0.6245}{2} = 0.55 \pm j\,0.3122$$ Modulus: $$\vert z_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{(0.55)^2 + (0.3122)^2} = \sqrt{0.3025 + 0.0975} = \sqrt{0.40} \approx 0.6325$$ Since $\vert z_{1,2} \vert \approx 0.63 < 1$, both roots are inside the unit circle, and the **closed-loop system is asymptotically stable**. --- > **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4]` > > **(a)** A discrete-time system is described in state-space form as: > $$\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k), \qquad y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k)$$ > > Derive the characteristic equation from the state matrix and determine stability. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For the closed-loop digital system with $G(z) = \dfrac{z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}}$ and $H(z) = 0.5$ (positive feedback configuration), determine the closed-loop characteristic equation, its roots, and the natural response. **(7 marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** #### Part (a) ‚Äî State-Space Characteristic Equation (7 marks) Given: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$ Form the matrix $zI - A$: $$zI - A = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.8 & -0.5 \\ 0 & z - 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$ Compute the determinant: $$\det(zI - A) = (z - 0.8)(z - 0.3) - (-0.5)(0) = (z - 0.8)(z - 0.3)$$ **Characteristic equation:** $$\boxed{(z - 0.8)(z - 0.3) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z^2 - 1.1\, z + 0.24 = 0}$$ The characteristic roots are $z_1 = 0.8$ and $z_2 = 0.3$. Since $A$ is upper triangular, the eigenvalues are simply its diagonal entries, confirming the roots. **Stability check:** $$\vert z_1 \vert = 0.8 < 1, \qquad \vert z_2 \vert = 0.3 < 1$$ Both roots lie inside the unit circle, so the system is **asymptotically stable**. The natural response is: $$\mathbf{x}_{\text{natural}}(k) = c_1 \begin{bmatrix} \cdot \\ \cdot \end{bmatrix} (0.8)^k + c_2 \begin{bmatrix} \cdot \\ \cdot \end{bmatrix} (0.3)^k \to \mathbf{0}$$ #### Part (b) ‚Äî Positive-Feedback Closed-Loop (7 marks) For a **positive feedback** system, the closed-loop transfer function is: $$T(z) = \frac{G(z)}{1 - G(z)H(z)}$$ Substitute $G(z) = \dfrac{z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}}$ and $H(z) = 0.5$: $$G(z)H(z) = \frac{0.5\, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}}$$ The closed-loop denominator is: $$1 - G(z)H(z) = 1 - \frac{0.5\, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}} = \frac{(1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}) - 0.5\, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}} = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}}$$ Therefore the closed-loop transfer function is: $$T(z) = \frac{\dfrac{z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}}}{\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5\, z^{-1}}} = \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$ **Closed-loop characteristic equation:** $$1 - z^{-1} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \boxed{z = 1}$$ **Stability check:** The single root is $z_1 = 1$, which lies **exactly on the unit circle**. Since it is a simple (non-repeated) root, the system is **marginally stable**. **Natural response:** The system will exhibit a **sustained, non-decaying response**. Specifically, for an impulse input, the output is $y(0) = 0$, $y(1) = 1$, $y(2) = 1$, $y(3) = 1$, $\dots$ ‚Äî the response steps up to a constant value of $1$ and remains there indefinitely. ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Error:** When converting from the difference equation to the characteristic polynomial, students often write the polynomial as $z^2 + a_1 z + a_2$ using the **same** signs $a_1, a_2$ as in the difference equation, when in fact if the difference equation is $y(k) - 0.7 y(k-1) + 0.1 y(k-2) = \dots$, the polynomial is $z^2 - 0.7 z + 0.1$ (signs are **preserved**, not flipped). Always re-derive by taking the z-transform. > > 2. **Forgetting to Clear Negative Powers:** Students frequently leave the characteristic equation in $z^{-1}$ form and then try to substitute values of $z$. Always multiply through by $z^n$ first. > > 3. **Conflating Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Characteristic Equations:** The open-loop denominator of $G(z)$ and the closed-loop denominator $1 + G(z)H(z)$ are **different polynomials**. The closed-loop poles (which determine stability) come from the latter, not the former. > > 4. **Using $\vert z \vert > 1$ as Stability:** A common reverse-mistake: thinking "all poles inside the unit circle means unstable" because of confusing s-plane and z-plane conventions. **In the z-plane, INSIDE the unit circle = STABLE.** > > 5. **Ignoring Repeated Roots on the Unit Circle:** A simple root on the unit circle gives marginal stability, but a **repeated** root on the unit circle (e.g., $(z-1)^2 = 0$) gives **unstable** behaviour (a ramp-like unbounded response). > > 6. **Not Mapping the Modulus Condition Correctly:** Some students check only the **real part** of the root, confusing z-plane analysis with s-plane analysis. In the z-plane, you must check the **modulus** $\vert z_i \vert$, not the real part. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Characteristic Equation** > > - **Definition:** Polynomial obtained by equating the denominator of the pulse transfer function (or the homogeneous part of the difference equation) to zero. > - **Three equivalent forms:** > 1. From difference equation: $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_n z^{-n} = 0$ > 2. From closed-loop: $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ (negative feedback) or $1 - G(z)H(z) = 0$ (positive feedback) > 3. From state-space: $\det(zI - A) = 0$ > - **Stability rule:** $\vert z_i \vert < 1 \;\; \forall i \Rightarrow$ **asymptotically stable**; any $\vert z_i \vert > 1 \Rightarrow$ **unstable**; simple roots with $\vert z_i \vert = 1 \Rightarrow$ **marginally stable**; repeated roots on $\vert z_i \vert = 1 \Rightarrow$ **unstable**. > - **Natural response:** $y_h(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i\, z_i^{\,k}$, where the $c_i$ are determined by initial conditions. > - **Complex conjugate roots:** Come in pairs and produce **damped oscillatory** modes with discrete frequency $\omega_d = \theta/T_s$. > - **Real positive roots:** Produce monotonic exponential modes. > - **Real negative roots:** Produce alternating-sign modes (period-2 oscillation envelope). > - **Mapping from s to z:** $z = e^{sT_s}$; left-half s-plane $\to$ interior of unit circle. > - **Cofactors in transfer functions:** The characteristic equation determines the **denominator** of the closed-loop transfer function ‚Äî never the numerator (numerator affects zeros, not the characteristic roots). > - **Order = number of energy-storage elements:** A system with $n$ delay elements (or $n$ integrators in continuous time) has an $n$-th order characteristic equation, and therefore $n$ characteristic roots. > - **Bilinear transform (Tustin's):** $z = \dfrac{1 + sT/2}{1 - sT/2}$ can be used to convert continuous-time characteristic polynomials to discrete-time, but always re-verify the result using direct z-plane analysis. > - **Most-tested topic in Module 4:** "Find the characteristic equation, solve for the roots, and determine stability" ‚Äî appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper with a sub-part worth 7-10 marks.

topics.title: Solution to Discrete State Equations. Controllability and Observability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT634 - DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solution to Discrete State Equations. Controllability and Observability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Solution to Discrete State Equations ‚Äî Formal Definition

A **Discrete-Time State Equation** is a difference equation written in vector-matrix form that describes the evolution of the state vector of a digital/sampled-data system at discrete instants $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$. The standard form is:

$$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)$$

where:
- $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector** at instant $k$
- $u(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **input vector** at instant $k$
- $y(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the **output vector** at instant $k$
- $A$ is the **state matrix** ($n \times n$), $B$ is the **input matrix** ($n \times m$), $C$ is the **output matrix** ($p \times n$), and $D$ is the **feedthrough matrix** ($p \times m$).

The **solution (state transition equation)** describes the state at any future instant $k$ in terms of the initial state $x(0)$ and the entire control history $u(0), u(1), \dots, u(k-1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be fluent in three solution techniques ‚Äî (i) iterative/recursive method, (ii) $z$-transform method, and (iii) the state transition matrix $\Phi(k) = A^{k}$ method. Each yields the same answer, but the choice of method depends on the question (board exam weightage: ~6‚Äì8 marks).

### 1.2 Controllability ‚Äî Formal Definition

A discrete-time system is said to be **completely state controllable** if there exists an unconstrained control sequence $u(0), u(1), \dots, u(N-1)$ that transfers any initial state $x(0)$ to any desired final state $x(N)$ in a finite number of sampling instants $N \leq n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kalman's Controllability Criterion:** The discrete LTI system $(A, B)$ is completely state controllable **if and only if** the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [B \;\; AB \;\; A^{2}B \;\; \dots \;\; A^{n-1}B]$ has **full rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$, where $n$ is the order of the system (dimension of $A$).

### 1.3 Observability ‚Äî Formal Definition

A discrete-time system is said to be **completely observable** if every initial state $x(0)$ can be uniquely reconstructed from a finite sequence of output measurements $y(0), y(1), \dots, y(N-1)$ when the input $u(k)$ is known.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kalman's Observability Criterion:** The pair $(A, C)$ is completely observable **if and only if** the observability matrix $\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^{2} \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$ has **full rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî "Driving a Car on a Digital Map"** üöó
>
> Imagine you are driving a car whose GPS samples your position **once every second** (discrete sampling).
>
> - **Solution to state equation** is like asking: *"Given my position now and knowing the steering inputs applied in the past, where will I be in 10 seconds?"* The state-transition matrix $A^{k}$ acts as a *time-shift operator* that propagates information forward.
> - **Controllability** asks: *"Can I reach ANY target intersection on the map using the steering wheel alone?"* If the steering is connected only to the rear wheels (rank-deficient $B$), I might be stuck ‚Äî that's an *uncontrollable* system. The controllability matrix checks whether the steering input can "reach" every state direction.
> - **Observability** asks: *"From the GPS readings alone, can I reconstruct where I started (the initial state)?"* If the GPS is broken (zero output), I can never know ‚Äî that's an *unobservable* system. The observability matrix checks whether the sensor output "sees" every state direction.
>
> In KTU board exams, controllability and observability are **dual concepts** ‚Äî swap $(A, B) \leftrightarrow (A^{T}, C^{T})$ to convert one problem into the other.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Order of system $n$**: dimension of $A$ matrix (number of energy-storing elements in the digital system).
- **Sample instant $k$**: a non-negative integer representing the $k$-th sampling step.
- **No physical constants are involved** ‚Äî these are purely structural/algebraic properties of the system matrices.
